title: Shared and Distinct Structural Features of Autism-Spectrum Traits and Personality Pathology: A Dimensional Approach
authors: Ludwig Ohse; Emanuel Jauk; Kerrin Riedel; Caroline Ibe; Alexander Lischke
summary: This study investigates the overlap between autism-spectrum traits and personality pathology using dimensional rather than categorical models, addressing a critical gap in differential diagnosis. In a sample of 704 non-clinical adults (mean age 23.6, 58.5% female), participants completed the Autism-Spectrum Quotient Short Form (AQ-k), the Personality Inventory for DSM-5 Brief Form (PID-5-BF), and the PAI Borderline Features Scale (PAI-BOR). Using confirmatory and exploratory factor analyses including bifactor models, the authors examined shared and distinct structural features while distinguishing general from domain-specific personality pathology. The communication and reciprocity domain of autism showed the strongest associations across personality pathology domains (r = .66 with negative affectivity, r = .61 with detachment, r = .47 with psychoticism), followed by social interaction and spontaneity (r = .59 with detachment), while imagination and creativity showed modest associations (r = .37 with detachment at most). Bifactor models revealed that autism-spectrum traits were mainly associated with general personality pathology (communication and reciprocity: r = .70 with the general factor). After accounting for general personality pathology, detachment was the only specific personality pathology domain that remained substantially associated with autism traits, particularly the social interaction and spontaneity domain (r = .38-.39). Exploratory models confirmed that social interaction and spontaneity indicators loaded on a specific factor together with detachment indicators representing withdrawal and intimacy avoidance.
clinical_insight: This study has direct implications for the longstanding clinical challenge of differentiating autism spectrum disorder (ASD) from personality disorders (PDs), particularly when patients present with social difficulties, emotional dysregulation, and interpersonal patterns that could reflect either condition. The finding that autism-spectrum traits related to social communication and interaction overlap mainly with general personality pathology (reflecting broad interpersonal impairment) but also specifically with detachment, suggests that clinicians should adopt a dimensional assessment approach rather than treating ASD-PD overlap as either categorical comorbidity or diagnostic redundancy. When a patient presents with social withdrawal and difficulty forming close relationships, clinicians should consider whether these features reflect autism-specific social communication deficits, personality pathology involving the detachment domain, or both operating at different levels of a hierarchical model. The study's bifactor approach provides a conceptual framework: assessing whether interpersonal difficulties reflect general personality pathology severity or specific detachment features above and beyond general impairment may guide treatment planning. For instance, if a patient's social difficulties are primarily driven by general personality pathology, transdiagnostic treatments targeting interpersonal functioning may be most appropriate, whereas if specific detachment features remain after accounting for general pathology, autism-informed approaches addressing social communication may be warranted. The non-clinical, student-heavy sample limits direct generalizability, and future work in clinical populations with interview-based assessments is needed.
relevant_for: Clinicians involved in autism assessment and differential diagnosis; those working with personality disorders, particularly schizoid, avoidant, and borderline presentations; researchers interested in the HiTOP and AMPD dimensional frameworks for understanding comorbidity between ASD and PD.

title: A comprehensive systematic review of participatory autism research over a 22-year period
authors: Diana Tan; Jacquiline den Houting; Marion Rabuka; Hannah Winspear-Schillings; Tori Haar; Liz Pellicano
summary: This systematic review maps the landscape of participatory autism research from 2001 to 2023, identifying 382 studies across six databases. The review examines who participates, at what depth, and in which research activities. Key findings reveal that while participatory language has become normative, genuine power-sharing remains rare. Two-thirds (67%) of studies involved Autistic people, yet one-third of studies claiming to be participatory excluded Autistic individuals entirely. The most common participation level was consulting (31%), with community-led research at only 5% and community-controlled research at 1%. Community involvement concentrated heavily in study design (73%) rather than analysis (33%) or dissemination (29%). Participatory research was geographically concentrated in four Western nations (USA, UK, Australia, Canada: 86%), predominantly used qualitative methods (over half), and focused on applied research areas. Deeper participation was associated with identity-first language use (co-producing: 80%, community-led: 89%, community-controlled: 100% identity-first) and publication in journals with community-centered editorial policies. Government funding was associated with higher participation levels, yet all five community-controlled studies were unfunded, raising questions about whether traditional funding structures are compatible with truly community-controlled research.
clinical_insight: For clinicians and researchers designing autism-related clinical studies, this review serves as both a mirror and a mandate. The finding that one-third of supposedly participatory studies excluded Autistic people entirely should prompt clinicians to critically evaluate the claims of any autism research they read or cite. When clinicians encounter autism research claiming to be participatory, they should check: were Autistic people actually involved as partners rather than subjects? At what level of the participation ladder did the study operate? The concentration of community involvement in study design (73%) but not analysis (33%) or dissemination (29%) means that even in participatory studies, the interpretation of data and communication of findings remain largely under academic control. For clinical practice, this matters because research that does not meaningfully involve Autistic people may produce findings that do not align with the priorities and lived experiences of the population it aims to serve. The strong association between deeper participation and identity-first language use suggests that meaningful community involvement influences not just processes but conceptual frameworks. Clinicians should be aware that the language they use to describe autism reflects broader assumptions about the condition, and that Autistic community preferences consistently favor identity-first language. The geographic concentration in Western countries (86%) also means that clinical research may not adequately represent Autistic experiences in non-Western contexts, limiting the cross-cultural applicability of assessment tools and interventions.
relevant_for: Autism researchers designing studies; clinicians interpreting autism research evidence; those developing assessment tools and interventions for autism who want to ensure alignment with community priorities; journal editors and reviewers evaluating participatory claims.

title: Syntactic analysis provides nuanced insight into linguistic distancing's relationship with momentary affect
authors: Ratna Kandala; Niels Vanhasbroeck; Eva Ceulemans; Miryam de Lhoneux; Katie Hoemann
summary: This study advances the measurement of linguistic distancing, an emotion regulatory strategy where describing events at greater psychological distance is associated with reduced negative affect. Using syntax-focused NLP applied to 24,668 experience-sampling descriptions from 102 Dutch-speaking Belgian adults collected over 70 days, the authors demonstrate that specific syntactic features provide more nuanced prediction of momentary affect than conventional word-counting approaches (LIWC). Pronoun case mattered beyond person and number: nominative first-person pronouns, which assign the speaker an active causal role, were more strongly associated with momentary affect (b = 1.61, p < .001) than objective case pronouns (b = 0.89, p < .001) or possessive pronouns (b = 0.17, p = .059). Verb aspect mattered beyond tense: imperfective verbs describing ongoing activities (b = 1.19, p < .001) were more predictive than perfective verbs (b = -0.31, p = .003, in the opposite direction). A syntax-based composite index combining nominative pronouns and imperfective aspect predicted momentary valence (b = 2.43, p < .001), performing comparably to the LIWC partial index (b = 1.81). An interaction with sentiment showed that linguistic distancing predicted valence at all sentiment levels but became non-significant at very high positive sentiment. All models used multilevel regression with within- and between-person decomposition.
clinical_insight: Linguistic distancing is used in clinical contexts ranging from cognitive therapy (cognitive reappraisal as distanced self-talk) to trauma processing (creating narrative distance from traumatic memories). This study provides clinicians and researchers with a more precise toolkit for measuring this therapeutic mechanism. The finding that nominative pronouns, which position the speaker as an active agent, carry the most weight suggests that when patients shift from an active first-person construal (I did, I am doing) to a more distanced perspective, this reflects genuine psychological distancing with affective consequences. For therapists using cognitive restructuring or narrative therapy, this implies that tracking not just the frequency of first-person pronouns but their syntactic role could provide a more sensitive marker of therapeutic change. The verb aspect finding adds another dimension: imperfective aspect (ongoing, unbounded descriptions) appears to reflect greater psychological immersion in events, whereas shifting to perfective aspect (completed, bounded descriptions) may facilitate emotional distancing. This has direct implications for how clinicians might guide patients to reframe their narratives, for example, encouraging descriptions of traumatic events as completed and bounded rather than ongoing and all-encompassing. The study also demonstrates the feasibility of applying these syntactic analyses to natural language data collected via experience sampling, opening possibilities for real-time monitoring of emotion regulation in clinical settings. However, the analyses were conducted in Dutch, and cross-linguistic validation is needed before clinical application in other languages.
relevant_for: Clinicians using cognitive restructuring or narrative therapy approaches; researchers studying emotion regulation mechanisms; those developing NLP-based assessment tools for clinical psychology; psychotherapy process researchers tracking linguistic markers of therapeutic change.

title: Mindfulness-based EMI for GAD: RCT
authors: Unknown
summary: Randomized controlled trial of a mindfulness-based emotion management intervention for generalized anxiety disorder.
clinical_insight: This RCT supports a structured mindfulness-based emotion management intervention for GAD, offering clinicians an evidence-based alternative or adjunct to CBT for clients who struggle with chronic worry and may respond better to present-moment awareness and nonjudgmental acceptance of anxious sensations.
relevant_for: Anxiety disorder specialists and mindfulness-based clinicians treating generalized anxiety disorder

title: Confabulation and False Memory in Clinical Psychology: A Review of Theoretical Perspectives and Clinical Implications
authors: Kryvosheia
summary: Reviews theoretical perspectives on confabulation and false memory, distinguishing their different aetiologies and clinical meanings. Confabulation typically follows neurological insult and reflects memory gap-filling, while false memories arise from source-monitoring errors and schema-driven reconstruction common in healthy cognition. The review synthesizes clinical implications for assessment and treatment across neuropsychological and psychotherapeutic contexts.
clinical_insight: Clinicians conducting trauma-focused therapy must distinguish between confabulation (suggesting neurological involvement requiring referral) and false memory (a normal cognitive process amplified by suggestive techniques). The review supports careful use of memory recovery techniques, emphasizing that source-monitoring training can help patients differentiate genuine memories from reconstructed ones. A key blind spot is the limited empirical guidance on how to apply these distinctions in real-time clinical sessions with trauma survivors reporting recovered memories.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and trauma therapists assessing memory accuracy in clinical practice

title: The Development of a Culturally-Adapted Dialectical Behavioral Therapy Single-Session Intervention: Encouraging Flexible Control
authors: Ramadurai, Nelson
summary: Develops and describes a culturally-adapted DBT single-session intervention for African American women experiencing intersectional stress and overcontrolled coping patterns. The adaptation validates environmental stressors while exploring flexibility in coping, integrating cultural context into the DBT framework of dialectical thinking between acceptance and change.
clinical_insight: DBT therapists working with marginalized populations should consider how cultural stressors shape overcontrolled coping patterns and adapt skills training accordingly. The single-session format makes this intervention practical for crisis intervention, intake sessions, or settings where full DBT programs are unavailable. The main limitation is the early development stage without controlled outcome data, though the cultural adaptation framework provides a replicable model for other populations experiencing intersectional stress.
relevant_for: DBT therapists and crisis clinicians working with African American women and other marginalized groups

title: Self-Esteem and Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors: A Meta-Analytic Review
authors: Buecker et al.
summary: Meta-analysis of 114 studies examining the association between self-esteem and suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Finds moderate-to-strong negative associations (r=-.435 for ideation, r=-.258 for behavior), with moderation by age, clinical status, and measurement approach. Results support self-esteem as both a risk marker and potential intervention target for suicide prevention.
clinical_insight: This meta-analysis provides robust evidence that self-esteem is a significant and modifiable risk factor for suicidal ideation and behavior, with effect sizes large enough to be clinically meaningful. Therapists should integrate self-esteem assessment into suicide risk formulations and consider interventions that directly target self-worth, such as compassion-focused therapy or schema therapy limited re-parenting, alongside standard safety planning. A critical blind spot is that most included studies are cross-sectional, limiting causal inference about whether low self-esteem causes suicidality or vice versa.
relevant_for: Suicide risk assessors and therapists using CFT or schema therapy with suicidal patients

title: Intolerance of Uncertainty and Psychosis: A Systematic Review
authors: Morriss, Ellett
summary: Systematic review examining the relationship between intolerance of uncertainty (IU) and psychosis-spectrum symptoms. Finds evidence that IU is elevated in individuals with schizophrenia-spectrum diagnoses and contributes to distress, delusional conviction, and anomalous experiences, positioning IU as a transdiagnostic mechanism relevant to early intervention approaches for psychosis.
clinical_insight: For clinicians working with early psychosis or high-risk populations, assessing intolerance of uncertainty provides a transdiagnostic target that bridges anxiety and psychotic experiences. ACT-based interventions that build psychological flexibility in the face of uncertainty may reduce both distress and delusional conviction, complementing antipsychotic medication. The review highlights the tension between psychosis-specific and transdiagnostic models, noting that IU-based interventions developed for anxiety may need significant adaptation for psychotic populations. A limitation is the relatively small evidence base compared to the anxiety-IU literature.
relevant_for: Early psychosis clinicians and ACT therapists working with schizophrenia-spectrum disorders

title: Predictive Formats and Informational Attenuation: A Unified Account of Autism Across Cognition, Phenomenology, and Social Interaction
authors: Safader
summary: Proposes a unified account of autism grounded in predictive processing and information theory, explaining autistic differences in perception, reasoning, and social interaction as systematic consequences of attenuated prior precision. The framework integrates cognitive, phenomenological, and social levels, offering a parsimonious alternative to fragmented domain-specific explanations of autistic cognition.
clinical_insight: This predictive processing framework gives therapists a coherent way to understand autistic clients experiences without pathologizing them: sensory sensitivities, social differences, and reliance on routine all follow from the same underlying mechanism of attenuated prior precision. For assessment, this supports moving away from deficit-based models toward understanding how information processing differences create specific strengths and challenges. The key limitation is that this remains a theoretical framework without direct clinical trial evidence, though it aligns with neurodiversity-affirming approaches gaining traction in clinical practice.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and therapists conducting autism assessments and neurodiversity-affirming therapy with adult autistic clients

title: Reduced Visual Imagery Vividness as a Risk Factor for PTSD: Implications for Aphantasia and Imagery-Based Treatments
authors: Swick et al.
summary: Investigates whether reduced visual imagery vividness serves as a risk factor for PTSD, examining implications for aphantasia (the inability to voluntarily visualize) and imagery-based trauma treatments. The study explores how individual differences in imagery ability may influence PTSD symptom development and treatment response, particularly for veterans and trauma-exposed populations.
clinical_insight: Veterans with aphantasia or reduced visual imagery vividness may not benefit from standard imagery-based PTSD treatments like prolonged exposure or imaginal rehearsal, requiring therapists to adapt toward narrative, somatic, or sensory-based processing instead. Clinicians should screen for aphantasia when trauma-focused therapy stalls, and shift to approaches such as written trauma accounts, EMDR, or body-oriented techniques that do not rely on voluntary visual imagery. This finding challenges the assumption that all patients can engage in imaginal exposure and calls for personalized trauma treatment matching.
relevant_for: Military and veteran PTSD therapists using trauma-focused exposure therapies

title: Am I Anxious or Rejected? Measuring Implicit Associations in Social Anxiety
authors: Pattison et al.
summary: Examines the use of the Implicit Association Test (IAT) as a measure of social anxiety, assessing whether implicit associations can distinguish between anxiety about social evaluation and concerns about rejection. The study evaluates the validity and clinical utility of implicit measures for capturing aspects of social anxiety that self-report questionnaires may miss.
clinical_insight: The IAT offers therapists an indirect measure of social anxiety that captures automatic negative self-associations patients may not report on self-questionnaires due to shame or lack of insight. Clinicians can use the IAT as a supplementary assessment tool to detect hidden social anxiety, monitor implicit attitude change during treatment, or identify patients who verbally minimize their social fears. This is especially useful when self-report and behavioral observation diverge, suggesting unconscious threat associations that may sustain social avoidance.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists specializing in social anxiety assessment and research

title: Is physical activity associated with lower borderline personality disorder severity, accounting for insomnia disorder?
authors: Valentin Krieger, Samuel St-Amour, Paquito Bernard, Niharika Parashar, Matthieu Samson, Lionel Cailhol
summary: Cross-sectional study (N=192) examining whether physical activity is associated with reduced BPD severity after accounting for comorbid insomnia disorder. Findings suggest physical activity may independently contribute to lower BPD symptom severity beyond its effects on sleep, supporting exercise as an adjunctive intervention in BPD treatment.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with BPD patients should systematically assess physical activity levels and sleep quality as modifiable treatment targets. The finding that exercise benefits BPD severity independently of insomnia suggests that prescribing physical activity alongside dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) or mentalization-based treatment (MBT) could address both affective dysregulation and behavioral impulsivity through non-pharmacological pathways. A notable limitation is the cross-sectional design, which cannot establish causation — exercise may simply be a marker of better overall functioning.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating borderline personality disorder in outpatient and partial hospitalization settings

title: A Neuroaffirmative Evidence Stack for Multi-Source Adult Neurodivergence Assessment: Framework Design and Formative Computational Evaluation
authors: Lucas Fortaleza de Aquino Ferreira, Lenifer Siqueira Landim
summary: This methods paper introduces the SPECTRA Evidence Stack, a three-layer computational framework (Response Quality, Multi-source Convergence, Confidence Synthesis) for integrating self-report, informant-report, and performance-based assessments in adult neurodivergence. Rather than collapsing discrepancies between sources into a single score, the framework preserves and structures disagreement as clinically meaningful data — reflecting phenomena like masking, context-dependent trait expression, and limited insight.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with neurodivergent adults routinely encounter the problem of mismatched assessment data — a client who presents as articulate in session but whose informant reports suggest severe functional impairment. This framework formalizes what experienced clinicians do intuitively: weigh conflicting signals, flag potential masking, and adjust confidence accordingly. However, the framework has not yet been validated against clinical diagnoses, so it should complement rather than replace comprehensive assessment.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing adult ADHD and autism, particularly those working with high-masking or late-diagnosed neurodivergent adults

title: Loneliness and Suicidal Ideation in the General Adult Population in Slovak Republic - Results from DISCONNECT study
authors: Veronika Bolekova; Michal Hajduk; Jakub Januska
summary: This population-representative study examined the association of loneliness and social isolation with suicidal ideation in 3006 Slovak adults (52.5% female, mean age 48.5, range 18-92) using face-to-face interviews from the DISCONNECT project baseline assessment (November 2024). Suicidal ideation was assessed via PHQ-9 item 9 (any endorsement scored as present), loneliness via the 3-item UCLA Loneliness Scale, social isolation via the Lubben Social Network Scale (LSNS-6), and depression via PHQ-8. The two-week prevalence of suicidal ideation was 14.6% (95% CI: 13.3-15.9), with 9.5% reporting ideation several days, 4.2% more than half the days, and 0.9% nearly every day. Hierarchical binary logistic regression revealed that depression was the strongest predictor (OR = 21.22, 95% CI: 16.03-28.08, p < .001). After controlling for sociodemographic factors, depression, social isolation, and current mental health treatment, loneliness remained independently associated with nearly threefold higher odds of suicidal ideation (OR = 2.93, 95% CI: 2.21-3.88, p < .001), showing a stronger association than social isolation (OR = 1.65, 95% CI: 1.22-2.23, p = .001). The final model achieved Nagelkerke R-squared = .442. Current treatment for a mental health condition was also independently associated (OR = 2.07, 95% CI: 1.32-3.25, p = .001).
clinical_insight: This study provides robust population-level evidence that loneliness is a distinct and clinically meaningful risk factor for suicidal ideation, independent of depression and objective social isolation. The nearly threefold increase in odds associated with loneliness, even after controlling for depression (which showed a 21-fold increase), suggests that subjective social disconnection operates through pathways beyond depressive affect. For clinicians conducting suicide risk assessments, this finding supports the routine inclusion of loneliness measures alongside depression screening. The clinical distinction between loneliness (subjective perception of inadequate social connections) and social isolation (objective absence of contacts) is critical: a patient may have social contacts yet still feel profoundly lonely, and it is this subjective experience that appears more strongly tied to suicidal thinking. Clinicians should not assume that a patient who is socially connected is protected from suicide risk. The finding that current mental health treatment was associated with higher odds of suicidal ideation (OR = 2.07) likely reflects treatment-seeking by those already at elevated risk rather than an iatrogenic effect, but it also suggests that currently available treatments may not adequately address the loneliness-suicidality pathway. Interventions targeting loneliness directly, such as social skills training, community integration programs, or therapeutic approaches that specifically address perceptions of social disconnection, may complement standard depression treatment in reducing suicide risk. The high two-week prevalence of suicidal ideation (14.6%) in the Slovak adult population underscores the public health significance of these findings in Central and Eastern Europe, a region where such data has been scarce.
relevant_for: Suicide risk assessors and crisis clinicians; those working in primary care or community mental health where brief screening is needed; public health professionals designing suicide prevention strategies in Central and Eastern Europe; researchers investigating the interpersonal theory of suicide.

title: Touch of Loneliness Hurts the Most: The Implications of Loneliness and Social Touch
authors: Zofia Kurasz; Michal Krzyzowski; Bartosz Baran; Marek Krzystanek
summary: This study tested whether loneliness and related psychosocial variables predict endogenous pain inhibition, and whether the hedonic appraisal of affective (C-tactile targeted) social touch tracks these social variables or links to pain modulation. In 119 healthy emerging adults (aged 18-27, median 22), participants completed questionnaires measuring social network structure, contact quality, perceived social support, loneliness (UCLA-3), self-concept clarity, early maladaptive schemas (Young's), depressive symptoms, and perceived stress. Participants rated the pleasantness of standardized slow forearm brushing at C-tactile optimal velocity of approximately 3 cm/s for 60 seconds. Conditioned pain modulation (CPM) was assessed using pressure pain thresholds before and after a tonic cuff-conditioning stimulus. In the multivariable CPM model, higher loneliness was significantly associated with weaker endogenous pain inhibition (b = -0.90, 95% CI [-1.65, -0.15], p = .019) after adjusting for demographics, baseline pain threshold, stress, depression, self-concept clarity, perceived support, contact quality, and Young's schemas. Contrary to predictions, a maladaptive schemas domain reflecting relational disconnection with affective inhibition showed a positive association with CPM (b = 11.28, 95% CI [2.91, 19.65], p = .009). Touch pleasantness was poorly explained by psychosocial variables, with sex as the only reliable predictor (males lower: b = -0.90, p = .022), and did not predict CPM.
clinical_insight: This study bridges the gap between subjective social experience and somatic physiology, demonstrating that loneliness is associated with diminished endogenous pain inhibitory capacity as measured by conditioned pain modulation. For clinicians working with somatic symptom disorders, fibromyalgia, or chronic pain conditions, this finding offers a mechanistic explanation for why lonely patients may experience more severe or persistent somatic symptoms. The loneliness-pain connection operates through threat-related biology that overlaps with nociceptive processing, consistent with the evolutionary theory that social pain and physical pain share neurobiological substrates. The finding that the association remained after controlling for depression, stress, and perceived support suggests that loneliness is not simply a proxy for these related constructs but has a unique relationship with pain processing. Clinicians treating patients with unexplained or treatment-resistant somatic complaints should consider assessing loneliness as a potential maintaining factor, as interventions that address social disconnection (such as social prescribing, group therapy, or behavioral activation targeting social engagement) may indirectly improve pain processing and reduce somatic burden. The unexpected finding that relational disconnection schemas with affective inhibition were associated with stronger (rather than weaker) CPM may reflect a compensatory emotional suppression mechanism, where individuals who have learned to inhibit affect in relational contexts develop heightened descending pain inhibition. This has implications for understanding the complex relationship between attachment history, emotion regulation, and somatic presentations in clinical practice.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with chronic pain and somatic symptom disorders; those treating fibromyalgia or functional somatic syndromes; therapists working with patients who have concurrent loneliness and physical health complaints; researchers investigating the neurobiology of social pain and physical pain overlap.

title: Heartbeat perception is dissociable into subjective detection and temporal judgement
authors: Yusuke Haruki; Keisuke Suzuki; Yuri Terasawa; Kenji Ogawa; Olaf Blanke; Yuko Yotsumoto
summary: This study introduces the oneshot heartbeat detection task, a novel paradigm that separates subjective heartbeat detection from cardiovisual synchrony judgement within the same trial, addressing the long-standing problem of poor convergence across heartbeat perception measures (meta-analytic shared variance of only 4.4% between counting and discrimination tasks). Across three experiments (N = 43, 24, 21; 62 unique individuals), the authors found that subjective heartbeat detection on a given trial increased confidence in the subsequent synchrony judgement but not its accuracy (replicated across all three experiments). Synchrony sensitivity remained stable across waiting intervals from 2 to 22 heartbeats, arguing against an attentional decay account. Pooled cross-study analysis (N = 88 experiment-level summaries) revealed that conventional heartbeat counting accuracy was associated with how frequently participants detected heartbeats (standardized beta = .364, BF = 441 for inclusion) and with their response criterion or willingness to endorse synchrony (beta = -.263, BF = 14.1), but not with synchrony sensitivity itself (beta = .088, BF = 0.424). Crucially, the matched sound condition showed no such relationship between detection and heartbeat counting, indicating the detection component is specific to the cardiac signal rather than a general response effect. The authors argue that weak convergence across cardiac tasks reflects genuine component structure (detection vs. temporal precision) rather than measurement failure.
clinical_insight: Interoceptive awareness, commonly measured via heartbeat perception tasks, is implicated in a wide range of clinical conditions including anxiety disorders (heightened interoception), depression (blunted interoception), eating disorders, somatic symptom disorders, and substance use. However, the field has been plagued by inconsistent findings, with different heartbeat tasks showing near-zero convergence. This study resolves a fundamental measurement problem by demonstrating that heartbeat perception is not a single ability but comprises at least two dissociable components: the subjective detection of a heartbeat and the temporal precision of placing it relative to external events. For clinical assessment, this means that a patient's score on the commonly used heartbeat counting task primarily reflects their subjective tendency to detect heartbeats and their response criterion, not their actual interoceptive accuracy. A patient who scores highly on heartbeat counting may simply be more willing to report feeling their heartbeat rather than being more accurate at perceiving it. Conversely, a low score may reflect conservative response tendencies rather than poor interoception. This has profound implications for how clinicians interpret interoceptive measures in clinical practice and research. The oneshot task offers a way to separately quantify these components, potentially improving the predictive validity of interoceptive assessment for clinical outcomes. For example, in anxiety treatment, tracking changes in detection sensitivity versus response criterion across therapy could provide more precise information about which aspect of interoceptive processing is changing.
relevant_for: Researchers and clinicians using interoception measures in anxiety, depression, eating disorders, and somatic symptom disorders; those developing or refining psychophysiological assessment tools; psychotherapy researchers investigating embodied cognition and body-focused therapeutic approaches.

title: Social anxiety and rumination
authors: Nexhmedin Morina, Thomas Meyer, Peter McCarthy, Thole H. Hoppen, Pascal Schlechter
summary: Investigates the relationship between social anxiety and rumination patterns, examining how repetitive negative thinking maintains social anxiety symptomatology.
clinical_insight: Post-event rumination is a key mechanism that maintains social anxiety by reinforcing negative self-imagery and threat appraisals after social encounters. Therapists should directly target repetitive negative thinking through techniques such as detached mindfulness, rumination-focused CBT, and behavioral experiments that test feared social outcomes. Intervening on rumination may yield greater symptom reduction than standard exposure alone.
relevant_for: Therapists specializing in social anxiety disorder and CBT

title: N-of-1 depression causality analysis
authors: Unknown
summary: Explores causal inference approaches for depression using N-of-1 designs, examining within-person temporal dynamics of depressive symptoms and potential causal mechanisms.
clinical_insight: N-of-1 causal methods enable therapists to identify which specific triggers and behaviors drive depressive episodes for individual clients, rather than relying on group-level predictors. Clinicians can use repeated symptom monitoring data to collaboratively map each patient's unique causal network and tailor interventions to disrupt personally relevant maintenance cycles. This approach supports precision mental health by shifting from diagnostic protocols to individualized formulation.
relevant_for: CBT therapists treating recurrent or treatment-resistant depression

title: Ecological momentary assessment for nonsuicidal self-injury
authors: Lisa Störkel, Inga Niedtfeld, Christian Schmahl, Johanna Hepp
summary: Uses EMA methodology to capture real-time data on NSSI urges and behaviors, providing ecological validity to understanding self-injury patterns in daily life.
clinical_insight: Ecological momentary assessment reveals that NSSI urges and acts are often preceded by specific real-time emotional and interpersonal triggers that retrospective self-report fails to capture. Clinicians can use these findings to develop individualized safety plans grounded in patients' actual daily patterns rather than assumed risk factors. Incorporating brief between-session EMA check-ins may also improve crisis prediction and timely intervention delivery.
relevant_for: Dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) clinicians working with self-harm

title: ADHD and neurodiversity
authors: Saskia van der Oord, Gert-Jan Vanaken, Blandine French
summary: Examines ADHD through a neurodiversity-affirming lens, discussing implications for clinical practice and assessment approaches that move beyond deficit-based models.
clinical_insight: A neurodiversity-affirming approach reframes ADHD traits as variations in cognition rather than deficits, reducing internalized stigma and improving therapeutic alliance. Therapists should adapt assessment to include strengths-based measures and collaborate with clients on environmental accommodations rather than solely targeting symptom reduction. This shift can improve treatment engagement, especially among adults who have had negative experiences with pathologizing clinical models.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists conducting adult ADHD assessments

title: Intolerance of uncertainty in clinical contexts
authors: Dalainey H. Drakes, Emmanuelle Rochon, Allison Ouimet
summary: Uses a mixed-methods design to examine how intolerance of uncertainty and anxiety shape the lived experiences of 139 individuals with chronic physical health and pain conditions, revealing IU as a cross-condition risk factor that extends beyond mental health diagnoses into daily functioning and healthcare engagement.
clinical_insight: Intolerance of uncertainty is a transdiagnostic maintaining factor across anxiety, depression, and obsessive-compulsive presentations, making it a high-yield treatment target regardless of primary diagnosis. Therapists can integrate uncertainty exposure and metacognitive strategies into existing protocols to address this common thread. Assessing IU at intake helps clinicians anticipate treatment barriers such as avoidance, reassurance-seeking, and therapy non-completion.
relevant_for: Transdiagnostic CBT practitioners and clinical psychologists

title: Couples therapy after brain injury
authors: Gerard Anthony Riley, Urszula Gajewska, Barbara Hagger, Asweeni Jansdin Raj, Natasha Yasmin, Giles Yeates
summary: Examines therapeutic approaches for couples navigating relationship challenges following acquired brain injury, addressing the intersection of neurological and relational factors.
clinical_insight: Standard couples therapy models often fail when one partner has an acquired brain injury because cognitive, emotional, and communication changes require significant adaptation. Therapists need to integrate psychoeducation about neurological sequelae, modify communication exercises for cognitive-linguistic deficits, and address grief and identity shifts in both partners. Tailoring interventions to the specific cognitive profile of the injured partner can significantly improve relational outcomes.
relevant_for: Couples therapists working in neuropsychological rehabilitation settings

title: Bipolar disorder and cognition
authors: Benjamin Katz, Personality Cognition and Psychopathology Lab - Iftah Yovel
summary: Investigates cognitive functioning across mood states in bipolar disorder through meta-analytic review, documenting trait-level impairments in executive function, verbal memory, and processing speed that persist during euthymia and significantly impact occupational and social functioning beyond acute symptom phases.
clinical_insight: Cognitive deficits in bipolar disorder persist during euthymic periods and directly impair occupational and social functioning, yet are rarely addressed in standard mood-stabilization treatment. Therapists should incorporate cognitive remediation strategies and compensatory techniques into treatment plans alongside pharmacotherapy and psychoeducation. Regular cognitive screening can help differentiate whether functional difficulties are driven by mood symptoms, cognitive impairment, or both.
relevant_for: Psychiatrists and clinical psychologists treating bipolar disorder

title: Eating disorder network analysis
authors: Jenni Leppanen, Dalia Brown, Hannah McLinden, Kate Tchanturia, Steven Williams
summary: Applies network analysis methods to understand symptom interactions in eating disorders, identifying central symptoms and potential treatment targets.
clinical_insight: Network analysis identifies specific central symptoms in eating disorders—such as fear of weight gain or body dissatisfaction—that, when targeted, may produce cascading improvements across the broader symptom network. Clinicians can use these findings to prioritize treatment targets and track network-level change rather than focusing solely on global symptom severity. This approach supports more efficient allocation of limited therapy time toward the most impactful intervention points.
relevant_for: Eating disorder specialists and clinical psychologists

title: Depression treatment outcomes
authors: David John Hallford, David W. Austin, Keisuke Takano, Joseph Yeow, Danielle Rusanov, Matthew Fuller-Tyszkiewicz, Filip Raes
summary: Examines predictors and patterns of depression treatment outcomes through systematic analysis, identifying factors associated with treatment response and non-response to inform measurement-based care models that enable early intervention adjustment rather than wait-and-see approaches.
clinical_insight: Understanding predictors of non-response to depression treatment—such as comorbid personality pathology, early adversity, or cognitive reserve—allows clinicians to proactively adjust treatment intensity or modality before a full trial fails. Therapists should systematically track early change signals and be prepared to switch or augment interventions by session 6-8 if expected trajectories are not observed. This evidence supports a measurement-based care model rather than a wait-and-see approach.
relevant_for: Outpatient mental health clinicians treating major depressive disorder

title: EMA methodology for NSSI research
authors: Glenn Kiekens, Laurence Claes, Steffie Schoefs, Nian D. F. Kemme, koen luyckx, Evan Kleiman, Matthew K. Nock, Inez Myin-Germeys
summary: Advances ecological momentary assessment methodology for capturing nonsuicidal self-injury in naturalistic settings, demonstrating how real-time monitoring reveals specific emotional and interpersonal triggers that retrospective self-report fails to capture, with implications for dynamic safety planning.
clinical_insight: Advances in EMA methodology for NSSI research offer clinicians practical tools for implementing real-time monitoring in clinical practice, such as smartphone-based urge tracking between sessions. These tools can transform safety planning from a static document into a dynamic, data-informed process that evolves with the patient's real-world patterns. Clinicians adopting EMA-informed approaches may detect worsening trajectories earlier and intervene before self-injury occurs.
relevant_for: Clinical researchers and DBT clinicians studying self-injury

title: Intolerance of uncertainty transdiagnostic review
authors: Tim Sandhu, Bowen Xiao, Rebecca P. Lawson
summary: Systematically reviews intolerance of uncertainty as a transdiagnostic maintaining factor across anxiety disorders, depression, OCD, and PTSD, confirming IU as one of the most robust transdiagnostic treatment targets and providing guidance for dedicated IU-focused intervention modules.
clinical_insight: This systematic review confirms that intolerance of uncertainty maintains pathology across anxiety disorders, depression, OCD, and PTSD, making it one of the most robust transdiagnostic targets available. Clinicians should consider adding a dedicated IU-focused module—such as uncertainty exposure, behavioral experiments with deliberate ambiguity, or metacognitive therapy for worry—to any protocol where IU is elevated. Measuring IU change during treatment can serve as an early indicator of broader symptom improvement.
relevant_for: Transdiagnostic therapists and CBT clinicians across anxiety and mood disorders

title: PEP therapy
authors: Unknown
summary: Explores the therapeutic model and clinical applications of PEP (Prescriptive Experiential Psychotherapy) as an approach to emotional processing.
clinical_insight: Prescriptive Experiential Psychotherapy (PEP) provides a structured yet emotion-focused framework that guides therapists in selecting specific experiential interventions based on session-by-session markers of emotional processing. Clinicians can use PEP's prescriptive principles to determine when to deepen emotional experiencing, when to use chair work, and when to shift to reflective meaning-making. This model offers a bridge between manualized protocols and the flexibility demanded by complex clinical presentations.
relevant_for: Emotion-focused therapists and experiential psychotherapy practitioners

title: Cognitive functioning in bipolar disorder
authors: Iain Campbell, Ivana Kamenska, Erin Louise Bellamy
summary: Meta-analytically reviews cognitive impairments in bipolar disorder across different mood states and treatment phases, establishing that deficits in executive function, verbal memory, and processing speed represent trait-level vulnerabilities rather than episode-specific phenomena requiring targeted cognitive remediation alongside mood stabilization.
clinical_insight: Meta-analytic findings confirm that cognitive impairments in bipolar disorder are not limited to acute episodes but represent a trait-level vulnerability affecting executive function, verbal memory, and processing speed. Therapists should advocate for cognitive accommodations at work and in daily life, use compensatory strategies during therapy sessions, and set realistic functional expectations. Incorporating cognitive rehabilitation exercises alongside mood management may improve both occupational outcomes and treatment adherence.
relevant_for: Psychiatrists and neuropsychologists managing bipolar disorder

title: N-of-1 depression causality (extended)
authors: Unknown
summary: Further examination of N-of-1 causal methods for understanding individual depression trajectories and personalized treatment planning.
clinical_insight: Extended N-of-1 causal methods move beyond identifying triggers to modeling time-lagged causal chains within individual depression trajectories, enabling truly personalized treatment planning. Therapists can use individual causal graphs collaboratively with clients to visually demonstrate how specific behaviors, thoughts, and contexts cascade into depressive episodes and where intervention points lie. This methodology is particularly valuable for patients with atypical or non-linear depression courses.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists specializing in personalized depression treatment

title: Therapeutic approaches for social anxiety rumination
authors: Nexhmedin Morina, Thomas Meyer, Peter McCarthy, Thole H. Hoppen, Pascal Schlechter
summary: Investigates therapeutic interventions targeting rumination in social anxiety disorder, examining mechanisms of change.
clinical_insight: Targeting rumination in social anxiety requires distinct strategies from standard exposure, including cognitive defusion from post-event review, attention training to interrupt self-focused processing, and behavioral experiments that directly test rumination content. Therapists should assess rumination frequency and its functional role at intake, as high ruminators may need extended treatment or integration of third-wave CBT techniques. Reducing rumination appears to enhance the durability of exposure gains by preventing relapse through negative self-review.
relevant_for: CBT therapists treating social anxiety disorder

title: Neurodiversity-affirming ADHD assessment
authors: Unknown
summary: Proposes neurodiversity-affirming frameworks for ADHD assessment that complement traditional diagnostic approaches.
clinical_insight: Neurodiversity-affirming ADHD assessment frameworks complement traditional diagnostic criteria by incorporating strengths profiles, environmental fit analysis, and self-identified support needs. Clinicians using these frameworks can provide more validating assessment feedback and co-create intervention plans that prioritize environmental adaptation over behavioral conformity. This is especially important for adults, women, and people of color who are frequently missed or misdiagnosed by standard assessments.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and clinical psychologists conducting ADHD evaluations

title: Symptom networks in eating disorders
authors: Unknown
summary: Network analysis of eating disorder symptoms identifying bridge symptoms between core ED pathology and comorbid conditions.
clinical_insight: Identifying bridge symptoms between eating disorder pathology and comorbid conditions—such as perfectionism linking ED symptoms to anxiety, or emotional dysregulation linking to depression—enables clinicians to target nodes with the greatest cross-disorder impact. Therapists should map individual symptom networks at intake and prioritize interventions on bridge symptoms to simultaneously reduce ED severity and comorbid distress. This network-informed approach may reduce the need for separate sequential treatments.
relevant_for: Eating disorder clinicians treating patients with complex comorbidity

title: Iatrogenic harm in BPD treatment
authors: Laurence Cobbaert
summary: Examines ways in which borderline personality disorder treatment can inadvertently cause harm, identifying systemic and clinical factors contributing to iatrogenic outcomes.
clinical_insight: BPD treatment can inadvertently cause harm through therapist burnout-driven invalidation, rigid boundary enforcement that feels punitive, or over-reliance on crisis services that reinforce dependency. Clinicians should monitor for signs of iatrogenic deterioration—such as increasing crisis frequency—and adjust their approach using consistent validation, collaborative treatment goals, and regular treatment reviews.
relevant_for: DBT and mentalization-based therapy clinicians treating borderline personality disorder

title: Cognitive bias modification for interpretation (CBM-I)
authors: M. Max Larrazabal, Jeremy William Eberle, Angel F. Vela de la Garza Evia, Mehdi Boukhechba, Dan Funk, Laura Barnes, Steven M. Boker, Bethany Teachman
summary: Evaluates cognitive bias modification for interpretation (CBM-I) as a standalone and adjunctive intervention for anxiety and depression, examining training effects on interpretation bias, clinical symptoms, and emotional processing across multiple randomized controlled trials with varied clinical populations.
clinical_insight: CBM-I provides a structured, accessible adjunct to traditional anxiety treatment by directly training patients to reinterpret ambiguous information in a less threatening way. Therapists can assign CBM-I exercises as between-session homework or use them in-session to complement cognitive restructuring, particularly for clients who struggle to engage in verbal Socratic questioning or have limited insight into their biased interpretations.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners treating anxiety disorders, especially with resistant or mild-moderate presentations

title: Sentiment analysis in text-based therapy
authors: Masataka Nakayama, Chihiro, Hatanaka, Hisae Konakawa, Yuka Suzuki, Alethea H.Q. Koh, Yasushi Sugihara, Toshio Kawai
summary: Applies sentiment analysis to text-based therapy transcripts, demonstrating computational approaches to monitoring therapeutic process and patient affect.
clinical_insight: Computational sentiment analysis of therapy transcripts can detect shifts in patient affect that may not be apparent to the therapist in real-time, serving as a potential early warning system for treatment deterioration. Clinicians offering text-based therapy or supervision can use these tools to review session patterns, identify emotional themes that warrant follow-up, and track progress in a more quantifiable way alongside clinical judgment.
relevant_for: Therapists providing online text-based therapy and clinical supervisors monitoring session quality

title: Interpersonal emotion regulation in couples
authors: Amna Akram, Mahwish Iqbal
summary: Examines how romantic partners regulate each other's emotions, and how interpersonal emotion regulation patterns relate to relationship and individual mental health outcomes.
clinical_insight: Couples therapists should assess not only how each partner self-regulates emotions but how they co-regulate or dysregulate each other, identifying patterns such as co-rumination, emotional flooding, or dismissive responses that perpetuate distress. Interventions targeting interpersonal emotion regulation — like teaching partners to accurately read and responsively soothe each other's emotional states — can improve both relationship satisfaction and individual mental health outcomes.
relevant_for: Couples and family therapists working with relational distress and emotional dysregulation

title: Depression and avoidance generalization
authors: Ryan J. Tomm, Liz Kalenteridis, Amanda C. Lee, Ashton Thorpe, Evan  White, Luke Clark, Stan B. Floresco, Trisha Chakrabarty, Rebecca M. Todd
summary: Investigates how depression is associated with过度generalization of avoidance behaviors, linking depressive cognition to behavioral patterns across contexts.
clinical_insight: Depressed patients often generalize avoidance from one stressful context to many unrelated situations, reinforcing behavioral withdrawal and depressive maintenance. Therapists should use behavioral experiments and graded exposure to help clients discriminate between contexts where avoidance was originally adaptive versus where it now functions as a barrier, directly targeting this overgeneralization pattern within CBT protocols.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners treating depression with comorbid anxiety and avoidance behaviors

title: Self-stigma in dissociative disorders
authors: Jeremy Cooper
summary: Examines the experience and impact of self-stigma among individuals with dissociative disorders, including its effects on help-seeking and treatment engagement.
clinical_insight: Self-stigma in dissociative disorders significantly undermines help-seeking and treatment engagement, meaning therapists must address internalized shame as a primary treatment target rather than assuming clients will readily disclose symptoms. Psychoeducation about the neurobiological basis of dissociation, normalizing experiences, and incorporating compassion-focused or acceptance-based approaches can reduce self-stigma and improve retention in phase-based trauma treatment.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists specializing in dissociative disorders and complex PTSD

title: Therapist-client affect dynamics in psychotherapy
authors: Miriam Hehlmann, Björn S. Siepe, Fabienne Mink, Juan Segundo Peña Loray, Juan Martín Gómez Penedo, Jessica Fritz, Danilo Moggia N., Julian Rubel, Wolfgang Lutz
summary: Uses dynamic systems modeling to examine therapist-client affect dynamics during psychotherapy sessions, identifying temporal patterns of emotional synchrony and mismatch that predict treatment outcomes and rupture-repair events across different therapeutic modalities.
clinical_insight: Therapists should develop awareness of their own emotional patterns during sessions, as moments of affective synchrony can deepen therapeutic alliance while persistent discord may signal ruptures needing repair. Training in affect attunement — monitoring and subtly matching or complementing the client's emotional tone — can be a practical skill for improving outcomes, particularly with clients who have difficulty identifying and expressing emotions.
relevant_for: Psychodynamic and relational psychotherapists focused on the therapeutic alliance

title: Personality disorder and ADHD overlap in adults
authors: Ludwig Ohse, Emanuel Jauk, Phileas Heim, Christine Knaevelsrud, Maria Böttche, André Kerber
summary: Examines the diagnostic overlap between ADHD and personality disorders in adults, discussing differential diagnosis and treatment implications of comorbidity.
clinical_insight: The diagnostic overlap between ADHD and personality disorders frequently leads to misdiagnosis or missed comorbidity, so clinicians should conduct thorough developmental histories and use validated ADHD screening tools even when personality pathology is prominent. Accurate differentiation matters because ADHD-responsive treatments — such as stimulant medication, executive function coaching, and CBT-ADHD protocols — may reduce functional impairment that has been misattributed solely to personality dysfunction.
relevant_for: Psychiatrists and clinical psychologists conducting adult ADHD and personality disorder assessments

title: Depression treatment outcomes and their predictors
authors: David John Hallford, David W. Austin, Keisuke Takano, Joseph Yeow, Danielle Rusanov, Matthew Fuller-Tyszkiewicz, Filip Raes
summary: Examines predictors of treatment outcomes in depression, identifying patient-level and therapy-level factors associated with response.
clinical_insight: Identifying specific predictors of depression treatment response enables clinicians to make more informed decisions about treatment selection and intensity at the outset of care. Therapists can use patient-level factors—such as chronicity and comorbidity—to set realistic expectations, adjust treatment pacing, and consider stepping up to alternative interventions earlier when poor-response indicators are present.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating depressive disorders in outpatient and community settings

title: Reconceptualizing schizophrenia relapse
authors: Carlos De las Cuevas
summary: Proposes a reconceptualization of relapse in schizophrenia that moves beyond symptom-based definitions to incorporate functional and subjective recovery dimensions.
clinical_insight: Redefining relapse in schizophrenia to include functional decline and subjective loss of well-being — not just symptom recurrence — allows clinicians to intervene earlier and more holistically. Therapists should collaboratively monitor functional markers such as social withdrawal, daily routine disruption, and self-reported quality of life alongside traditional symptom ratings, enabling preemptive adjustment of psychosocial and pharmacological interventions before full relapse occurs.
relevant_for: Community mental health teams and ACT/CBT practitioners in early intervention for psychosis

title: Prescriptive Experiential Psychotherapy (PEP): clinical applications
authors: Unknown
summary: Details the PEP therapeutic model and its application to various clinical presentations, discussing its theoretical foundations and practical implementation.
clinical_insight: PEP offers a structured yet flexible framework that combines experiential techniques with prescriptive treatment matching, allowing therapists to tailor interventions based on patient presentation and readiness. Clinicians can draw on PEP's principles to select and sequence experiential exercises—such as chair work, imagery, and emotional focusing—based on formulation-driven treatment planning rather than a one-size-fits-all approach.
relevant_for: Experiential and emotion-focused therapists seeking integrative treatment frameworks

title: Predictors of psychotherapy dropout
authors: Benjamin Giber, Abhishek Sharma, Daniel Broadie, Thomas McCoy, Faith Gunning, Roy Perlis, Finale Doshi-Velez, Nili Solomonov
summary: Systematically identifies predictors of psychotherapy dropout across modalities and diagnostic groups through meta-analytic methods, finding that early therapeutic alliance, patient expectations, and demographic factors interact to predict premature termination, with implications for pre-therapy preparation and retention strategies.
clinical_insight: With identified predictors of premature dropout spanning patient expectations, therapeutic alliance quality, and structural barriers, clinicians should proactively address dropout risk at intake by negotiating clear treatment goals, discussing expectations openly, and monitoring alliance early. Implementing routine outcome monitoring and providing motivational enhancement at critical early sessions can significantly improve retention, especially for clients with personality pathology, low readiness for change, or practical access barriers.
relevant_for: Clinic directors and therapists seeking to improve treatment retention across modalities

title: Multi-Trauma Inference Integration Framework (MTIIF) for complex trauma
authors: Unknown
summary: Introduces the MTIIF, a framework for understanding and treating complex trauma presentations that integrates multiple trauma exposures and their interacting effects.
clinical_insight: The MTIIF provides a structured approach to conceptualizing how multiple trauma exposures interact to create complex clinical presentations, moving beyond single-event trauma models. Therapists can use this framework to map the cumulative and interactive effects of repeated traumas, prioritize which trauma narratives to process first, and anticipate how processing one memory may activate or clarify others.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists working with complex PTSD and multiply-traumatized clients

title: Suicidal ideation response inventory
authors: Jai Carmichael, Alexia Samiotis, Kayla Andrews, Jao-Yue Carminati, Lisa Johnston, Gershon Spitz, Kate Rachel Gould, Jennie Ponsford
summary: Develops and validates an inventory measuring responses to suicidal ideation, creating a tool for clinical assessment of how patients manage suicidal thoughts.
clinical_insight: This new inventory for measuring how patients respond to and manage suicidal ideation gives clinicians a structured way to assess protective cognitive and behavioral strategies — such as reason-giving, distraction, and help-seeking — alongside risk factors. Therapists can use it to identify which coping responses a patient lacks, then target those specific deficits in safety planning and skills-based interventions like DBT's crisis survival strategies.
relevant_for: Suicide prevention clinicians and DBT therapists conducting risk formulations

title: ADHD in women and adversity
authors: Rosaline Qi, Phuc T. Nguyen
summary: Investigates how experiences of adversity and gender-specific pathways shape ADHD presentation in women, examining the interaction between childhood adversity, hormonal factors, and social expectations that contribute to delayed diagnosis and internalizing comorbidity in female ADHD populations.
clinical_insight: Women with ADHD frequently experience cumulative adversity — including relationship violence, educational dismissal, and medical misattribution of symptoms — creating a cycle where undiagnosed ADHD increases adversity risk and adversity worsens ADHD impairment. Clinicians should maintain a high index of suspicion for ADHD in women presenting with trauma histories, and integrate executive function support into trauma-informed treatment to address both pathways simultaneously.
relevant_for: Women's mental health clinicians and trauma therapists assessing for ADHD in adult women

title: Predictors of intimate partner violence
authors: Kelly Jordan, Dean Fido
summary: Identifies psychological and relational predictors of intimate partner violence perpetration through systematic review and meta-analysis, examining the relative contributions of attachment insecurity, emotion dysregulation, trauma history, and substance use across perpetrator gender and relationship type.
clinical_insight: Clinicians conducting couples assessments can use the identified risk factor profiles to inform structured risk formulations, enabling earlier identification of escalating dynamics. Findings support integrating individual-level risk screening (e.g., emotion dysregulation, substance use) into couples therapy intake to guide safety planning and targeted referrals.
relevant_for: Couples therapists and domestic violence intervention program clinicians

title: Cognitive reappraisal efficacy and mechanisms
authors: Unknown
summary: Meta-analytic review of cognitive reappraisal as an emotion regulation strategy, examining its efficacy across disorders and identifying moderators of effectiveness.
clinical_insight: This meta-analysis clarifies when and for whom cognitive reappraisal is most effective, helping therapists determine whether to prioritize this strategy or complement it with alternative emotion regulation techniques. Clinicians can use the identified moderators—such as disorder type, symptom severity, or cognitive capacity—to personalize reappraisal training and set appropriate expectations for its role within a broader emotion regulation repertoire.
relevant_for: CBT and emotion-focused therapists treating mood and anxiety disorders

title: Contingency instructions and fear extinction
authors: Annalisa Lipp, Christian Merz, Beray Macit, Marcella Lydia Woud, Oliver T. Wolf, Jürgen Margraf, Armin Zlomuzica
summary: Tests how contingency instructions during exposure therapy influence fear extinction learning and recall, using experimental paradigms to determine whether informing participants about stimulus-outcome contingencies enhances or inhibits extinction processes relevant to optimizing clinical exposure protocols.
clinical_insight: Therapists conducting exposure therapy should carefully consider how they frame the rationale for extinction — explicit contingency instructions that clarify the safety of the exposure context can enhance inhibitory learning. This supports using more precise psychoeducation scripts that specify why feared outcomes no longer apply in the exposure setting.
relevant_for: Exposure therapists and anxiety disorder treatment specialists

title: Couples therapy approaches and outcomes
authors: Unknown
summary: Reviews evidence for different couples therapy approaches, comparing efficacy across presenting problems and identifying common change mechanisms.
clinical_insight: Understanding the comparative efficacy and common change mechanisms across couples therapy models helps clinicians select the most appropriate approach for specific relational presentations. Therapists can focus on identified common factors—such as improved communication and emotional responsiveness—while choosing the modality that best fits the couple's conflict style and attachment dynamics.
relevant_for: Couples therapists and marriage and family therapists selecting evidence-based treatment approaches

title: Autistic parents: support needs and experiences
authors: Laura Fox, Kathryn Asbury
summary: Explores the support needs and lived experiences of autistic parents through qualitative research, documenting unique challenges including sensory demands of parenting, navigating healthcare and education systems, managing social expectations, and the intersection of autism-related executive functioning differences with parenting responsibilities.
clinical_insight: Clinicians supporting autistic parents should assess for sensory overload, social camouflaging fatigue, and executive function demands specific to parenting rather than assuming standard parenting interventions fit. Adapting parent-training programs to accommodate neurodivergent processing styles and offering practical environmental accommodations can significantly improve family functioning.
relevant_for: Perinatal and family clinicians working with neurodivergent populations

title: Suicide disclosure patterns and clinical response
authors: Unknown
summary: Investigates how patients disclose suicidal ideation in clinical settings and how clinicians respond, identifying gaps and best practices for suicide risk assessment.
clinical_insight: Patients often disclose suicidality indirectly or incrementally, meaning clinicians must be attuned to subtle verbal and nonverbal cues rather than relying solely on direct questioning. Therapists should create explicit opportunities for disclosure, respond with nonjudgmental validation rather than immediate risk-management reflexes, and use collaborative safety planning that preserves the therapeutic alliance while ensuring appropriate care escalation.
relevant_for: All mental health clinicians conducting suicide risk assessments in any treatment setting

title: Transdiagnostic anhedonia: mechanisms and treatment
authors: Unknown
summary: Reviews anhedonia as a transdiagnostic construct across depression, schizophrenia, and substance use disorders, examining shared mechanisms.
clinical_insight: Recognizing anhedonia as a transdiagnostic target allows therapists to move beyond disorder-specific protocols and apply reward-sensitization interventions — such as behavioral activation, positive event scheduling, and approach-oriented exposure — across depression, schizophrenia, and substance use presentations. Clinicians should routinely assess anhedonia severity using tools like the SHAPS and address reward-processing deficits early in treatment to prevent chronicity and functional decline.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating mood disorders, psychosis, and addiction in community mental health settings

title: MDMA and social functioning: systematic review
authors: Unknown
summary: Systematic review of MDMA's effects on social functioning, with implications for understanding its therapeutic mechanism in social anxiety and PTSD.
clinical_insight: MDMA's pro-social effects — including enhanced trust, empathy, and reduced fear of social evaluation — suggest it may serve as a catalyst for processing interpersonal trauma in PTSD and social anxiety treatment. Therapists integrating MDMA-assisted therapy should prepare patients for intensified emotional processing sessions and plan structured integration work afterward to consolidate relational gains into daily life.
relevant_for: Clinicians providing MDMA-assisted psychotherapy for PTSD and social anxiety

title: State mindfulness and repetitive negative thought
authors: Unknown
summary: Investigates the relationship between state mindfulness and repetitive negative thinking, examining mindfulness as a moderator of rumination and worry.
clinical_insight: Momentary mindfulness appears to disrupt the escalation of repetitive negative thinking, suggesting therapists should teach clients brief, in-session mindfulness exercises — such as anchored breathing or body scan micro-practices — to use at the first sign of rumination or worry. Rather than relying solely on formal meditation homework, clinicians should emphasize state-level mindfulness as a real-time coping skill accessible during daily stressors.
relevant_for: Mindfulness-based therapists and CBT practitioners treating rumination and generalized worry

title: Shame experiences in autistic adults
authors: Unknown
summary: Qualitative exploration of shame experiences among autistic adults, examining how masking, social difficulties, and societal attitudes contribute to chronic shame.
clinical_insight: Autistic adults carry pervasive shame rooted in years of masking, social rejection, and internalized ableism that often goes unaddressed in standard autism-supportive therapy. Clinicians should explicitly explore shame narratives, validate the exhaustion of camouflaging, and use interventions from compassion-focused therapy or radical acceptance frameworks to help clients decouple their self-worth from neurotypical social expectations.
relevant_for: Neurodiversity-affirming therapists working with autistic adults

title: Future thinking fluency: meta-analysis
authors: Unknown
summary: Meta-analytic review of future thinking fluency across clinical populations, examining its role as a cognitive marker for depression and suicidality.
clinical_insight: Impaired future thinking fluency — difficulty generating specific, vivid mental representations of future events — is a robust cognitive marker for depression and suicidality that therapists can assess and target directly. Clinicians should incorporate future-oriented interventions such as positive future imagery exercises, goal-setting with mental simulation, and values-based behavioral activation to strengthen prospective thinking and reduce suicide risk.
relevant_for: Suicide risk assessors and CBT therapists working with depressed and suicidal patients

title: Autism and ADHD camouflaging: clinical implications
authors: Unknown
summary: Reviews camouflaging behaviors in autism and ADHD, examining their mental health consequences and implications for assessment accuracy in adults.
clinical_insight: Camouflaging in autism and ADHD is associated with significant mental health costs including burnout, anxiety, and delayed diagnosis, so assessors must look beyond surface presentation and use multi-informant or self-report measures that capture internal experience. Therapists should support clients in reducing compensatory masking where safe and appropriate, and address the underlying anxiety and identity confusion that camouflaging creates rather than reinforcing assimilation to neurotypical norms.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and diagnosticians assessing adult autism and ADHD

title: Psilocybin for alcohol use disorder
authors: Unknown
summary: Examines emerging evidence for psilocybin-assisted therapy in treating alcohol use disorder, discussing proposed mechanisms and preliminary clinical data.
clinical_insight: Psilocybin-assisted therapy shows preliminary promise for reducing alcohol use by promoting insight into addictive patterns, enhancing motivation for change, and fostering a sense of meaning beyond substance use. Clinicians working in addiction settings should stay informed about emerging regulatory developments and be prepared to integrate preparation and integration psychotherapy sessions around psychedelic experiences, while maintaining evidence-based relapse prevention strategies as the core of treatment.
relevant_for: Addiction psychiatrists and therapists exploring psychedelic-assisted treatments for substance use disorders

title: Holotropic states framework for psychotherapy
authors: Unknown
summary: Proposes a theoretical framework for understanding holotropic (non-ordinary) states in psychotherapy and their potential therapeutic applications.
clinical_insight: A framework for understanding holotropic or non-ordinary states of consciousness provides therapists with a conceptual map for working with clients who experience altered states through meditation, breathwork, spiritual emergencies, or psychedelic use. Clinicians can use this framework to normalize these experiences, distinguish between transformative and destabilizing states, and integrate insights from non-ordinary consciousness into conventional psychotherapy without pathologizing the experience.
relevant_for: Transpersonal therapists and clinicians working with spiritual or psychedelic experiences

title: Hostile attribution bias in personality pathology
authors: Unknown
summary: Investigates hostile attribution bias across personality disorders, examining how interpersonal perception distortions maintain pathological personality patterns.
clinical_insight: Hostile attribution bias — the tendency to interpret ambiguous social cues as intentionally harmful — is a key maintaining factor in personality pathology, particularly in borderline and paranoid presentations. Therapists should use social information-processing training, mentalization-based techniques, and real-time interpersonal experiments within sessions to help clients slow their automatic interpretations, consider alternative explanations, and test biases through behavioral experiments.
relevant_for: Schema therapists and MBT practitioners treating personality disorders

title: Machine learning for therapist-patient matching
authors: Unknown
summary: Tests whether machine learning algorithms can predict optimal therapist-patient matching, reporting null results that challenge assumptions about algorithmic matching.
clinical_insight: The null findings for algorithmic therapist-patient matching suggest that resources currently invested in complex matching systems may be better directed toward training all therapists in evidence-based alliance-building skills and common factors. Clinic administrators should focus on ensuring consistent treatment quality, robust supervision, and regular outcome feedback rather than relying on proprietary matching algorithms to improve patient outcomes.
relevant_for: Behavioral health clinic administrators and healthcare system designers

title: Time-passage experience in schizophrenia: EMA study
authors: Unknown
summary: Uses ecological momentary assessment to study alterations in time-passage experience in schizophrenia, linking temporal distortion to symptom severity.
clinical_insight: Alterations in the subjective experience of time passing are common in schizophrenia and correlate with symptom severity, meaning therapists should routinely assess temporal distortions as part of symptom monitoring. Grounding techniques, structured daily scheduling, and mindfulness-based attention to present-moment anchoring can help patients re-establish a more stable sense of temporal flow, potentially reducing dissociative and psychotic experiences.
relevant_for: Psychosis clinicians and occupational therapists in community mental health

title: Mindfulness facets and depression mechanisms
authors: Unknown
summary: Dismantles the relationship between different mindfulness facets and depression, identifying which specific mindfulness components drive therapeutic change.
clinical_insight: Not all mindfulness facets contribute equally to depression reduction; this dismantling research suggests therapists should prioritize teaching non-judgmental awareness and decentering over generic relaxation or attentional focus. By identifying which specific mindfulness skills drive improvement for a given patient, clinicians can personalize mindfulness-based interventions rather than delivering one-size-fits-all protocols.
relevant_for: MBCT and mindfulness-based therapists treating recurrent depression

title: Psychological distancing in bereavement: RCT
authors: Unknown
summary: Randomized controlled trial testing psychological distancing interventions for prolonged grief, examining effects on emotional processing and adjustment to loss.
clinical_insight: Psychological distancing techniques — helping bereaved individuals observe grief-related thoughts and memories from a detached, third-person perspective — show promise in RCTs for reducing prolonged grief symptom severity.
