Therapists should teach clients concrete distancing strategies such as temporal distancing, linguistic distancing in journaling, and imagery rescripting to facilitate adaptive emotional processing without emotional avoidance or rumination.
relevant_for: Grief counselors and bereavement therapists treating complicated or prolonged grief

title: Early maladaptive schemas in gay men
authors: Unknown
summary: Examines early maladaptive schema patterns in gay men, exploring how minority stress and developmental experiences interact with schema formation.
clinical_insight: Gay men often carry early maladaptive schemas shaped by minority stress experiences such as rejection, concealment, and internalized homophobia, which schema therapists should explicitly assess and link to developmental context. Treatment should integrate schema-mode work with affirming approaches that validate these experiences as responses to systemic oppression rather than individual pathology, targeting schemas like defectiveness/shame, social isolation, and emotional deprivation.
relevant_for: Schema therapists and LGBTQ+-affirming clinicians working with sexual minority men

title: CBT-I mechanisms in depression treatment
authors: Unknown
summary: Examines the mechanisms by which cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) improves depression outcomes, testing whether sleep improvement mediates mood change.
clinical_insight: CBT-I improves depression largely through direct sleep improvement rather than nonspecific therapy effects, supporting the use of CBT-I as a first-line intervention for comorbid insomnia and depression rather than waiting for mood symptoms to remit first. Therapists treating depressed patients with sleep complaints should prioritize sleep restriction, stimulus control, and cognitive restructuring of sleep-related beliefs early in treatment, as sleep gains appear to catalyze broader mood improvement.
relevant_for: CBT therapists and sleep medicine clinicians treating comorbid insomnia and depression

title: Group CBT dropout: predictors and prevention
authors: Unknown
summary: Identifies predictors of dropout from group-based CBT programs and proposes strategies for improving retention in group therapy settings.
clinical_insight: Therapists running group CBT can proactively screen for identified dropout risk factors at intake and early sessions, then implement targeted retention strategies such as orientation sessions, group cohesion exercises, and early check-ins with at-risk members. Monitoring engagement patterns session-by-session allows timely intervention before disengagement solidifies.
relevant_for: Group CBT facilitators in community mental health and outpatient clinics

title: CBM-I for social anxiety: randomized trial
authors: Unknown
summary: Randomized controlled trial of cognitive bias modification for interpretation in social anxiety disorder, testing its efficacy as a standalone and adjunctive intervention.
clinical_insight: CBM-I offers a low-burden digital adjunct that therapists can assign as homework to help social anxiety patients practice interpreting ambiguous social scenarios in a non-threatening way. It can be used as a stepping-stone for patients too avoidant to begin exposure therapy, or layered alongside standard CBT to accelerate threat-bias reduction.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners treating social anxiety disorder in private and outpatient settings

title: Childhood maltreatment and OXTR gene
authors: Unknown
summary: Examines the interaction between childhood maltreatment and oxytocin receptor gene (OXTR) variants in predicting adult psychopathology.
clinical_insight: While OXTR genotyping is not yet ready for routine clinical use, these findings reinforce that patients with maltreatment histories and certain oxytocin receptor profiles may be at heightened risk for relational and affective psychopathology. Therapists should prioritize trauma-informed relational approaches and may pay particular attention to attachment difficulties in this subgroup.
relevant_for: Trauma-focused psychologists and clinical researchers in developmental psychopathology

title: Disinhibition and anankastia (additional)
authors: Unknown
summary: Further examination of the relationship between behavioral disinhibition and anankastic traits across clinical and non-clinical populations.
clinical_insight: Understanding the co-occurrence of disinhibition and anankastic (obsessive-compulsive) traits helps clinicians recognize a clinically challenging presentation where impulsivity and rigidity coexist. Treatment planning should address both poles — using behavioral strategies for disinhibition while respecting the patient's need for structure to avoid therapeutic resistance.
relevant_for: Personality disorder specialists and clinicians in forensic or specialist personality services

title: FND and dissociation: ecological momentary assessment
authors: Amy Crellin, L.S. Merritt Millman, Matthew Hotopf, Susannah Pick
summary: Uses ecological momentary assessment to examine the temporal relationship between dissociative experiences and functional neurological (conversion) disorder symptoms in daily life, testing whether dissociation precedes, follows, or co-occurs with FND symptom fluctuations and identifying contextual triggers for both phenomena.
clinical_insight: Uses real-time EMA data to establish that dissociative experiences and functional neurological (conversion) symptoms fluctuate together in daily life, suggesting dissociation may be a proximal trigger for symptom exacerbation. Clinicians can teach clients with functional neurological disorder to recognize early dissociative states and use grounding or orienting techniques as a preemptive strategy, potentially reducing the frequency or severity of functional symptoms.
relevant_for: Clinical neuropsychologists and therapists treating functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder)

title: Limits of CBT evidence for eating disorders
authors: Laurence Cobbaert
summary: Critically examines the evidence base for cognitive behavioral therapy in eating disorders, identifying methodological limitations in existing trials, diagnostic heterogeneity that obscures treatment effects, and specific populations and symptom presentations where CBT evidence remains insufficient or absent.
clinical_insight: Critically examines the limits of CBT's evidence base in eating disorders, identifying specific subgroups—such as those with severe personality comorbidity, long illness duration, or anorexia nervosa—where CBT outcomes are less robust. Clinicians should consider alternative or adjunctive approaches such as DBT, psychodynamic therapy, or specialist eating disorder models when clients with eating disorders do not respond to standard CBT-ED, rather than persisting with an approach that may not be the optimal fit.
relevant_for: Eating disorder treatment teams and clinical supervisors guiding evidence-based ED practice

title: Paranoia and social hallucinations
authors: Krisztina Jedlovszky, Santiago Castiello de Obeso, Praveen Suthaharan, Daniel Yon, Philip R. Corlett
summary: Investigates the relationship between paranoid ideation and hallucinatory experiences in social contexts using experimental and EMA methods, testing social models of paranoia that propose hallucinations and paranoid beliefs share common social-cognitive mechanisms triggered by interpersonal threat cues.
clinical_insight: Demonstrates that paranoid ideation and hallucinatory experiences are closely linked in social contexts, suggesting that social threat perception may drive both symptom domains simultaneously. Therapists can address both paranoia and social hallucinations through integrated social cognition interventions—such as social skills training, virtual social exposure, or metacognitive reflection—rather than treating these as separate symptom clusters, potentially improving outcomes for clients with comorbid psychotic features.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists in early psychosis programs and community mental health teams treating schizophrenia-spectrum disorders

title: Chronic pain coping model
authors: Pierrick Dodeur-Delangre, Lucile Dellerie, Louise His, Camille Perrignon, Florence Cousson-Gélie, Christophe Adam, Guillaume Broc
summary: Develops and tests a chronic pain coping model integrating cognitive, affective, and behavioral pathways, examining how pain catastrophizing, acceptance, and social support interact to predict pain-related disability and psychological distress across different chronic pain conditions.
clinical_insight: Presents a comprehensive coping model for chronic pain that distinguishes adaptive pathways (e.g., acceptance, paced activity) from maladaptive ones (e.g., catastrophizing, avoidance), providing a clear map for intervention targeting. Clinicians can use this model to assess which coping pathway a client is on, then selectively reinforce adaptive strategies while restructuring maladaptive ones within a CBT or ACT framework for pain management.
relevant_for: Pain management psychologists and occupational therapists in chronic pain rehabilitation programs

title: TVI practice model for trauma therapy
authors: Sally Marsden, Cathy Humphreys, Kelsey Hegarty
summary: Introduces a trauma-informed practice model (Trauma and Violence Informed, or TVI) for structuring trauma therapy sessions, providing a framework for organizing session content, managing pacing and containment, and balancing trauma processing with stabilization across different phases of trauma treatment.
clinical_insight: Introduces the TVI (Trauma-informed) practice model as a structured framework for organizing trauma therapy sessions, ensuring safety, pacing, and stabilization are systematically addressed before and during trauma processing. Clinicians can adopt this model to improve session planning and reduce the risk of client destabilization during trauma-focused work, providing a clear roadmap for when to use grounding, resource-building, and processing techniques within and across sessions.
relevant_for: Trauma-focused therapists and EMDR practitioners seeking structured session-planning frameworks

title: EEG neurofeedback for PTSD: RCT
authors: Jonathan Lieberman, Brendan Brady, Ruth Lanius, Tomas Ros, Jean Theberge, Benicio Frey, Venkat Bhat, Frank Scharnowski, Andrew A. Nicholson
summary: Conducts a randomized controlled trial examining the efficacy of EEG neurofeedback protocols for reducing PTSD symptoms, comparing neurofeedback conditions to active controls and measuring effects on re-experiencing, hyperarousal, avoidance, and associated neurophysiological markers of autonomic dysregulation.
clinical_insight: EEG neurofeedback offers a non-pharmacological avenue for PTSD patients who have not responded to first-line trauma therapies by directly training neural regulation patterns. Clinicians could consider it as an adjunctive treatment, particularly for patients with prominent hyperarousal or dissociative symptoms where traditional exposure-based approaches may be poorly tolerated.
relevant_for: Trauma-focused therapists and neuropsychologists working with treatment-resistant PTSD patients

title: Passive vs. active suicidal ideation: network analysis
authors: Lara Kirchhofer, Ai Ye, Isaac Galatzer-Levy, Urte Scholz, Birgit Kleim, Stephanie Homan
summary: Uses network analysis to differentiate between passive and active suicidal ideation, identifying distinct network structures and clinical implications.
clinical_insight: Distinguishing between passive and active suicidal ideation through network structures helps clinicians identify which bridging symptoms signal escalation from passive wishes to active planning. This allows for more targeted risk monitoring and intervention at the specific network nodes that drive transitions to higher-risk states.
relevant_for: Suicide risk assessors and crisis intervention clinicians in emergency and inpatient settings

title: BPD symptoms and sexual orientation: DIF analysis
authors: Alexandra K. Barnhill, Julia Nicholas, Hannah Croom, Troy Hubert, Sarah Cecil, Caden Maynard, Vincent Buchenberger, Reagan A. Franks, Megan J. Hutchins, Matthew W. Southward, et al.
summary: Uses differential item functioning analysis to examine whether Borderline Personality Disorder symptom measures function equivalently across sexual orientations, investigating potential measurement bias that could lead to inaccurate BPD diagnoses or severity estimates in LGBTQ+ populations.
clinical_insight: Standard BPD measures may function differently across sexual orientations, potentially leading to over- or under-diagnosis of BPD in LGBTQ+ individuals. Clinicians should interpret BPD assessment results with caution for sexual minority clients, supplementing standardized measures with culturally sensitive clinical interviews that distinguish identity-related distress from personality pathology.
relevant_for: Assessors and diagnosticians evaluating BPD in LGBTQ+ populations

title: ISTDP mechanisms in treatment-resistant depression
authors: Ziad Nahas, Robert Hermosillo, Spencer H. Eiting, Tariq Hattab, Maya Hazimeh, Rachel A. Johnson, Thomas Madison, Kimberly B. Weldon, Seth Koenig, Cash Differding, et al.
summary: Examines the mechanisms of intensive short-term dynamic psychotherapy (ISTDP) in treatment-resistant depression, identifying active therapeutic ingredients.
clinical_insight: ISTDP appears effective for treatment-resistant depression through mechanisms involving the unlocking of blocked emotions and resolution of unconscious anxiety pathways. Therapists working with chronically depressed patients who have failed CBT or medication may find ISTDP's focus on somatic experiencing of blocked emotions and defense restructuring to be a viable alternative approach.
relevant_for: Psychodynamic therapists treating chronic and treatment-resistant depression

title: ACEs, stress sensitivity, and suicidal ideation: EMA
authors: Ana Portillo-Van Diest, Gemma Vilagut, Laura Ballester Coma, Johan Bjureberg, Paula Carrasco, Raquel Falcó, Margalida Gili, Mathilde Husky, Glenn Kiekens, Francisco H. Machancoses, et al.
summary: Applies ecological momentary assessment to examine how adverse childhood experiences contribute to stress sensitivity and dynamic fluctuations in suicidal ideation during daily life, testing whether ACEs amplify the momentary association between stress exposure and suicidal thoughts in at-risk young adults.
clinical_insight: EMA findings confirm that individuals with high ACE scores experience amplified stress-reactivity in daily life that directly increases momentary suicidal ideation, suggesting a stress-sensitization pathway. Clinicians should prioritize real-time stress management and grounding skills in trauma-informed work, and consider EMA-based monitoring to identify acute risk windows in patients with significant adverse childhood histories.
relevant_for: Trauma-informed therapists working with clients who have complex trauma histories

title: Bipolar self-stigma: RCT of intervention
authors: Nan Zhao, Andrea B Vassilev, David Vogel, Emma Morton, Allison Loomis, Sterling Hubbard, Chantel Wormington
summary: Randomized controlled trial testing a targeted intervention to reduce self-stigma in bipolar disorder, evaluating whether psychoeducation and cognitive restructuring focused on internalized stigma can improve treatment adherence, quality of life, and mood outcomes beyond standard pharmacotherapy and psychoeducation alone.
clinical_insight: Targeted interventions that reduce self-stigma in bipolar disorder can improve treatment adherence, self-efficacy, and quality of life independent of mood symptom change. Clinicians should integrate psychoeducation and cognitive restructuring of internalized stigma into standard bipolar treatment, addressing beliefs like 'I am dangerous' or 'my diagnosis defines me' that undermine engagement with mood stabilization strategies.
relevant_for: Psychiatrists and psychotherapists treating bipolar disorder in outpatient settings

title: CBT and interpersonal problems: causal effect
authors: Jonas Hallgans, Stephan Bartholdy, Tim Kaiser, Marit Kristine List, Eva-Lotta Brakemeier
summary: Uses causal inference methods and longitudinal data to examine whether successful CBT for depression produces causal reductions in interpersonal problems, or whether pre-existing interpersonal difficulties moderate depression treatment response, with implications for integrating interpersonal approaches into depression treatment.
clinical_insight: CBT exerts a direct causal effect on reducing interpersonal difficulties, suggesting that cognitive restructuring and behavioral activation may improve relational functioning beyond symptom remission. Therapists can leverage this by explicitly linking cognitive interventions to interpersonal goals, helping clients recognize how maladaptive thinking patterns perpetuate relationship conflicts and social withdrawal.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners treating clients with comorbid depression and interpersonal dysfunction

title: Rumination facets and PTSD: DAG analysis
authors: Miguel A. Segura-Vargas, Kristof Hoorelbeke, Michelle Kelley, Adrian Bravo
summary: Applies directed acyclic graph (DAG) analysis to differentiate the roles of brooding, reflection, and other rumination facets in PTSD symptom maintenance and recovery, identifying which rumination components have direct causal effects on specific PTSD symptom clusters versus mediating through other pathways.
clinical_insight: Different facets of rumination—such as brooding versus reflective pondering—play distinct causal roles in maintaining PTSD symptoms, with brooding acting as a stronger driver of the re-experiencing and hyperarousal clusters. Clinicians should assess rumination subtype specifically in PTSD treatment and prioritize interventions that disrupt brooding (e.g., mindfulness-based attentional retraining) rather than assuming all rumination is equally pathological.
relevant_for: PTSD therapists using cognitive processing or trauma-focused CBT

title: Obsessional jealousy: diagnostic criteria review
authors: David Mataix-Cols, Josef Isung, Anna Norberg, Sigrid Elfström, Johanna Linde, Johan Bjureberg, Long-Long Chen, Johan Ahlen
summary: Systematically reviews the diagnostic criteria for obsessional jealousy (morbid jealousy, Othello syndrome) across current classification systems, examining its phenomenological overlap with OCD, delusional disorder, and attachment pathology to argue for clearer diagnostic classification and assessment approaches.
clinical_insight: Obsessional jealousy often presents with intrusive, ego-dystonic thoughts about infidelity that can be misdiagnosed as delusional jealousy or BPD-related interpersonal pathology. Clinicians should assess for OCD-spectrum features when patients present with relationship-focused intrusive thoughts, as these may respond better to ERP techniques than to interventions aimed at delusional disorders or personality pathology.
relevant_for: OCD specialists and couples therapists assessing pathological jealousy

title: Meta-emotional beliefs: transdiagnostic perspective
authors: Laura Vuillier, Rachel Moseley
summary: Examines meta-emotional beliefs—beliefs about one's own emotions—as a transdiagnostic construct across anxiety, depression, and emotional disorders, testing whether maladaptive meta-emotional schemas predict symptom severity and treatment response beyond primary emotion regulation difficulties.
clinical_insight: Negative beliefs about one's own emotions (e.g., 'my sadness is dangerous' or 'I must control my anger') cut across mood and anxiety disorders and maintain emotional avoidance cycles. Clinicians working transdiagnostically should assess and directly target meta-emotional beliefs using techniques from metacognitive therapy and schema-based approaches, as these beliefs may be more central to maintaining distress than the primary emotions themselves.
relevant_for: Transdiagnostic CBT therapists and metacognitive therapy practitioners

title: Digital biomarkers for suicidality: scoping review
authors: Justus Theiling, Julius Hardt, Jurrijn Koelen, Jannis Kraiss
summary: Conducts a systematic scoping review of digital biomarkers for suicidality, cataloguing smartphone-derived behavioral indicators including activity patterns, communication changes, GPS mobility, and linguistic markers that show promise for real-time suicide risk detection and clinical decision support.
clinical_insight: Digital biomarkers from smartphones, wearables, and social media can detect suicidal risk shifts before they are reported in clinical sessions, offering a passive early-warning system. Clinicians should consider incorporating digital phenotyping tools into their practice where available, using data on sleep disruption, social withdrawal, and behavioral patterns to inform between-session risk monitoring and proactive outreach.
relevant_for: Clinicians in suicide prevention programs and digital mental health researchers

title: Smartphone-based interpretation bias modification: RCT
authors: Alexandra Lee Silverman, Gabi Kovarsky Rotta, Doah Shin, Irelee Ferguson, Courtney Beard
summary: Randomized controlled trial testing a smartphone-delivered interpretation bias modification program for anxiety, comparing app-based training to waitlist and active control conditions on interpretation bias, anxiety symptoms, and real-world stress reactivity in a clinically anxious sample.
clinical_insight: Smartphone-based interpretation bias modification allows therapists to extend therapeutic work between sessions through brief, accessible cognitive exercises that patients complete on their own devices. Clinicians can prescribe these apps as homework to actively reshape threat-related interpretation biases in generalized or social anxiety, complementing in-session CBT. The digital delivery format reduces barriers for clients who cannot attend frequent sessions while maintaining an evidence-based cognitive mechanism of change.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners treating generalized and social anxiety disorders

title: Narrative therapy for moral injury: case study
authors: J. Irene Harris, Susannah Robb Kondrath, Adam McGuire, Brandon Griffin, Rochelle Binion, Elizabeth Brandt, Timothy Usset, Katherine A. Kosman, Kristin B. Golden, Jeffrey Pyne
summary: Presents a detailed case study applying narrative therapy approaches to moral injury in a clinical context, demonstrating how externalization, re-authoring, and outcome questioning techniques can help clients reconstruct coherent identity narratives following morally distressing experiences.
clinical_insight: Narrative therapy's re-authoring approach offers clinicians a non-pathologizing method for moral injury that helps patients reconstruct a coherent, morally integrated life narrative. Therapists can guide clients through externalizing the morally injurious event, identifying preferred values, and re-storying their experience in ways that incorporate both self-compassion and accountability. This is particularly valuable when traditional trauma processing alone leaves patients stuck in shame and self-condemnation.
relevant_for: Military and veteran mental health therapists treating moral injury

title: BPD etiology: integrative review
authors: Valentin Radoi, Mark L. Ruffalo
summary: Integrative review examining biological, psychological, and social etiological factors in borderline personality disorder, synthesizing evidence for genetic vulnerability, childhood maltreatment, attachment disruption, and transactional models to propose a unified developmental framework for BPD pathogenesis.
clinical_insight: The integrative BPD etiology review helps therapists move beyond unidimensional models and present a biopsychosocial formulation to patients that normalizes their experience as understandable rather than characterological. Clinicians can use this synthesis to explain to patients why they react intensely—linking biological sensitivity, attachment disruption, and invalidating environments into a coherent narrative. This facilitates engagement in evidence-based treatments like DBT by providing a rationale that reduces shame and increases treatment motivation.
relevant_for: DBT therapists and clinicians treating borderline personality disorder

title: Cognitive restraint vs. restriction in eating disorders: EMA
authors: Vittoria Trolio, Elizabeth Hales, Ege Biçaker, Alexia Miller, Sarah Elizabeth Racine
summary: Uses ecological momentary assessment to differentiate cognitive restraint (deliberate dietary restriction) from dietary restriction (actual food limitation) in eating disorders, examining how each relates to momentary affect, binge eating episodes, and purging behaviors in daily life.
clinical_insight: Differentiating cognitive restraint (thoughts about controlling eating) from actual dietary restriction helps eating disorder therapists identify which cognitive processes most strongly predict symptom maintenance. Clinicians can use EMA-informed assessments to determine whether a patient's primary driver is rigid dietary rules or preoccupation with food, and then target the more pathological process in treatment. This nuance supports moving beyond calorie-counting metrics to address the cognitive dimensions that sustain disordered eating patterns.
relevant_for: Eating disorder therapists and CBT-E practitioners

title: CPTSD-BPD and structural stigma
authors: Unknown
summary: Examines how structural stigma affects individuals with comorbid CPTSD and BPD, identifying systemic barriers to care.
clinical_insight: Individuals with comorbid CPTSD and BPD face compounded stigma from both diagnostic labels and structural barriers in healthcare systems, leading to fragmented care and treatment avoidance. Clinicians must actively counter institutional stigma by validating patients' experiences, advocating for integrated trauma-personality treatment pathways, and examining their own diagnostic biases.
relevant_for: Clinicians in community mental health and personality disorder treatment programs

title: Culturally adapted DBT for SSI
authors: Unknown
summary: Describes the cultural adaptation of dialectical behavior therapy for reducing nonsuicidal self-injury in specific cultural contexts.
clinical_insight: Adapting DBT skills training to align with cultural values and communication styles significantly improves engagement and reduces nonsuicidal self-injury in diverse populations. Therapists should incorporate cultural formulations into DBT skills coaching, ensuring that examples, metaphors, and behavioral chain analyses resonate with the client's cultural context.
relevant_for: DBT therapists working with culturally diverse and immigrant populations

title: Suicide attempt networks: IMV model
authors: Unknown
summary: Applies the influential machine vision (IMV) model to analyze suicide attempt networks, identifying risk pathways.
clinical_insight: The IMV model applied to suicide attempt networks clarifies the distinct pathways from ideation through to action, identifying key intervention points where clinicians can disrupt the chain. Therapists can use this framework to map individual patients' risk pathways and intervene specifically at the volitional modulation stage—addressing acquired capability, access to means, and mental rehearsal of attempts.
relevant_for: Suicide prevention clinicians conducting structured risk formulation

title: NSSI dynamic network analysis: case studies
authors: Unknown
summary: Uses dynamic network analysis to examine NSSI behaviors in individual case studies, revealing person-specific symptom networks.
clinical_insight: Idiographic network analysis reveals that NSSI triggers and maintaining factors are highly individualized, challenging one-size-fits-all formulations. Therapists can use repeated symptom monitoring to map each client's unique network and target the most central, changeable nodes—such as specific emotional precursors or interpersonal triggers—rather than applying generic relapse prevention plans.
relevant_for: Therapists providing individualized treatment for recurrent self-injury

title: Sharing cognitive reframes boosts reappraisal
authors: Unknown
summary: Investigates whether sharing cognitive reframes with others enhances the effectiveness of cognitive reappraisal as an emotion regulation strategy.
clinical_insight: Verbalizing and sharing cognitive reframes with others appears to strengthen the effectiveness of cognitive reappraisal, suggesting that the social process of articulation enhances cognitive restructuring. Therapists should encourage clients to discuss their reframes with trusted others, use between-session homework that involves sharing alternative perspectives, and consider group therapy formats that leverage peer modeling of cognitive flexibility.
relevant_for: CBT therapists facilitating individual or group cognitive restructuring

title: Incels and psychotherapy: clinical considerations
authors: Unknown
summary: Examines the psychological profile and therapeutic needs of incel-identified individuals, discussing approaches for engaging this population in treatment.
clinical_insight: Incel-identified clients often present with a complex blend of depression, social anxiety, shame, entitlement, and grievance narratives that can challenge standard therapeutic engagement. Clinicians should approach these clients with genuine curiosity about their subjective experience while maintaining clear boundaries around harmful ideology, focusing on underlying emotional needs for connection and belonging rather than confronting beliefs directly in early treatment.
relevant_for: Psychotherapists working with young men experiencing social isolation and identity distress

title: Pre-treatment depression dynamics and outcome
authors: Unknown
summary: Analyzes how depression symptom dynamics in the pre-treatment period predict treatment response and dropout.
clinical_insight: The trajectory of depression symptoms during the pre-treatment waiting period strongly predicts subsequent treatment response and dropout risk, suggesting that symptom volatility itself is a clinically meaningful indicator. Clinicians should consider providing brief stabilization interventions or monitoring during waitlist periods, as patients with rapidly fluctuating symptoms may need additional preparation or a modified treatment approach to prevent early dropout.
relevant_for: Clinic directors and intake therapists managing treatment waitlists

title: Digital ACT intervention for postsurgical pain
authors: Unknown
summary: Tests a digital acceptance and commitment therapy intervention for managing pain after surgery.
clinical_insight: Delivering ACT principles digitally for postsurgical pain management improves pain acceptance and psychological flexibility, leading to better pain outcomes without requiring in-person therapy sessions. Surgeons and pain management teams should consider prescribing digital ACT interventions as a standard adjunct to postsurgical care, particularly for patients at risk of chronic pain development or opioid overuse.
relevant_for: Pain psychologists and perioperative clinical teams

title: Personality pathology limits ADHD pharmacotherapy
authors: Unknown
summary: Examines how comorbid personality pathology moderates response to ADHD pharmacotherapy in adults.
clinical_insight: Comorbid personality pathology significantly attenuates the therapeutic response to ADHD medication in adults, suggesting that pharmacotherapy alone is insufficient when personality features are present. Prescribing clinicians should assess for personality disorder features before or during ADHD medication trials and integrate psychotherapy—particularly targeted interventions for emotional dysregulation and interpersonal dysfunction—alongside pharmacological treatment for optimal outcomes.
relevant_for: Psychiatrists and psychiatric nurse practitioners treating adult ADHD

title: Conflict Square Algorithm for therapy
authors: Unknown
summary: Introduces the Conflict Square Algorithm, a structured tool for analyzing and resolving therapeutic impasses and alliance ruptures.
clinical_insight: The Conflict Square Algorithm provides a structured framework for identifying and resolving therapeutic impasses by mapping competing needs of both therapist and client across four domains (autonomy, connection, competence, and security). Therapists can use this tool when alliance ruptures occur to systematically diagnose the source of the impasse, select the appropriate repair strategy, and prevent premature termination.
relevant_for: Psychotherapists of all orientations experiencing alliance ruptures or therapeutic impasses

title: Suicidality in refugees
authors: Unknown
summary: Reviews suicide risk factors and prevention approaches among refugee populations, examining the intersection of trauma, displacement, and cultural factors.
clinical_insight: Suicide risk in refugee populations is shaped by a unique intersection of pre-migration trauma, displacement stressors, cultural bereavement, and barriers to mental health access including stigma and language. Clinicians should employ culturally adapted suicide risk assessments, work with interpreters trained in mental health, and address practical stressors alongside psychological treatment, recognizing that Western conceptualizations of suicidality may not fully capture risk in this population.
relevant_for: Clinicians and case managers working in refugee resettlement and cross-cultural mental health services

title: Pain-intrusions in PTSD
authors: Unknown
summary: Examines the phenomenon of pain intrusions in PTSD, investigating shared mechanisms between physical pain and traumatic memories.
clinical_insight: Pain intrusions in PTSD—where physical pain sensations trigger trauma memories and vice versa—suggest overlapping neurobiological mechanisms that maintain both conditions in a reinforcing cycle. Clinicians treating comorbid chronic pain and PTSD should integrate exposure-based approaches that simultaneously address pain-related fear and trauma re-experiencing, using interoceptive exposure techniques that help patients differentiate pain signals from trauma responses.
relevant_for: Health psychologists and trauma therapists treating comorbid PTSD and chronic pain

title: Fear-antagonistic actions in acrophobia: RCT
authors: Unknown
summary: Randomized controlled trial testing fear-antagonistic action strategies (acting opposite to fear-driven impulses) in exposure therapy for acrophobia.
clinical_insight: Fear-antagonistic actions—deliberately acting opposite to fear-driven impulses during exposure—offer therapists a concrete behavioral strategy to enhance extinction learning beyond standard graded exposure. Clinicians can coach acrophobia patients to lean toward edges, look down voluntarily, or reach outward, transforming passive endurance into active disconfirmation of threat beliefs. This approach may accelerate habituation and reduce dropout by giving patients a sense of agency during the most distressing moments of exposure.
relevant_for: Behavioral therapists and exposure therapists treating specific phobias

title: Somatic yoga for FND: feasibility RCT
authors: Unknown
summary: Feasibility randomized controlled trial of trauma-sensitive somatic yoga for functional neurological disorder.
clinical_insight: Trauma-sensitive somatic yoga provides a body-first therapeutic entry point for patients with functional neurological disorder whose symptoms may be worsened by traditional talk therapy. Clinicians can integrate gentle, interoceptive-awareness practices to help patients reconnect with bodily signals without triggering dissociation or symptom escalation. This feasibility evidence supports offering somatic yoga as an adjunct or alternative early in treatment when patients are ambivalent about psychological framing of their symptoms.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and rehabilitation therapists working with functional neurological disorder

title: Prolonged grief: cross-cultural network study (China/US)
authors: Unknown
summary: Cross-cultural network analysis comparing prolonged grief symptom structure between Chinese and US samples.
clinical_insight: Cross-cultural network findings reveal that prolonged grief may present through different symptom pathways in Chinese versus US populations, suggesting clinicians should not assume universal symptom centrality. Therapists working with culturally diverse bereaved clients should assess for culturally salient grief expressions—such as yearning versus role disruption—rather than applying a one-size-fits-all diagnostic lens. This supports culturally adapted grief counseling that prioritizes the most network-central symptoms for each cultural group.
relevant_for: Grief counselors and bereavement therapists in multicultural settings

title: Transdiagnostic treatments with somatic comorbidity: meta-analysis
authors: Unknown
summary: Meta-analysis examining the efficacy of transdiagnostic treatments when somatic comorbidities are present.
clinical_insight: Transdiagnostic treatments appear effective even when somatic comorbidities are present, meaning therapists do not need to defer CBT or ACT until medical conditions are resolved. Clinicians should proceed with evidence-based protocols while monitoring how physical symptoms interact with psychological change, and may need to adapt pacing or psychoeducation accordingly. This meta-analysis reassures practitioners that treating the psychological dimension actively benefits patients regardless of co-occurring medical burden.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT practitioners in integrated primary care settings

title: Depression trajectories from adolescence to adulthood: network analysis
authors: Unknown
summary: Uses network analysis to trace depression symptom trajectories from adolescence into adulthood, identifying stable and changing network features.
clinical_insight: Network analysis identifying which depressive symptoms remain stable versus shift from adolescence to adulthood helps therapists prioritize intervention targets with the longest developmental impact. Clinicians working with depressed teens should focus on bridge symptoms—such as anhedonia or self-worth—that most strongly predict chronicity into adulthood, rather than treating all symptoms equally. This developmental lens supports early, targeted intervention that may alter the long-term depressive trajectory.
relevant_for: Adolescent and child psychiatrists treating early-onset depression

title: Anxious symptom patterns: COVID-19 3-year longitudinal
authors: Unknown
summary: Three-year longitudinal study of anxiety symptom patterns across the COVID-19 pandemic, identifying risk and resilience trajectories.
clinical_insight: Three-year anxiety trajectories during COVID-19 reveal distinct resilience and chronicity patterns, enabling therapists to identify which patients are on a deteriorating versus recovering course. Clinicians can use trajectory-informed screening to allocate stepped-care resources—intensive therapy for chronic clusters and psychoeducation for resilient ones. Understanding that pandemic-related anxiety followed predictable patterns also normalizes prolonged distress for some clients, reducing secondary self-blame.
relevant_for: Community mental health clinicians treating post-pandemic anxiety disorders

title: Late-diagnosed autistic women: interpretive phenomenological analysis
authors: Unknown
summary: IPA study exploring the lived experiences of women diagnosed with autism in adulthood, examining the impact of late diagnosis on identity and wellbeing.
clinical_insight: Late-diagnosed autistic women often carry years of misdiagnoses and invalidation, so clinicians should approach adult autism assessment with sensitivity to masking histories and complex identity reintegration. Therapists can support post-diagnosis adjustment by validating the grief, relief, and identity reconstruction that follow a late autism diagnosis, rather than treating it as purely informational. This research underscores the need for gender-sensitive diagnostic pathways and post-diagnostic counseling that addresses decades of camouflaging.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists conducting adult autism assessments and neurodivergence-affirming therapists

title: OCD decision-making styles
authors: Unknown
summary: Examines decision-making styles in obsessive-compulsive disorder, linking decisional patterns to symptom severity and treatment response.
clinical_insight: Identifying specific decision-making styles in OCD—such as excessive information-seeking or intolerant-of-uncertainty patterns—allows therapists to tailor ERP and cognitive interventions to the patient's core decisional impairment. Clinicians can use decision-making assessments to predict treatment resistance and proactively address the checking, reassurance-seeking, or avoidance that maintains compulsions. This supports moving beyond symptom counts to target the underlying decisional architecture that drives OCD behaviors.
relevant_for: OCD specialists and ERP therapists

title: PTSD and chronic pain: network with pain-intrusions
authors: Unknown
summary: Network analysis examining the interplay between PTSD symptoms and chronic pain, with focus on pain-intrusion phenomena.
clinical_insight: The pain-intrusion network between PTSD and chronic pain suggests that pain sensations can trigger trauma-related intrusive memories and vice versa, creating a reinforcing cycle. Therapists should assess for both conditions simultaneously and address pain-intrusion phenomena directly—using grounding techniques when pain triggers flashbacks, and pain management strategies when trauma processing exacerbates physical symptoms. Integrated treatment plans that disrupt this bidirectional loop are likely more effective than treating each condition in isolation.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists working in chronic pain and interdisciplinary rehabilitation clinics

title: Interpersonal variability in personality functioning
authors: Unknown
summary: Examines how personality functioning varies across different interpersonal contexts and relationships.
clinical_insight: Personality functioning that shifts dramatically across interpersonal contexts suggests clinicians should assess patients across multiple relationships rather than relying on a single clinical interview snapshot. Therapists can use this variability as both a diagnostic indicator and a treatment target, helping patients develop more stable self-functioning regardless of relational context. This is particularly useful for case formulation in personality disorder treatment, where contextual triggers for decompensation can be mapped and anticipated.
relevant_for: Personality disorder specialists and psychodynamic therapists

title: Autism-psychosis association: meta-analysis
authors: Unknown
summary: Meta-analysis examining the association between autism spectrum conditions and psychotic experiences/disorders.
clinical_insight: The autism-psychosis association means that clinicians working with autistic individuals should maintain a high index of suspicion for psychotic experiences and vice versa, especially when patients present with atypical or treatment-resistant features. Therapists should adapt assessment tools to account for overlapping social-cognitive difficulties—distinguishing autistic literalness from psychotic thought disorder requires careful differential diagnosis. Treatment planning should address the compounded social and cognitive challenges that arise when both conditions co-occur.
relevant_for: Psychosis clinicians and clinical psychologists in neurodevelopmental assessment services

title: CBT bridging elements for relapse prevention
authors: Unknown
summary: Develops CBT bridging elements designed to prevent relapse after acute depression treatment, maintaining treatment gains.
clinical_insight: CBT bridging elements—structured transition activities that maintain treatment gains after acute-phase therapy—provide a practical framework for therapists to reduce relapse rates in depression. Clinicians can incorporate relapse-prevention modules that identify early warning signs, practice adaptive responses, and create personalized bridging plans during the final sessions of acute treatment. This approach extends the therapist's influence beyond the formal therapy window, addressing the well-documented gap between acute response and sustained recovery.
relevant_for: CBT therapists treating recurrent major depressive disorder

title: Vulnerable and grandiose narcissism: shame and mentalizing
authors: Unknown
summary: Compares vulnerable and grandiose narcissism in relation to shame and mentalizing capacity, with implications for therapeutic engagement.
clinical_insight: Distinguishing shame-driven vulnerable narcissism from mentalizing-deficient grandiose narcissism enables therapists to select the correct therapeutic stance—empathic exploration for the former and mentalization-based interventions for the latter. Clinicians should assess which narcissistic presentation predominates, as vulnerable narcissists may initially appear depressed or anxious while grandiose narcissists often present with interpersonal conflict. This differentiation prevents misapplication of empathic approaches that may be experienced as threatening by grandiose patients or therapeutic techniques that feel dismissive to vulnerable ones.
relevant_for: Psychodynamic and mentalization-based therapists treating narcissistic personality presentations

title: Waiting time and clinical implications using ROM data
authors: Unknown
summary: Uses routine outcome monitoring data to examine the clinical implications of waiting time for psychotherapy.
clinical_insight: Routine outcome monitoring data showing the impact of waiting time on symptom deterioration provides clinicians and service administrators with evidence to advocate for reduced waitlists or stepped interim support. Therapists should be aware that patients often worsen while waiting, so providing even minimal contact—such as psychoeducation or self-help resources during the wait period—may preserve baseline functioning. This data also supports using ROM data proactively to identify at-risk patients whose symptoms are escalating before their first session.
relevant_for: Clinic directors and intake coordinators in publicly funded mental health services

title: Negative affect mediating restriction-binge eating: EMA
authors: Unknown
summary: EMA study testing whether negative affect mediates the relationship between dietary restriction and binge eating episodes.
clinical_insight: Confirming negative affect as the mediator between restriction and binge eating gives therapists a clear mechanism to target—emotion regulation skills should be prioritized alongside nutritional rehabilitation. Clinicians can teach patients to recognize the negative emotional shift that precedes binge episodes and intervene with distress tolerance or affect regulation strategies before the binge is triggered. This EMA evidence supports integrated approaches like DBT-informed eating disorder treatment that address both behavioral and emotional pathways simultaneously.
relevant_for: Eating disorder specialists and DBT-informed therapists treating binge eating

title: Client-Reported Prevalence of Repressed Memory Recovery in Therapy: A Representative U.K. Sample
authors: Lawrence Patihis
summary: Critically examines the evidence for repressed memory recovery in therapy, addressing the ongoing scientific debate about memory repression and its clinical implications.
clinical_insight: Therapists working with trauma patients should exercise caution with memory recovery techniques, as the evidence base for repressed memory remains contested. Best practice involves using memory-grounding strategies, avoiding suggestive questioning, and focusing on present-day symptom management rather than pursuing recovered memories as a treatment goal.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists and EMDR practitioners

title: Reducing Cognitive Rigidity in Psychotherapy: Effects of an Inspiration Tool on Creative Cognition in Adults With Psychiatric Disorders
authors: Maya Wenzel, Marco Filippi, Anja Vandersmissen, Paula Kattermann, Anja Rauthmann, Charlotte P. Malycha, Jens C. Pruessner, Rainer Krähenmann, Patrick Fissler Plischke
summary: Develops and validates a clinical tool for measuring cognitive rigidity, testing its psychometric properties and clinical utility in a randomized design.
clinical_insight: A validated cognitive rigidity measure enables therapists to systematically track flexibility as a treatment target across conditions like OCD, depression, and personality disorders. Clinicians can administer the tool at intake and at intervals to monitor whether interventions such as behavioral experiments or schema work are producing measurable shifts in cognitive flexibility.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists and neuropsychologists in assessment and therapy roles

title: Disinhibition and Anankastia as Distinct Domains of Maladaptive Control
authors: Janan Mostajabi, Aidan G.C. Wright
summary: Examines the relationship between disinhibition and anankastia (obsessive-compulsive personality traits) and their differential associations with clinical outcomes across disorders.
clinical_insight: The differential impact of disinhibition versus anankastia on clinical outcomes suggests that personality profiling using these dimensions can guide treatment selection and prognostic planning. High disinhibition may predict impulsive self-harm, substance use, and therapy dropout, warranting a focus on distress tolerance and behavioral regulation. High anankastia may predict rigidity, treatment over-engagement without change, and obsessive coping, requiring flexible pacing and attention to therapeutic alliance.
relevant_for: Personality disorder specialists and clinical psychologists

title: Understanding How Autistic People Use Apps to Cope with Suicidality: A Mixed-Methods Study
authors: Holly Hodges, Sarah Marsden, Rachel Moseley, Elizabeth Weir, Lana Bojanić, Amy Orben, Ashley Wilson, Tanya Procyshyn, Tanatswa Chikaura, Carrie Allison, Simon Baron-Cohen, Mirabel Pelton
summary: Reviews and evaluates mobile applications designed to monitor and support autistic individuals experiencing suicidal ideation, assessing their clinical utility and accessibility.
clinical_insight: Mobile apps for suicidality monitoring in autistic populations can bridge the accessibility gap created by difficulties with in-person crisis disclosure and alexithymic presentations. Clinicians should integrate app-based mood and urge tracking into safety plans, enabling real-time data sharing between sessions and more responsive care. Selecting apps with sensory-friendly interfaces, clear visual cues, and optional caregiver alerts is critical for adoption in this population.
relevant_for: Crisis clinicians and autism spectrum disorder specialists

title: Language-Based Detection of Adherence to Evidence-Based Psychotherapy Scripts
authors: Samuel Thomas Campione, Betsy Stade, Stefanie T. LoSavio, Shreya Singhvi, William Xuan, Phi Long Bui, Maria Martin Lopez, Shashanka Subrahmanya, Bailee B. Schuhmann, Courtney B. Worley, Shannon Wiltsey Stirman, johannes C. Eichstaedt, H. Andrew Schwartz
summary: Uses natural language processing to analyze therapy session transcripts and predict patient adherence, with implications for early identification of drop-out risk.
clinical_insight: NLP-based analysis of session transcripts can identify subtle linguistic markers of disengagement and dropout risk—such as reduced emotional word use, increased pronoun distance, or topic avoidance—weeks before a patient actually leaves treatment. Clinicians and clinic managers can use these tools as an early warning system to trigger check-ins, motivational enhancement, or treatment plan adjustments. Integrating automated adherence prediction into electronic health records could become a standard quality improvement tool.
relevant_for: Community mental health clinic directors and psychotherapy researchers

title: The Alliance in Blended Care: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
authors: Laura Bielinski, Jessica Mehlis, Marlene Rosendahl, Thomas Berger, Johanna Boettcher, Christine Knaevelsrud, Carmen Schaeuffele
summary: Meta-analysis examining the therapeutic alliance in blended (online + face-to-face) mental health care, comparing alliance quality and its relationship with outcomes across modalities.
clinical_insight: Clinicians delivering blended care should actively invest in alliance-building during both in-person and digital components, as the meta-analytic findings clarify which digital touchpoints most strengthen the therapeutic bond. Structured video check-ins, personalized feedback on online modules, and consistent therapist presence across platforms are key leverage points.
relevant_for: Telehealth and blended-care mental health providers

title: The role of interoception in dissociation etiology: a network analysis of dissociation, trauma, sleep, and interoception
authors: Marta Łukowska, Ari Nowacki, Weronika Różycka, Eva Schäflein
summary: Uses network analysis to examine the relationship between interoceptive awareness and dissociative experiences, identifying pathways between body awareness and dissociation.
clinical_insight: Network findings suggest that targeting interoceptive awareness through grounded body-based techniques (e.g., body scanning, mindfulness of internal sensations) may reduce dissociative symptoms by disrupting key pathways. Therapists can sequence interventions so that interoceptive grounding is established before processing trauma memories, creating a more stable foundation for exposure-based work.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists and clinicians treating dissociative disorders and functional neurological symptoms

title: Mismatch Between Routine Care and Personalized Selection of Low-Intensity Psychological Interventions Using Causal Machine Learning
authors: Christian A. Webb, Jaime Delgadillo
summary: Applies causal machine learning methods to match patients to optimal depression interventions, demonstrating a precision psychiatry approach to treatment selection.
clinical_insight: As causal machine learning tools for treatment matching mature, clinicians in stepped-care systems can use them to make more informed first-line treatment recommendations for depression rather than relying solely on clinical judgment or simple symptom checklists. This supports moving toward data-driven referral pathways that match patient profiles to CBT, IPT, behavioral activation, or pharmacotherapy.
relevant_for: Clinical directors and care coordinators in large mental health systems

title: Cognitive drivers of feedback-seeking decisions differ between socially fearful and reassurance-seeking individuals
authors: Yukta Thyagaraj, Caroline J Charpentier
summary: Investigates feedback-seeking behaviors in social anxiety disorder, examining how reassurance-seeking patterns maintain social anxiety and negative self-beliefs.
clinical_insight: Therapists should assess for excessive feedback-seeking and reassurance patterns as maintaining factors in social anxiety, and address them through behavioral experiments that test the patient's ability to tolerate uncertainty about others' evaluations. Reducing digital reassurance-seeking (e.g., monitoring social media reactions) should be a specific behavioral target in treatment plans.
relevant_for: CBT therapists specializing in social anxiety disorder

title: Using LLMs to Evaluate the Interplay Between PTSD Symptom Severity and Intolerance to Uncertainty: A Large-scale EMA Study
authors: Elisa Xu, Scott Feltman, Siddharth Mangalik, Whitney R. Ringwald, Roman Kotov, Aprajita Mohanty, H. Andrew Schwartz
summary: Applies large language models to study the relationship between intolerance of uncertainty and PTSD symptomatology, demonstrating novel computational approaches to trauma research.
clinical_insight: Computational modeling with LLMs reveals that intolerance of uncertainty interacts with PTSD symptom clusters in nuanced ways, particularly by amplifying hyperarousal and avoidance through catastrophic uncertainty appraisals about future threat. Therapists treating trauma should directly assess IU and incorporate uncertainty tolerance training alongside trauma-focused work to address this maintaining factor. This approach may be especially important for patients whose PTSD presentations include prominent anticipatory anxiety and safety behaviors.
relevant_for: Trauma-focused therapists and EMDR practitioners

title: More Imagery, Less Vividness? Maladaptive Daydreaming Dissociates Imagery Use from Imagery Vividness
authors: Mohammad Atashrooz, Fatemeh Mirzai, Maede Amin Roaya, Hannaneh Fayyaz Rouhi, Arash Ghadir, Hoda Doosalivand, Reza Khosrowabadi, Amir Sam Kianimoghadam
summary: Investigates the paradoxical relationship between imagery use and vividness in maladaptive daydreaming, finding that individuals with maladaptive daydreaming report high levels of mental imagery engagement but unexpectedly low subjective vividness, suggesting a dissociation between imagery generation and phenomenological experience.
clinical_insight: Clinicians should screen for maladaptive daydreaming in patients presenting with excessive fantasy absorption, procrastination, or difficulty distinguishing internal imagery from external goals. Treatment can draw on behavioral activation and mindfulness to reduce compulsive daydreaming episodes, helping patients reallocate attentional resources toward valued life activities.
relevant_for: Outpatient therapists and psychiatrists assessing patients with excessive fantasy or dissociative-absorptive presentations

title: Clinicians’ Practices and Attitudes Toward Depression Assessment: A Cross-Sectional Survey Study
authors: Damon Navandi, Veerle Eijsbroek, Clara Wiebel, Aaron Marker, Katarina Kjell, Marc Molendijk, Betsy Stade, Oscar Kjell
summary: Surveys clinicians about their depression assessment practices, identifying gaps between evidence-based recommendations and real-world clinical practice.
clinical_insight: The identified gaps between recommended and actual depression assessment practices highlight the need for clinicians to adopt structured, measurement-based screening tools (e.g., PHQ-9) as standard intake and progress-monitoring procedures. Integrating routine symptom tracking into electronic health records and audit workflows can close these evidence-to-practice gaps.
relevant_for: Primary care mental health clinicians and outpatient psychiatric assessors

title: Men’s Experiences and Understandings of the Therapeutic Relationship in Psychotherapy for Depression: A Qualitative Systematic Review
authors: Tsuki Gao, Dr. Lesley-Anne MacRae, Ananyaa Rodricks
summary: Examines how men experience the therapeutic relationship in depression treatment, identifying gender-specific factors that influence engagement and outcomes.
clinical_insight: Therapists treating men for depression should attend to gendered barriers such as reluctance to disclose vulnerability, discomfort with emotional exploration, and preferences for action-oriented or problem-solving approaches. Adapting the therapeutic frame to include collaborative goal-setting, skill-building, and normalizing help-seeking can significantly improve engagement and outcomes for male patients.
relevant_for: Psychologists and counselors treating depression in men's health and primary care settings

title: Exploring Psilocybin-Assisted Schema Therapy: A Conceptual Framework for Potential Therapeutic Synergies in Personality Disorders
authors: Barbieri
summary: This conceptual paper proposes a novel integration of psilocybin-assisted therapy with Schema Therapy (PAST) for personality disorders. Within a predictive coding framework, the authors draw parallels between Early Maladaptive Schemas and high-level priors, arguing that psilocybin’s ability to relax entrenched beliefs (per the REBUS/REBAS models) may enhance Schema Therapy’s experiential and cognitive interventions. The model suggests combining psilocybin sessions with ST integration work to strengthen adaptive modes and reduce dysfunctional schema modes, potentially reducing treatment duration for Cluster B and C personality disorders.
clinical_insight: Schema therapists working with personality-disordered patients may find the predictive coding rationale useful for conceptualizing why some patients show rigid resistance to schema mode shifts. The proposed PAST model offers a theoretical justification for combining psychedelic-facilitated cognitive flexibility with established ST techniques. However, the authors appropriately caution that this remains conceptual—no clinical data yet support psilocybin use in PD populations, and the potential for destabilization in borderline patients requires careful consideration.
relevant_for: Schema therapists and clinicians working with personality disorders who are following developments in psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy

title: From Noise to Signal: Leveraging Heterogeneity to Inform Personalised Mental Health for Autistic Adults
authors: Lauren Lawson, Mikle South, Krishan Chavinda, Achini Adikari, Ellen K. Richardson, Kogul Srikandabala, Eric M. J. Morris, Louise Hayes, Melanie Muniandy, vicki gibbs, Natasha Staheli, Amanda L. Richdale, Damminda Alahakoon
summary: Develops and evaluates personalised mental health interventions tailored to the specific needs of autistic adults, moving beyond one-size-fits-all approaches.
clinical_insight: Personalized mental health interventions for autistic adults significantly outperform generic approaches when they account for sensory sensitivities, social communication differences, and alexithymia in treatment delivery. Therapists should adapt session pacing, use visual supports, offer written session summaries, and avoid implicit social demands that standard protocols assume. Co-designing treatment goals with autistic clients around self-identified priorities rather than neurotypical benchmarks improves both engagement and outcomes.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists and therapists working with autistic adults

title: MDMA-Assisted Therapy for Social Anxiety Disorder: A Randomized, Open-label, Wait-list Controlled Trial
authors: Jason Luoma, M. Kati Lear, Brian Pilecki, Jenna Lejeune, Kyong Yi, Christina Chwyl, Seth Mehr, Aryan Sarparast, Liliana Julia Kennedy, Will Lucas, Angelica Spata, Christopher Stauffer
summary: Examines the potential of MDMA-assisted psychotherapy as a treatment for social anxiety disorder, discussing proposed mechanisms and preliminary evidence.
clinical_insight: MDMA-assisted therapy may reduce social anxiety by lowering threat sensitivity, increasing emotional openness, and enhancing the therapeutic alliance during exposure to social fear memories. If preliminary evidence holds, this approach could benefit treatment-resistant social anxiety patients who have not responded to multiple CBT or pharmacotherapy trials. Clinicians should stay informed of regulatory developments and clinical trial results to appropriately counsel interested patients about benefits, risks, and evidence status.
relevant_for: Clinical trial therapists and psychedelic-assisted therapy researchers

title: Dismantling the Mechanism of VR Self-Compassion Training: A Two-Session Controlled Trial with Active Controls
authors: Hongfeng Xia, Sarah Coundouris, Thomas C. Elliott, Nilufar Baghaei, Lena Oestreich, Lachlan Greig, Julie Henry, James Kirby
summary: Uses a dismantling design to identify the active components of virtual reality-based self-compassion interventions, determining which elements drive therapeutic change.
clinical_insight: Understanding which VR components—such as embodied perspective-taking, visual immersion, or compassionate avatar interaction—drive therapeutic change allows clinicians to adopt the most cost-effective elements of VR self-compassion interventions. Therapists integrating technology into practice can prioritize evidence-backed VR features while using traditional self-compassion exercises for the remaining components.
relevant_for: Clinicians incorporating virtual reality and digital therapeutics into practice

title: Dialectical Behavior Coaching to Prevent Activist Burnout
authors: Monika Neff Lind, Alexis Adams-Clark
summary: Adapts dialectical behavior therapy coaching principles for preventing and addressing burnout among social activists, extending DBT beyond traditional clinical populations.
clinical_insight: DBT skills such as distress tolerance, emotion regulation, and self-validation can be adapted into coaching formats for activists experiencing chronic burnout from sustained advocacy work. Clinicians in community and organizational settings can offer brief DBT-informed psychoeducation workshops that help activists recognize early burnout signs and apply actionable coping strategies.
relevant_for: Community psychologists and organizational mental health consultants working with advocacy and nonprofit groups

title: Matching cognitive behavioural interventions to psychological processes to personalise chronic pain treatments: an adapted group Delphi study with clinical and science experts
authors: Verena Eunike Hofmann, Julia Glombiewski, Saskia Scholten
summary: Uses Delphi methodology to develop consensus on which CBT process strategies best match different chronic pain presentations, creating a clinical decision-making framework.
clinical_insight: Provides a consensus-based decision framework to help clinicians select the most appropriate CBT process strategies—such as cognitive restructuring, behavioral activation, or acceptance-based techniques—for specific chronic pain presentations rather than applying a one-size-fits-all protocol. Therapists can use this framework during case conceptualization to tailor interventions, potentially improving outcomes for patients who have not responded to standard CBT for pain.
relevant_for: Pain psychologists and behavioral medicine clinicians working in multidisciplinary chronic pain clinics

title: Emotion Regulation in the Daily Lives of Women With and Without Eating Disorders: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
authors: Sarah Elizabeth Racine, Ege Bicaker, Alexia Miller, Vittoria Trolio, Lisa Yujia Zhu
summary: Ecological momentary assessment study of emotion regulation strategies in eating disorders, examining real-time associations between ER and disordered eating behaviors.
clinical_insight: Reveals real-time associations between specific emotion regulation strategies and disordered eating behaviors as they unfold in daily life, allowing therapists to pinpoint which ER strategies (e.g., suppression vs. reappraisal) serve as proximal triggers or buffers for bingeing or purging episodes. Clinicians can use these findings to personalize ER skills training, helping clients develop situation-specific regulation plans that target their unique antecedent patterns.
relevant_for: Eating disorder therapists and registered dietitians working in outpatient or residential ED treatment

title: Advancing Dimensional Models of Psychopathology in Cancer: Insights from Applying the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology (HiTOP)
authors: Darren Haywood, Jai Carmichael, Miriam K. Forbes, Roman Kotov, Robert Krueger, Aidan G.C. Wright, Susan Rossell, David Castle, Wendy Lam, Maryam Lustberg, Colin Vize, Anna Ugalde, Phyllis Butow, Nicolas Hart
summary: Applies the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology (HiTOP) dimensional model to cancer populations, examining how HiTOP's spectrum-based framework can improve understanding of comorbid psychopathology in oncology settings beyond traditional categorical diagnoses and inform personalized psychosocial interventions.
clinical_insight: Applying HiTOP dimensions to cancer-related psychopathology helps oncology clinicians move beyond comorbidity counting toward a dimensional understanding of how emotional distress, somatic symptoms, and personality factors interact. Therapists can use this framework to identify which hierarchical dimensions—such as internalizing, detachment, or thought dysfunction—are most prominent in a given cancer patient, guiding more precise psychopharmacological and psychotherapeutic interventions. This dimensional approach is particularly valuable given the complex, overlapping symptom profiles common in cancer populations.
relevant_for: Psycho-oncologists and mental health clinicians embedded in cancer treatment centers

title: Barriers and facilitators to improving sleep in mental health settings: A mixed-methods systematic review of patient and professional perspectives using the Theoretical Domains Framework
authors: Alexander Scott, Ciara Kelly, Abigail Locke, Jade Campbell, Professor Susan M. Sherman
summary: Identifies systemic and individual barriers to addressing sleep problems in mental health treatment settings, proposing organizational changes to integrate sleep care.
clinical_insight: Highlights that sleep problems are frequently overlooked in mental health treatment settings due to both systemic barriers (e.g., lack of screening protocols) and individual clinician factors (e.g., low confidence in sleep interventions). Clinicians and clinic directors should advocate for routine sleep screening at intake, integrate basic sleep hygiene and CBT-I principles into treatment plans, and pursue training in evidence-based sleep interventions to address this critical comorbidity.
relevant_for: Community mental health clinicians and clinical directors in outpatient psychiatric settings

title: Empathy for Suffering, Compassion, and Emotional Distancing in Police Work: A Longitudinal Cross-Lagged Study on Dual Pathways to Well-Being
authors: Nicolai J. Kleineidam, Andrea Fischbach, Marcel Kern
summary: Longitudinal cross-lagged study examining dual pathways between empathy for suffering, compassion, and emotional distancing in police work, testing whether compassion fatigue and protective emotional detachment develop through distinct mechanisms and differentially predict officer well-being and occupational functioning over time.
clinical_insight: Empathy and compassion training for police officers has downstream effects on crisis intervention outcomes, giving mental health consultants a structured curriculum to recommend when training law enforcement. Clinicians collaborating with police departments can advocate for evidence-based compassion programs that reduce escalation during mental health crisis calls, ultimately improving outcomes for the individuals in crisis. This research bridges clinical psychology and public safety, supporting the integration of mental health perspectives into first-responder training.
relevant_for: Police and public safety mental health consultants and crisis intervention team trainers

title: Modifying Belief Updating and Paranoia with Group Based Song-Making
authors: Deanna Greco, Sandy Camilo, Santiago Castiello de Obeso, Charlotte Freeland, Anthony Pavlo, Claire Bien, Constance Lubinski, Julia Nachemson, Adam Christoferson, Joshua Kenney, Philip R. Corlett
summary: Tests whether song-making activities can facilitate belief updating in individuals with paranoid ideation, exploring creative arts-based approaches to psychosis treatment.
clinical_insight: Shows that creative, non-verbal approaches like song-making can facilitate belief updating in individuals with paranoid ideation, offering an alternative pathway when traditional cognitive techniques face resistance. Therapists working with psychosis can incorporate structured creative arts activities to indirectly challenge rigid paranoid beliefs, particularly for clients who struggle with direct cognitive disputation or verbal confrontation.
relevant_for: Psychosis therapists and early intervention in psychosis (EIP) team clinicians

title: Towards precision psychotherapy: Leveraging session-level data to predict session-by-session treatment outcomes
authors: Felix Strakeljahn, Tania Marie Lincoln, Niklas Jacobs, Celine Brandt, Kevin Hilbert, Katrin Bahlinger, Inga Frantz, Björn Schlier
summary: Using data from 327 outpatients receiving CBT for affective, anxiety, and psychotic disorders in routine German care, this study built mixed-effect random forest models to predict session-by-session mental health scores. The models achieved meaningful prediction accuracy, and SHAP analyses identified therapist-reported cognitive change and patient-reported coping as key drivers of outcome predictions.
clinical_insight: This study demonstrates that session-level process data can meaningfully predict next-session outcomes, moving beyond the traditional 'wait and see' approach. The finding that therapist-rated cognitive change is a stronger predictor than patient self-report suggests clinicians' in-session judgments carry incremental predictive value. The monocentric design limits generalizability across treatment settings and orientations.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners and clinic directors interested in session-by-session outcome monitoring and early warning systems

title: Efficacy of Anonymous Internet-Based Self-Help for Social Anxiety in a Partially Randomized Patient Preference Trial
authors: Stefanie Arnold, Michael Vogt, Dajana Šipka, Johanna Boettcher, Friederike Fenski, Malte Elson, Thomas Berger
summary: In a partially randomized patient preference trial, 452 adults with social anxiety were assigned to an anonymous or standard 8-week unguided internet-based CBT self-help program. Both conditions produced significant reductions with medium-to-large within-group effects maintained at 24-week follow-up. The anonymous version did not differ in efficacy from the identified version.
clinical_insight: Privacy concerns are among the most frequently cited barriers to seeking mental health treatment, particularly for social anxiety where fear of negative evaluation is the core pathology. The finding that anonymity preserves efficacy is practically significant for iCBT platforms. The preference trial design revealed that patient format preference did not differentially affect outcomes.
relevant_for: Clinicians offering or recommending internet-based CBT for social anxiety, and developers of digital mental health interventions

title: Internet-Based Self-Help Treatment for Panic Disorder with Minimal Therapist Contact: A Randomized Controlled Trial in a Spanish Clinical Population
authors: Jorge Orrego Bravo
summary: Randomized controlled trial of internet-based cognitive behavioral therapy for panic disorder, examining efficacy and comparing guided and unguided formats.
clinical_insight: Demonstrates that internet-based CBT for panic disorder can be effective, with guided formats likely showing superior outcomes to unguided ones. Clinicians can consider recommending guided iCBT as a scalable first-line or stepped-care option for patients with panic disorder, particularly those in underserved areas or who prefer remote treatment, while maintaining therapist oversight to maximize engagement and outcomes.
relevant_for: CBT therapists and anxiety disorder specialists expanding into digital or telehealth service delivery

title: Difficulties in Identifying Causes of Emotions Prospectively Predict the Persistence of Suicidal Thoughts: A Six-Week Longitudinal Study
authors: Veronika Chernetska, Yael Millgram
summary: Investigates how the ability to identify causes of one's emotions relates to the persistence of suicidal thoughts, with implications for emotion-focused interventions.
clinical_insight: Suggests that difficulty identifying the causes of one's emotions may contribute to the persistence of suicidal thoughts, pointing to alexithymia or emotional awareness deficits as therapeutic targets. Clinicians should assess and explicitly build clients' capacity for emotion cause identification—using skills from emotion-focused therapy or dialectical behavior therapy—as part of suicide risk interventions to help disrupt the ruminative cycles that maintain suicidal ideation.
relevant_for: Suicide prevention clinicians and crisis intervention therapists working with high-risk clients

title: Daily symptom associations in prolonged grief: a dynamic network approach
authors: Huy Hoang Le, Kai Jannik Nehler, Mirjam Sophie Rueger, Qiqi Liang, Ilena Fruchi, Eva-Maria Stelzer, Clare Killikelly
summary: Applies dynamic network analysis to understand symptom interactions in prolonged grief disorder, identifying central symptoms and temporal patterns of symptom activation.
clinical_insight: Identifies central symptoms and temporal activation patterns in prolonged grief disorder through network analysis, suggesting which symptoms act as primary drivers of the grief network at different stages. Clinicians can prioritize targeting these bridge or central symptoms—such as yearning or identity disruption—early in treatment to produce cascading improvements across the broader symptom network, optimizing the sequencing of grief-focused interventions.
relevant_for: Grief counselors, bereavement therapists, and complicated grief treatment providers

title: Breaking barriers to accessing mental healthcare for autistic people: the Autistic SPACE Framework for adult mental health settings
authors: Catherine Dakin, Daniel Davies, Bernadette Grosjean, Malcolm Kinnear, Jason Lang, Meng-Chuan Lai, Sam Porter, Anna Rebowska, Esther Sabel, Khurram Sadiq, Sebastian C. K. Shaw, Mary Doherty
summary: Introduces the SPACE Framework for supporting autistic individuals in clinical practice, providing a structured approach to neurodiversity-affirming care.
clinical_insight: Introduces a structured, neurodiversity-affirming framework (SPACE) that guides clinicians in adapting their assessment, formulation, and intervention approaches for autistic clients. Therapists can use this model to move beyond deficit-based models, ensuring sensory sensitivities, communication preferences, and social context are systematically addressed in treatment planning and session structure.
relevant_for: Neurodiversity-affirming therapists and clinicians assessing or treating autistic adults in outpatient settings

title: Self-Harm in Eating Disorders: Identifying Contextual Patterns Using an Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
authors: Pendrous et al.
summary: This EMA study recruited 43 participants from specialist eating disorder outpatient services, prompting them six times daily for 14 days via smartphone app. Seventy-eight percent reported at least one self-harm thought and 42% reported at least one self-harm act. When self-harm thoughts arose, participants were commonly alone, experiencing worry, and considering cutting. Actual self-harm acts were preceded by 1-30 minutes of self-harm rumination and were accompanied by self-hatred. The findings highlight the co-morbidity of eating disorders and self-harm and the need for co-designed safety planning in outpatient ED services.
clinical_insight: This study provides the first granular, momentary-level map of when and how self-harm unfolds in eating disorder patients in their daily lives. For clinicians working with ED populations, the key takeaway is that self-harm thoughts often emerge in the context of worry and solitude, with a build-up period of 1-30 minutes before acting — a window that safety plans can explicitly target.
