This computational approach provides an objective framework for distinguishing them: OCD patients may be more responsive to interventions targeting positive feedback processing (e.g., behavioral experiments that disconfirm catastrophic predictions), while paranoid patients may need interventions targeting negative feedback processing (e.g., cognitive restructuring of threat beliefs). A blind spot is that the study does not examine how these computational signatures change with treatment, leaving it unclear whether successful therapy normalizes the specific deficit or works through compensatory mechanisms.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating OCD and psychosis-spectrum presentations who need to differentiate between obsessive and paranoid cognitive patterns

title: Period and Prejudice: Cultural Differences in Menstrual Health between South Asian and White British Women
authors: Michele Denise Birtel, Lucia Volpi, Aisha Mirza, Laura M Konig
summary: This preregistered study (N=306, equal South Asian and White British women) provides a comprehensive comparison of menstrual health across four domains: experiences, literacy, stigma, and healthcare microaggressions. The most striking finding was the large effect for avoidance behaviors: South Asian women reported significantly greater avoidance in 8 of 11 domains assessed, including working outside the home, exercise, cooking, housekeeping, talking to men about menstruation, seeking support from both male and female health professionals, engaging in prayers, and sexual intimacy (partial eta-squared = .319). This avoidance was largely mediated by menstrual secrecy (not by internalized loathing) and by lower menstrual health literacy. South Asian women also reported higher pain-related menstrual distress, lower menstrual health literacy (less likely to attribute menstruation to physiological causes), and more frequent ethnic microaggressions in healthcare settings. Contrary to simplistic expectations, South Asian women reported more positive emotions toward menstruation than White British women, suggesting their cultural framing of menstruation may be more accepting in principle even while behavioral avoidance remains high. Mediation analyses confirmed that menstrual literacy and secrecy are key mechanistic pathways between cultural background and stigma outcomes.
clinical_insight: For therapists, this study reframes menstrual stigma in South Asian women as fundamentally a behavioral avoidance problem driven by secrecy and literacy gaps, rather than an attitudinal problem of disgust or shame. This distinction matters for treatment: psychoeducation that normalizes menstruation physiologically may be more effective than approaches targeting emotional attitudes alone. The healthcare microaggression findings are particularly concerning — if South Asian women are already avoiding male healthcare providers and experiencing microaggressions in medical settings, this creates a compounding barrier where both the cultural secrecy and the healthcare system itself reinforce avoidance of necessary medical care. Therapists should be aware that South Asian women may need support specifically for navigating gynaecological appointments, and that asking about menstrual health should be approached with cultural sensitivity. The large effect size for avoidance behaviors (partial eta-squared = .319) indicates this is not a minor cultural difference but a substantial clinical concern affecting multiple domains of daily functioning.
relevant_for: Therapists working with women from South Asian backgrounds, sexual health therapists, couple therapists, gynaecological counsellors, clinicians working on cultural competence.

title: Clinical Utility of the Level of Personality Functioning Scale: A Survey of German Mental Health Professionals
authors: Leon P. Wendt, Eva M. Klein, Cord Benecke, Susanne Singer, Lena Maier, Deborah Engesser, Yannik van Haaren, Johannes Zimmermann
summary: Surveyed 346 German mental health professionals on the clinical utility of the Level of Personality Functioning Scale (LPFS), a clinician-rated global evaluation of personality functioning aligned with the DSM-5 AMPD and ICD-11 dimensional approach to personality disorders. The study examined perceived usefulness, ease of use, and barriers to implementation in routine clinical practice.
clinical_insight: The shift from categorical to dimensional personality disorder assessment (DSM-5 AMPD, ICD-11) is gaining traction internationally. This survey provides practical intelligence on how frontline clinicians experience the LPFS — its strengths in capturing severity gradients, and its limitations in time-pressed settings. Therapists working with personality pathology should consider integrating the LPFS or similar dimensional tools to track functional impairment across self and interpersonal domains, which can guide treatment planning more flexibly than categorical diagnoses.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing and treating personality disorders, particularly those transitioning to DSM-5 Alternative Model or ICD-11 dimensional approaches

title: Identifying Signal in the Noise: A Systematic Review of Resting State fMRI after Traumatic Brain Injury
authors: Emily L. Dennis, Mary R Newsome, Hannah M. Lindsey, Nicola L. de Souza, Adrian I. Onicas, Finian Keleher, Courtney McCabe, Charles Rey, C. Lexi Baird, Hollie Mullen, Hilary Russell, Maya Wilde, Dayna Thayne, Sabrina Cange, Manuel Estrada, David Mercado, Ocean Pruden, Brian Szekely, Christine M. Mullen, Carrie Esopenko, Elisabeth A. Wilde, Hector Arciniega, Frank Hillary
summary: This large-scale systematic review synthesizes 219 resting-state fMRI studies of traumatic brain injury across six distinct populations (civilian mTBI, moderate-severe TBI, sports-related, military, pediatric, and IPV-related), remapping all findings onto a common 13-network cortical atlas to enable cross-study comparison for the first time. The review addresses the field's central challenge — apparent inconsistency in findings — by identifying three key moderators: time since injury (the single most important), population and injury severity, and analytical methodology (ICA vs. seed-based vs. graph theory approaches, which produce systematically different results from the same data). The review documents coherent network-level patterns: the default mode network is the most heterogeneous and population-sensitive, the frontoparietal network is the most consistently hypoconnected, and the salience network varies most with clinical context. A non-monotonic recovery trajectory is described for moderate-to-severe TBI, progressing from acute disconnection through compensatory hyperconnectivity to either normalization or persistent disconnection. The review includes AI-assisted data extraction using NotebookLM and Claude (verified against primary sources) and proposes specific recommendations for the field including adoption of shared processing pipelines, longitudinal designs with multimodal measures, and data sharing through existing repositories.
clinical_insight: This review's most clinically actionable finding is that the apparent chaos in the rsfMRI-TBI literature resolves into coherent patterns when organized properly. For therapists, this means that a client's neuroimaging results should not be interpreted in isolation but in the context of their injury-to-assessment interval and population characteristics. A patient scanned at 2 months post-injury with DMN hypoconnectivity may be on a very different trajectory than one scanned at 18 months. The review's emphasis on time-since-injury as the dominant moderator has a direct formulation implication: therapists should obtain the date of injury and map the assessment timeline when interpreting neuroimaging reports, as the clinical meaning of connectivity changes shifts substantially over the first 1-2 years. The identification of consistent frontoparietal hypoconnectivity across populations suggests that executive function deficits may be the most robust neurological marker of TBI across diverse injury types, which can guide cognitive rehabilitation planning. The practical barriers to clinical translation — methodological variability and small sample sizes — are also important for therapists to understand when evaluating the reliability of neuroimaging referrals.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists, therapists in brain injury rehabilitation, clinical researchers, clinicians interpreting neuroimaging reports for TBI clients, rehabilitation specialists.

title: Guided Recall of Positive Autobiographical Memories Increases Anticipated Pleasure and Psychological Resources, and Reduces Depressive Symptoms: A Replication and Extension of a Randomized Controlled Trial of Brief Positive Cognitive-Reminiscence Therapy
authors: Hallford et al.
summary: This RCT (N=75) replicated and extended findings on a three-session group-based positive-memory version of cognitive-reminiscence therapy (CRT). Relative to controls, the CRT group showed significantly higher anticipated pleasure (d=0.76-0.93), improved self-esteem, self-efficacy, and optimism (d=0.58-0.99), and reduced depressive symptoms (d=0.56-0.67) at post-treatment and follow-up. The intervention works partly through reducing automatic negative thoughts and enhancing awareness of narrative identity.
clinical_insight: CRT offers a brief, structured, and highly transportable intervention that directly targets the impoverished positive future-thinking seen in depression. Clinicians can deploy it as a standalone 3-session module or as an adjunctive ‘memory booster’ within CBT, schema therapy, or behavioral activation protocols. The anticipated pleasure findings are particularly noteworthy—they suggest CRT may directly remediate anhedonia, one of the most treatment-resistant depressive symptoms. A blind spot: the sample was predominantly female and the long-term durability beyond follow-up remains untested.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with adults experiencing depression, anhedonia, or low self-worth, especially in brief-therapy or primary care settings.

title: Autistic adults integrate the timing of beat gestures and spoken prosody
authors: Matteo Maran, Kim Jonkman, Robert Jertberg, Lotus Verbiest, Sander Begeer, Erik Van der Burg, Hans Rutger Bosker
summary:
This large-scale online study (N=166: 101 autistic adults, 65 non-autistic adults) investigated whether autistic adults integrate beat gestures (simple up-and-down hand movements that accompany speech) with spoken prosody to the same extent as non-autistic adults. Participants judged the perceived stress pattern of disyllabic words (e.g., CONtent vs. conTENT) presented in three audiovisual conditions: beat gesture on the first syllable (BeatOn1), beat gesture on the second syllable (BeatOn2), or no beat gesture (NoBeat). Using a visual analog scale, participants rated stress patterns on a Strong-Weak to Weak-Strong continuum.

Results showed that beat gestures significantly biased stress perception in both groups: BeatOn1 increased Strong-Weak ratings compared to BeatOn2 and NoBeat (all p<.001), and BeatOn2 decreased SW ratings compared to NoBeat (p=.003). Critically, there was no significant group difference (GroupNon-Autistic contrast: p=.461), no group-by-condition interaction (p=.598), and no relationship between audiovisual integration effects and autistic traits measured by the Autism Quotient-28 or the Action and Feelings Questionnaire. Bayesian analysis provided moderate evidence for the null hypothesis of no group difference (BF10 = 0.22). The study was pre-registered and used a large sample compared to prior work in this area.
clinical_insight: These findings challenge the long-standing assumption that autistic individuals have attenuated multisensory integration. For therapists working with autistic adults, this suggests that visual communicative cues such as hand gestures are processed effectively and can be leveraged in therapy without special accommodation. The results align with a growing body of evidence showing that audiovisual integration differences in autism may be developmental rather than persistent into adulthood. Clinicians should be cautious about applying child-based research findings to adult autistic clients, and should not assume that multimodal communication approaches need modification for autistic adults. The study also found no relationship between autistic traits and integration ability, suggesting this is not a trait-dependent phenomenon.
relevant_for: Therapists working with autistic adults, neurodivergence-affirming practitioners, speech-language therapists, clinical psychologists updating their understanding of autism across the lifespan.

title: Evidence that Tetris Reduces Immediate but Not Subsequent Daily Intrusions of a Trauma Film: A Multilab Replication Study
authors: Wessel et al.
summary: A multilab replication (13 labs, N=739) tested whether playing Tetris after trauma film viewing reduces intrusive memories. Results confirmed Tetris reduces immediate intrusions but found no evidence it reduces subsequent daily intrusions, challenging the clinical promise of brief Tetris-based interventions for preventing PTSD symptom development after trauma exposure.
clinical_insight: The finding that Tetris reduces immediate but not daily intrusions tempers enthusiasm for using it as a standalone early intervention after trauma. Therapists working in acute settings should view Tetris as a potentially useful adjunct that may help in the immediate hours post-trauma but cannot replace comprehensive early interventions such as psychological first aid or trauma-focused CBT. This also highlights the gap between lab analog paradigms and real-world traumatic experiences, reminding clinicians that experimental findings require careful translation to clinical practice.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists and crisis intervention workers in emergency and acute care settings

title: Off-days have real-world consequences: Using high-frequency cognitive assessment to predict naturalistic driving
authors: Andrew J. Aschenbrenner, Joshua J. Jackson, Sarah H. Stout, Yiqi Zhu, David C. Brown, Noor Al-Hammadi, John C. Morris, Jason Hassenstab, Ganesh M. Babulal
summary:
This study examined whether day-to-day fluctuations in cognitive performance ("off-days") predict real-world behavior in 160 older adults (mean age ~72, many at elevated risk for cognitive decline) who completed in-home cognitive testing and had GPS-tracked driving behavior over approximately 3 years (19,628 individual trips). Participants completed brief cognitive assessments via the Digit Symbol Substitution Test (DSST) on a tablet up to twice daily. Driving outcomes included adverse events (hard braking, speeding, lane deviations) and the proportion of trips avoided at night.

Using mixed-effects models with AICc-based model selection, the study found that within-person deviations in DSST scores from each individual's personal mean were significantly associated with adverse driving events on the same day — even after controlling for global cognitive ability, age, and sex. A model including within-person cognitive deviations was 3 times more supported than a base model and over 8 times more likely than a model using only global cognition. Crucially, it was not the absolute level of cognitive performance that predicted unsafe driving, but deviations from one's own baseline. Higher cognitive variability was also associated with a greater tendency to avoid driving at night, suggesting partial awareness of off-days.
clinical_insight: This study has direct implications for assessing capacity and safety in older adults and clinical populations where cognitive fluctuation is relevant. The key finding — that within-person deviation, not absolute performance, predicts functional outcomes — suggests that clinicians should track cognitive performance over time rather than relying on single time-point assessments. For therapists working with older adults, dementia risk, or post-concussion populations, this supports incorporating ambulatory cognitive monitoring into treatment planning. The "off-day" concept may also apply to psychiatric conditions where cognitive function fluctuates (e.g., depression, bipolar disorder, chronic fatigue). Clients who report having a "bad day" cognitively may indeed be at elevated risk for functional impairment, and this should be validated rather than dismissed.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with older adults, neuropsychologists, occupational therapists assessing driving fitness, therapists working with conditions involving cognitive fluctuation.

title: Stability and Transition Likelihood of Primary Symptoms in Adults with Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A 5-Year Prospective Follow-Up Study
authors: Obisie-Orlu et al.
summary: A 5-year prospective follow-up study examining the stability and transition patterns of primary OCD symptoms in adults, tracking how symptom presentations change or persist over time. The study provides longitudinal data on which OCD symptom dimensions are most stable and which are most likely to transition, informing long-term treatment planning and prognosis.
clinical_insight: Understanding which OCD symptoms remain stable versus which tend to shift over a 5-year period helps therapists set realistic treatment expectations and plan for potential symptom migration. When patients present with changing OCD presentations years into treatment, this may reflect natural symptom transition rather than treatment failure, and clinicians should adapt intervention targets accordingly rather than assuming relapse. The finding that some symptom dimensions are highly stable reinforces the value of dimension-specific treatment planning using approaches like exposure and response prevention tailored to the dominant symptom profile.
relevant_for: OCD specialists and clinical psychologists providing long-term treatment for adult obsessive-compulsive disorder

title: Quantum of Silence: The Burden of Imposed Secrecy
authors: Valentina Bianchi, Katharine Greenaway
summary:
This theoretical paper proposes a five-component framework for understanding how professionally imposed secrecy — secrets that individuals are organizationally mandated to keep — affects psychological well-being. Unlike personal secrets (freely chosen), professional secrets are extrinsically motivated and carry high stakes if disclosed, creating a distinct psychological burden. The framework integrates insights from motivation theory (Self-Determination Theory), secret-keeping research, mind-wandering literature, and occupational psychology.

The five components are: (1) WHY — the motivational continuum from intrinsic to extrinsic regulation, predicting that externally imposed secrets carry greater well-being costs than internally motivated ones; (2) WHAT — the content and subjective appraisal of secrets (valence, importance, uncertainty, morality), where negatively appraised secrets are hypothesized to be most burdensome; (3) HOW — the processes of actively concealing information, including conscious suppression, deliberate mind-wandering to secret content, and rumination, all linked to cognitive load and reduced well-being; (4) WHO — individual differences including personality traits (e.g., honesty-humility, conscientiousness), psychological hardiness, and prior trauma history that may buffer or amplify secrecy's impact; (5) WHERE — the professional context, including organizational culture, duration of secrecy requirements, availability of social support, and whether secrecy is occasional or central to the role.

The paper specifically discusses implications for psychologists and therapists who hold client confidences as professional secrets, noting the potential for vicarious trauma when secret content is disturbing, and the tension between ethical obligations of confidentiality and the personal burden of concealment.
clinical_insight: This framework is directly relevant to therapists, counselors, and psychologists who routinely hold professional secrets. The distinction between the burden of knowing disturbing content (vicarious trauma pathway) versus the burden of active concealment (cognitive load pathway) maps onto well-known clinical challenges. Therapists working in forensic settings, with high-risk clients, or under mandated reporting obligations may experience particular strain. The framework suggests that organizational support structures, peer consultation groups, and supervision may mitigate the burden by shifting secrets from externally regulated toward more integrated regulation on the motivational continuum. The paper also highlights that individual differences matter — therapists with higher psychological hardiness or stronger identity integration around their professional role may be more resilient to secrecy-related strain.
relevant_for: Psychotherapists, clinical psychologists, counselors, supervisors, forensic mental health practitioners, organizational psychologists supporting professionals in high-secrecy roles.

title: Self-efficacy in Internet-based Mental Health Interventions: A Meta-analysis
authors: Behr et al.
summary: Meta-analysis of 70 RCTs (N=17,407) found moderate effects of internet-based interventions on self-efficacy, with guided interventions showing the largest effects. Self-efficacy emerged as a mediator of treatment outcomes, and lower baseline self-efficacy patients may benefit most.
clinical_insight: Supports explicitly targeting self-efficacy early in iCBT, particularly for patients with low confidence. The finding that lower self-efficacy patients benefit most is clinically important but counterintuitive. Lack of long-term follow-up on self-efficacy trajectories is a notable gap.
relevant_for: Clinicians delivering guided internet-based interventions who want to optimize treatment mechanisms

title: Sorting the Good from the mIERda — Introducing and Testing the Multidimensional Insufficient Effort Responding Detection Algorithm
authors: Ariane Gauthier, Luc Pelletier, Christopher Gravel
summary:
This paper introduces mIERda (multidimensional Insufficient Effort Responding detection algorithm), a machine learning approach using fair-cut random forests to detect insufficient effort responding (IER) in questionnaire data — a pervasive problem in clinical and research assessments that can inflate or distort measurement. Unlike existing methods that rely on arbitrary cut-off scores on single indices, mIERda combines multiple IER indices (IERIs) into a single continuous Insufficient Effort Responding Index (IERI) and uses a classification algorithm that outperforms sequential cut-off approaches.

Across four simulation studies (Study 1a-1c) and one empirical study (Study 2), the algorithm was tested under varying conditions: severity of IER (low, medium, high), prevalence (5-30%), sample sizes (500-5000), and six distinct IER response patterns (random, straight-lining, alternating, infrequent, floor, mixed). Key findings: mIERda achieved AUC values of 0.85-0.99 across simulation conditions, significantly outperforming the best sequential cut-off approach (balanced accuracy ~0.70-0.85). The algorithm was particularly robust at detecting mixed IER patterns (AUC = 0.97), which are the most common but hardest to detect. In the empirical validation using real questionnaire data, mIERda identified respondents with significantly lower standard deviations, shorter completion times, and more predictable response patterns compared to flagged-and-removed respondents.

The algorithm is implemented in an R package that takes raw item-level data as input and outputs a probability score for each respondent, requiring no pre-specified cut-offs from the user.
clinical_insight: IER is a critical threat to the validity of clinical assessments, symptom measures, and personality inventories used in therapy and research. A client who responds randomly or mechanically to a depression inventory, trauma questionnaire, or personality assessment can produce misleading results that may lead to incorrect case formulation, wrong treatment decisions, or erroneous research conclusions. mIERda offers a practical, cut-off-free solution that can be applied post-hoc to existing datasets. The key advantage over existing tools (such as long-string indices, Mahalanobis distance, or evenness indices used individually) is the combination of multiple IER indices into a single model, capturing heterogeneous response patterns that single-index approaches miss. For clinicians and researchers who use self-report measures, integrating mIERda screening into data processing pipelines could substantially improve data quality and diagnostic accuracy.
relevant_for: Clinical researchers using self-report measures, psychometricians, assessment developers, program evaluators, any practitioner who relies on questionnaire data for clinical decision-making.

title: Underground Small-Group Therapy of Treatment-Resistant Depression and C-PTSD with Psilocybin: A Retrospective Case Study
authors: Turkia
summary: This retrospective case study details the complete treatment process of a woman in her early forties with three decades of treatment-resistant depression rooted in early complex trauma (domestic violence, sexual abuse, poverty). After antidepressant medications proved ineffective and caused adverse effects, she participated in approximately six psilocybin sessions over 1.5 years within an underground small-group format combining breathing exercises, cold exposure, physical exercise, and music. Psilocybin appeared to produce an 'anti-dissociative' effect, interrupting trauma-related dissociation and allowing controlled re-experiencing of previously overwhelming somatic sensations. Depression remission was achieved and maintained at 2.5-year follow-up. The author argues that current clinical trials using only one or two doses may be insufficient for complex trauma presentations.
clinical_insight: While this is a single-case retrospective study from an uncontrolled setting — limiting generalisability — it offers a detailed clinical narrative that resonates with therapists working treatment-resistant complex trauma. The concept of psilocybin as an 'anti-dissociative' that reconnects patients with embodied traumatic memory is clinically provocative and aligns with emerging theories of psychedelic-assisted therapy. The key clinical insight is the multi-dose, extended-timeline argument: current trial designs may underestimate the number of sessions needed for C-PTSD remission. Clinicians should be aware that patients with complex trauma histories who pursue psychedelic therapy may need more than the standard one-to-two session protocol to achieve lasting change.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists and clinicians whose patients are considering or undergoing psychedelic-assisted therapy for treatment-resistant depression and C-PTSD

title: Subjective and physiological measures reveal emotionally salient moments in virtual everyday life
authors: Ruhoollah Akhundzadeh, Johanna Hokka, Mikko Sams, Eeva Puumala, Heini Saarimaki
summary:
This study investigated whether emotionally salient moments measured in a naturalistic virtual reality (VR) environment correspond to those identified in actual everyday life, addressing the longstanding question of whether laboratory emotion findings generalize to real-world contexts. Participants experienced ethnographically informed VR scenarios depicting everyday activities (commuting, socializing, working, relaxing) while their subjective emotional responses and physiological signals (EDA, heart rate, respiration) were continuously recorded.

The study compared subjective emotional experience and physiological responses across VR scenarios, examining convergence between self-reported emotional salience and physiological markers. The VR environment was designed based on time-use surveys and ethnographic observation to maximize ecological validity. Results demonstrated that subjective and physiological measures captured emotionally salient moments in VR, though the correspondence between subjective ratings and physiological activation was not always straightforward — some emotionally significant moments showed physiological responses that differed from traditional laboratory findings.
clinical_insight: The ecological validity gap between controlled laboratory paradigms and real-world emotional experience is a fundamental challenge for clinical emotion research. This study shows that VR can serve as a bridge, offering experimental control while maintaining ecological validity. For clinical assessment, this suggests that VR-based emotion probes could eventually complement or enhance traditional clinical interviews and self-report measures, particularly for conditions where emotional reactivity is central (e.g., anxiety disorders, PTSD, emotion regulation difficulties). The finding that subjective and physiological measures do not always align in naturalistic contexts also has clinical implications: therapists should not assume that physiological arousal always corresponds to subjective distress, and multi-method assessment remains essential.
relevant_for: Clinical researchers studying emotion, therapists working with emotion regulation difficulties, developers of clinical assessment tools, researchers interested in ecological momentary assessment and VR-based interventions.

title: MDMA, Internal Family Systems Therapy, and the Minnesota Model in the Resolution of C-PTSD-Induced Alcohol and Diazepam Addiction: A Retrospective Case Study
authors: Turkia
summary: This case study illustrates the integrated use of MDMA-assisted therapy combined with Internal Family Systems (IFS) for a man in his forties with C-PTSD-induced addiction to alcohol and diazepam, rooted in witnessing severe chronic domestic violence in childhood. After multiple failed quit attempts and a suicidal crisis, the patient completed a 28-day Minnesota-model abstinence retreat, achieving initial but fragile sobriety. A single MDMA-assisted IFS session then allowed him to safely re-experience previously dissociated traumatic memories in an embodied way, resolving core C-PTSD symptoms. A subsequent non-drug IFS session consolidated gains. The case demonstrates how MDMA may facilitate the IFS process of accessing and compassionately engaging with exile parts carrying traumatic burden.
clinical_insight: This is the first recent case study to detail the practical integration of MDMA with IFS — a combination with strong theoretical alignment, as both approaches emphasise compassionate engagement with protective and wounded parts of the psyche. For clinicians in Australia (where MDMA is now prescribable for PTSD) and those preparing for potential regulatory changes elsewhere, this case provides a concrete clinical roadmap. The sequence matters: abstinence stabilisation first, then MDMA-assisted processing of the deepest traumatic material, followed by non-drug IFS consolidation. The case highlights that substance addiction in C-PTSD often functions as avoidance of emotional pain, and that resolving the underlying trauma rather than targeting the addiction directly may produce more durable outcomes. The single-case design and underground treatment setting limit evidential strength.
relevant_for: Addiction and trauma therapists, particularly those integrating IFS or preparing for MDMA-assisted therapy practice

title: Revisiting the Blind Implantation Paradigm: Robustness of False Memory Induction Under Controlled Conditions
authors: Elsa Gewehr, Sarah Viertel, Henry Otgaar, Kristin Jankowsky
summary:
This study conducted a conceptual replication and extension of the Blind Implantation paradigm (Otgaar et al., 2022), a scalable method for inducing false autobiographical memories without human interaction. The sample (N=348) was three times larger than the original, using a multi-target event design with three critical events and a non-suggestive control group. The procedure involved a personalized profiling phase where participants reported real autobiographical events, followed by an AI-assisted suggestive feedback phase claiming that certain events had been independently verified.

Results confirmed that the paradigm reliably induces false beliefs and false memories, supporting the reconstructive nature of memory. However, induction rates were substantially lower than originally reported: 12-15% for false memories and approximately 30% for false beliefs across events, compared to the original study's ~40% and ~60% respectively. The multi-target design revealed variability across events, suggesting that event characteristics influence suggestibility. The control group showed minimal false endorsement (<2%), confirming that the suggestive feedback — not demand characteristics — drives the effect. Memory quality ratings for false memories were significantly lower than for true memories, suggesting that even induced false memories are experienced as less vivid and detailed.
clinical_insight: False memory research has direct relevance to clinical practice, particularly in forensic psychology, trauma therapy, and any context where memory accuracy matters. The lower-than-originally-reported induction rates are reassuring — while false memories can be created through suggestive techniques, a majority of participants resisted the manipulation even under optimized conditions. For trauma-focused therapists, this study underscores the importance of using non-suggestive memory recovery techniques. The finding that false memories are experienced as less vivid than true memories provides a potential metacognitive cue that clinicians and clients can use to evaluate memory accuracy. The study also demonstrates that AI-assisted methods can create convincing but false personal narratives, with implications for understanding how suggestive influences operate in therapeutic, legal, and digital contexts.
relevant_for: Forensic psychologists, trauma therapists, clinical psychologists working with memory, legal mental health professionals, researchers studying suggestibility and memory distortion.

title: Self-Treatment of Depression and Complex Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder with Psilocybin and LSD: A Retrospective Case Study
authors: Turkia
summary: This retrospective case study describes a young man with early-onset complex trauma from chronic school-based abuse who developed severe, treatment-resistant anxiety and depression. After supportive psychotherapy failed and psychiatric contact was experienced as rejecting, he turned to unsupervised self-treatment with psilocybin at age 19. Occasional high-dose sessions alone produced only temporary relief. From age 25, he adopted a more intensive, structured approach with gradually declining doses, focusing on reliving traumatic events while maintaining conscious awareness of somatic sensations and avoiding overwhelm. By age 30, he reported resolving most early trauma and achieving a foundational sense of stability, though societal trauma exposure continued to cause suffering. The author proposes reconceptualising psychedelics as tools for understanding ordinary mental states rather than pathological ones.
clinical_insight: This case is notable for its detailed documentation of a self-directed, multi-year psychedelic process — a reality many clinicians will encounter as patients increasingly present with unsupervised psychedelic use histories. The key clinical insight is the patient own discovery that gradual dose reduction combined with sustained somatic focus produced better results than occasional high-dose sessions, directly challenging the prevailing clinical trial model of single or double high-dose administration. The case also illustrates how iatrogenic harm from psychiatric services (experienced rejection) can push patients toward self-treatment, raising questions about how clinicians receive and respond to patients psychedelic experiences. A significant limitation is the retrospective, self-reported nature of the data, and the absence of standardised outcome measures.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists whose patients have used or are considering using psychedelics for self-treatment of C-PTSD and treatment-resistant depression

title: The Relationship Between Trauma Type, Coping Style, and Posttraumatic Symptomatology
authors: Pauline Hartmann, Aleksandra E. Rupietta, Antonia Döring, Marcella L. Woud
summary:
This secondary analysis of an online study (N = 300, 76.7% female, M age = 25.52, SD = 6.79) examined how trauma type (interpersonal vs. non-interpersonal) and coping style (problem-focused, emotion-focused, avoidant) relate to posttraumatic symptomatology. Participants had experienced a DSM-5 traumatic event within the past 18 months, assessed via the Life Events Checklist for DSM-5 (LEC-5). The sample included 99 participants with interpersonal trauma and 201 with non-interpersonal trauma. Sexual assault was the most common interpersonal trauma (n = 32), while severe human suffering was the most common non-interpersonal trauma (n = 53).

Interpersonal trauma was associated with significantly higher PTSD symptoms (d = .53, p < .001), depression (d = .44, p < .001), anxiety (d = .37, p < .01), and stress (d = .36, p < .01), plus lower life satisfaction (d = -.39, p < .01). The interpersonal trauma group also reported significantly higher avoidant coping (d = .31, p = .03) and lower problem-focused coping (d = -.34, p < .01), with no difference in emotion-focused coping (d = -.23, p = .07).

Within both trauma groups, avoidant coping was the only coping style significantly correlated with PTSD symptoms. In hierarchical regression, avoidant coping was the sole significant predictor of PTSD symptoms in both the interpersonal group (β = .61, p < .001) and the non-interpersonal group (β = .42, p < .001). Cross-group, trauma type itself predicted PTSD symptoms (β = .52, p < .001).

An exploratory mediation analysis (not pre-registered) found that avoidant coping partially mediated the association between trauma type and PTSD symptoms (indirect effect a*b = .15, 95% CI [.02, .28], p = .02), with the direct effect remaining significant (c' = .37, 95% CI [.15, .58], p = .001). Measures used: PCL-5 (α = .95), DASS-21 (depression α = .92, anxiety α = .84, stress α = .90), SWLS (α = .90), and Brief-COPE. Data collection ran September 2022 to October 2024. Both the original study and these secondary analyses were pre-registered on AsPredicted.
clinical_insight: The finding that avoidant coping partially mediates the relationship between interpersonal trauma and PTSD severity has direct implications for clinical assessment and treatment planning. Clinicians working with trauma survivors should routinely assess coping style using the Brief-COPE, as avoidant coping emerged as the strongest predictor of PTSD symptoms regardless of trauma type, with a notably larger effect in the interpersonal trauma group (β = .61 vs. β = .42). The small-to-medium effect sizes for group differences suggest that while interpersonal trauma confers additional risk, avoidant coping is a more proximal and modifiable target. The study supports CBT for PTSD, which directly targets avoidant coping strategies (Bourdon et al., 2019;
relevant_for: Trauma therapists, PTSD specialists, clinical psychologists conducting trauma assessments, CBT practitioners working with avoidance

title: A conceptual and empirical review of acceptance and commitment therapy for hoarding
authors: Jennifer Krafft, Clarissa W. Ong
summary: This review conceptualizes hoarding through ACT's psychological inflexibility model — as a narrowed behavioral repertoire maintained by experiential avoidance, cognitive fusion, and values disconnection. Three initial clinical trials provide preliminary evidence that ACT for hoarding is efficacious, with the intervention tailored to address insight deficits, low motivation, and executive dysfunction.
clinical_insight: Hoarding remains one of the most treatment-resistant presentations, with CBT dropout rates exceeding 50%. ACT's focus on values clarification and committed action offers a fundamentally different entry point that can sidestep the insight-motivation bottleneck. The three trials are small and preliminary; ACT for hoarding cannot yet be considered first-line. For therapists frustrated by CBT for hoarding, ACT provides a coherent alternative.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT practitioners treating hoarding disorder in adult outpatient settings

title: Intercorrelations of Paraphilic Interests in a General Population and a Pedophilic Subsample
authors: Emma Neofotistos, Susanna Karlkvist, Pelin Yilmaz, Timothy J. Luke
summary:
This study examined the intercorrelation structure of paraphilic interests in a US-representative sample (N = 1409; 50% women, 48.5% men, 1.5% nonbinary; ages 18-84, M = 46.34, SD = 15.99) recruited via Prolific. Fourteen paraphilic interests were assessed using DSM-5-aligned items (1-4 scale), along with hypersexuality via the HBI-19. Network analysis (Gaussian graphical model, GLASSO) revealed three clusters in the full sample: (1) Potentially consensual (transvesticism, masochism, asphyxiation, sadism, urophilia, fetishism), (2) Nonconsensual (exhibitionism, voyeurism, frotteurism, scatologia, pedophilia), and (3) Rare (coprophilia, necrophilia, zoophilia). Pedophilic interest was connected to other paraphilias in the full sample, contrary to predictions.

Latent class analysis identified three classes: Minimal interest (n = 1011, 71.8%), Moderate interest (n = 366, 26.0%), and High interest (n = 32, 2.3%). HBI-19 scores differed significantly across all classes (Minimal M = 9.55 vs. Moderate M = 23.3 vs. High M = 38.7, all p < .001).

In the pedophilic subsample (n = 142, those scoring 2+ on the pedophilia item; 92 men, 53 women), the network structure changed markedly: pedophilic interest became almost completely disconnected from other paraphilias, forming its own isolated cluster. Latent class analysis in this subsample yielded two classes: Primarily pedophilic (n = 109, lower HBI-19 M = 22.3) and Multi-paraphilic (n = 36, higher HBI-19 M = 40.0, p < .001). Sensitivity analyses using subsamples defined by other paraphilic interests (asphyxiation, voyeurism, zoophilia) and by hypersexuality levels did not reproduce this disconnection pattern, suggesting it is specific to pedophilic interest rather than a methodological artifact.

The network structure from a previous CSAM-user sample (Latt et al., 2026b) fit poorly in both the general population (CFI = .63, RMSEA = .133) and the pedophilic subsample (CFI = .73, RMSEA = .141), indicating meaningfully different correlation structures across populations. The authors speculate that hypersexuality may act as a third variable explaining why pedophilia correlates with other paraphilias in the general population but not in the pedophilic subsample.
clinical_insight: The finding that pedophilic interest disconnects from other paraphilias in the pedophilic subsample has important implications for clinical assessment and case formulation in sexology. In the general population, a person endorsing pedophilic interests is likely to also endorse other paraphilic interests, potentially reflecting a general hypersexuality factor. However, in clinical or forensic samples where pedophilic interest is more pronounced, this co-occurrence diminishes substantially. This suggests that clinicians should not assume that pedophilic interest implies broader paraphilic psychopathology. The three-class latent structure (minimal, moderate, high paraphilic interest) with corresponding hypersexuality levels provides a useful heuristic for triage: the High interest class (2.3% of the population) showed mean HBI-19 scores (M = 38.7) well above clinical thresholds, suggesting that comprehensive paraphilia assessment may be most efficiently targeted at individuals presenting with high hypersexuality. The consent-based clustering (consensual vs. nonconsensual) aligns with clinical distinctions between paraphilic interests and paraphilic disorders, supporting the DSM-5 framework that pathologizes only nonconsensual or distressing expressions.
relevant_for: Sexologists, forensic psychologists, clinicians assessing paraphilic interests, therapists working with hypersexuality

title: The Costs and Benefits of Mindfulness and Reappraisal in Daily Life
authors: Wenzel et al.
summary: An ecological momentary assessment study comparing mindfulness and cognitive reappraisal in daily life found that reappraisal was more effective at reducing negative affect but required more effort, while mindfulness had lower cost but weaker immediate emotion-change effects. The two strategies showed divergent profiles in their temporal dynamics and affective outcomes across daily experiences.
clinical_insight: This study provides evidence-based guidance for helping patients choose between mindfulness and reappraisal strategies in daily life rather than prescribing one uniformly. For patients with limited cognitive resources or those prone to rumination, mindfulness may be more sustainable long-term; for those needing rapid affect reduction in specific situations, reappraisal remains the stronger option. Therapists can use these differential cost-benefit profiles to support personalized emotion regulation strategy selection during skills training in CBT, DBT, or ACT.
relevant_for: CBT, DBT, and ACT therapists teaching emotion regulation skills to adults with mood and anxiety disorders

title: Sustained Attention in the Presence of a Smartphone: Does Reward Expectation Matter?
authors: Matthias Maerevoet, Mariek Vanden Abeele, Yannick Vander Zwalmen, Laura Smeets, Ernst H. W. Koster
summary:
This pre-registered lab experiment (N = 111, 70.3% female, M age = 25.29, SD = 8.58) investigated whether reward expectation drives the "brain drain" effect of smartphone presence on sustained attention. Participants were randomly assigned to three conditions (n = 37 each): (1) no smartphone present (control), (2) smartphone present face-down on silent (mere presence), (3) smartphone present with induced reward-message expectation (1/4 chance to win 40 euros via text). All completed a Sustained Attention to Response Task (SART; 336 trials, digits 1-9, withhold response to "3") before and after the manipulation.

The mere presence condition showed significantly smaller RT improvement from pre to post compared to control (Time x Condition interaction: β = 7.69, SE = 1.93, t(65948) = 3.98, p < .001), supporting the brain drain hypothesis. However, the expectation condition did not differ from control (β = 1.19, SE = 1.93, t(65948) = 0.62, p = .54), failing to support reward-based attentional capture as the mechanism. Accuracy on No-Go trials showed no significant effects across conditions.

The exploratory moderation analysis revealed that Online Vigilance (OV; measured by the 12-item OVS, M = 2.95, SD = 0.65) significantly moderated the effect. High-OV participants showed reduced RT improvements in both the mere presence condition (Time x Condition x OV: β = 32.87, SE = 2.91, t(65649) = 11.28, p < .001) and the expectation condition (β = 20.56, SE = 3.25, t(65645) = 6.32, p < .001). Adding smartphone addiction (SAS), OV, and BAS reward responsiveness as covariates did not change the pattern. The reward expectation manipulation was credible (M = 73.46/100 on a VAS), and a sensitivity analysis excluding participants below 50/100 (n = 5) yielded the same results. Data and R scripts are available on OSF.
clinical_insight: These findings are relevant to clinical work with clients presenting with attention difficulties, problematic smartphone use, or anxiety disorders where digital connectivity perpetuates hypervigilance. The key clinical takeaway is that the mere physical presence of a smartphone impairs sustained attention (effect size d derived from the RT interaction), and this effect is substantially amplified in individuals with high Online Vigilance, a trait-like cognitive orientation toward online communication. This suggests that for patients who score high on the OVS, even having their phone nearby during therapy sessions or while engaging in homework tasks (e.g., exposure exercises, mindfulness) may subtly undermine cognitive engagement. The finding that momentary reward expectation did not add to the mere presence effect implies that the attentional cost is driven by learned associations rather than acute anticipation. Clinicians working with digital wellbeing or ADHD-like presentations should consider the OVS as a screening tool and discuss phone placement strategies. The 91.9% White, predominantly female, young Belgian student sample limits generalizability.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists working with attention difficulties, digital wellbeing practitioners, therapists incorporating behavioral experiments, clinicians assessing problematic smartphone use

title: Efficacy and Working Mechanisms of a Go/No-Go Task-Based Inhibition Training in Smoking: A Randomized-Controlled Trial
authors: Franziska Motka, Charlotte Wittekind, Leonie Ascone, Simone Kühn
summary: This pre-registered RCT (N = 122) tested the efficacy of a Go/No-Go task-based inhibitory control training on tobacco use and craving in adult smokers. Participants were randomized to either substance-specific inhibition training or an active control condition. The study also investigated potential working mechanisms, examining whether changes in inhibitory control mediated reductions in smoking behavior, providing insight into whether cognitive training can serve as a viable adjunct to standard smoking cessation treatments.
clinical_insight: Inhibitory control training represents a low-cost, easily disseminable adjunct to standard smoking cessation protocols that clinicians can recommend to clients who struggle with impulse control. The RCT design and mechanism-focused analysis are methodological strengths that address the long-standing criticism that cognitive training effects are poorly understood. For therapists working with substance use disorders, these findings contribute to the growing evidence that targeting domain-general cognitive processes (inhibition) can complement domain-specific interventions (nicotine replacement, motivational interviewing). A limitation is the focus on a single substance (tobacco) and the absence of long-term follow-up data beyond the immediate training period.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with tobacco dependence and substance use disorders who are interested in evidence-based adjunctive cognitive training interventions

title: Acts and Aftermath: Differential Prediction of Moral Injury, Secondary Traumatic Stress, Burnout, and Depression in Animal Care Workers
authors: Jamie McNally
summary: In a cross-sectional survey of 291 U.S. animal care workers across six sectors, McNally examined whether 12 specific occupational exposures differentially predicted moral injury (MIOS; α = .91), secondary traumatic stress (ProQOL-5 STS; α = .86), burnout (ProQOL-5; α = .73), and depression (PHQ-9; α = .89). Total exposure dose predicted STS (r = .22), burnout (r = .13), and depression (r = .14) but not moral injury (r = .06, p = .30), demonstrating that moral injury is driven by exposure type rather than cumulative dose. Euthanasia decision-making and administration predicted STS and burnout but not moral injury, whereas handling deceased animal remains uniquely predicted moral injury total (β = .188, p = .022) and trust violations (β = .202, p = .015) after controlling for both euthanasia variables. Public threats and moral accusations was the only exposure predicting all four conditions and added significant incremental variance for moral injury (ΔR² = .039, p = .012). Clinical moral injury (17.4% of sample) was characterized by elevated interpersonal threat exposure, not by elevated animal care task exposure.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with animal care workers should assess for moral injury separately from burnout and STS rather than treating these as a single compassion fatigue construct, since each has a distinct exposure profile. Interventions targeting moral injury need to address the moral-identity threat from public accusations and the institutional betrayal inherent in how remains are handled, rather than focusing primarily on euthanasia-related distress, which appears to drive STS and burnout but not moral injury.
relevant_for: Clinicians and organizational consultants supporting animal care workers, shelter medicine professionals, and those working with moral injury in non-military populations.

title: Emotion Dysregulation in Nonsuicidal Self-Injury: Dissociations Between Global Self-Reports and Real-Time Responses to Emotional Challenge
authors: Robinson et al.
summary: This study compared young adults with past-year NSSI (n=51) and controls (n=50) using both real-time (heart rate, HRV, electrodermal activity during stress induction) and retrospective self-report measures of emotional responding. Despite the NSSI group reporting significantly greater emotion dysregulation on global questionnaires, both groups showed remarkably similar subjective and physiological reactivity and recovery during the laboratory emotional challenge. Both groups also used similar cognitive reappraisal and expressive suppression strategies, and remembered the emotional challenge similarly at two-week follow-up. These findings challenge dominant theories that NSSI is driven by amplified emotional responses.
clinical_insight: This study delivers a significant challenge to the widely held assumption that NSSI is characterised by heightened emotional reactivity. The dissociation between self-reported and real-time emotional responding suggests that people who self-injure may perceive their emotion regulation as poorer than their actual in-the-moment capacity — or that global self-report measures capture a different construct than laboratory challenges. For therapists, this has immediate implications: assessment should not rely solely on trait-level questionnaires, and interventions that target presumed emotional hypersensitivity may be misdirected. Instead, the clinical focus might better address the beliefs and narratives clients hold about their emotional lives, the meaning they ascribe to emotional experiences, and the contextual triggers that lead to self-injury despite adequate real-time regulation capacity. A limitation is the mild and sporadic NSSI in the sample, which may not generalise to clinical populations with severe, frequent self-injury.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing and treating nonsuicidal self-injury in adult mental health settings

title: Parenting and children's ADHD symptoms: A longitudinal twin-difference study
authors: Sophie von Stumm
summary: ADHD symptoms in children are often attributed to the parenting that they experience, despite strong evidence for genetic influences. Here, we tested whether associations between parenting and children’s ADHD symptoms reflected shared family-level confounding. At the population level, random-intercept cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPM) suggested significant bidirectional processes between children’s ADHD symptoms and their experiences of harsh discipline, constructive parenting, and negative parent-child feelings across the ages 8, 12, and 14 years (N = 7,429, including 2,762 monozygotic twin
clinical_insight: Twin-difference designs that isolate environmental parenting effects from genetic transmission are valuable for clinicians working with families of children with ADHD, as they help identify which parenting strategies are most likely to produce behavioral change independently of genetic risk.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with ADHD families, child psychologists, parenting intervention researchers

title: Generative AI-Enabled Therapy Support Tool Improves Clinical Outcomes and Patient Engagement in NHS Talking Therapies
authors: Habicht et al.
summary: This real-world observational study (N=137) tested whether a generative AI-powered personalised therapy support tool deployed between CBT sessions could improve treatment outcomes in UK NHS Talking Therapies services. Patients using the AI tool (n=93) showed greater session attendance, fewer dropouts, and higher rates of reliable improvement, recovery, and reliable recovery compared to standard CBT worksheet controls (n=44). Within the intervention group, greater usage of the AI tool predicted more sessions attended and fewer dropouts. The study suggests that AI-enabled between-session support can meaningfully enhance CBT engagement and outcomes.
clinical_insight: Between-session homework non-compliance is one of the most persistent barriers to successful CBT. This study provides early but compelling evidence that AI-powered personalisation of between-session materials can substantially improve engagement and clinical outcomes. For private practice clinicians, this signals a near-future where AI tools could handle the 'homework gap' — personalising psychoeducation, behavioural experiments, and thought records to each client's specific presentation and progress. A critical caveat is the observational design: without randomisation, the improvement may partly reflect patient motivation rather than tool efficacy. The ethical implications of AI in therapeutic spaces also warrant careful consideration, particularly around data privacy and the therapeutic relationship.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners and clinic directors interested in technology-enhanced therapy delivery for depression and anxiety

title: Development of an Abbreviated Form of the Extended Strengths and Weaknesses Assessment of Normal Behavior for Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (E-SWAN DMDD)
authors: Juliette B. H. Brook, Isabella C. Stallworthy, Golia Shafiei, Joëlle Bagautdinova, S. Parker Singleton, Brooke L. Sevchik, Giovanni A. Salum, Michael P. Milham, Theodore D. Satterthwaite, Tyler M. Moore
summary: Using data from 3,835 youth in the Healthy Brain Network (mean age = 10.25 years; 63% male), the authors applied the Graded Response Model and computerized adaptive testing (CAT) simulations to reduce the 30-item E-SWAN DMDD questionnaire to a 4-item short form. The short form correlated highly with the full form (Levy-corrected r = 0.776) and showed moderate convergent validity with the Affective Reactivity Index (ρ = 0.231) and CBCL/TRF Externalizing domains (ρ = 0.345–0.394). Discriminant validity evidence was strong: the short form had a significantly weaker association with the general psychopathology p-factor than the full form (ρ = 0.424 vs. 0.530, p < .001), suggesting greater specificity for DMDD. Convergent validity with the ARI was replicated in an independent University of Pennsylvania sample (ρ = 0.307, p < .001).
clinical_insight: The 4-item E-SWAN DMDD short form offers a practical, brief self-report screener for DMDD-specific irritability that reduces response burden in children and adolescents without sacrificing construct validity. Its stronger specificity for DMDD over general psychopathology makes it particularly useful for distinguishing DMDD from comorbid conditions in triage and screening settings.
relevant_for: Clinicians and researchers assessing irritability and mood dysregulation in youth ages 6–18.

title: Obsessive-Compulsive Symptoms and Sound Sensitivities: Comparing Misophonia and Hyperacusis
authors: Castro et al.
summary: This multi-trait, multi-method study examined the relationship between obsessive-compulsive symptoms and two types of sound sensitivity — misophonia and hyperacusis — in a large online sample (N=1,094) with a subset completing in-person audiological and psychological assessments (N=102). Results showed that O-C symptoms were associated with both misophonia and hyperacusis, and that this association was consistent regardless of measurement method (questionnaire, clinician rating, or laboratory assessment). Depression was less strongly associated with sound sensitivities than O-C symptoms, suggesting the link is not an artifact of general distress. The findings indicate that sensory sensitivities broadly, rather than misophonia specifically, are linked to O-C symptoms.
clinical_insight: Patients with OCD frequently report sound sensitivities, yet the nature of this relationship has been unclear. This study clarifies that the O-C/sound sensitivity link is robust across measurement methods and extends beyond misophonia to hyperacusis. For clinicians, this means that assessing for sound sensitivities in OCD patients is warranted as part of a comprehensive formulation — these sensory issues may maintain compulsive behaviours (e.g., avoidance of triggering environments, mental rituals to neutralise distress). The finding that general distress does not fully account for the association pushes back against the tendency to dismiss sensory complaints as non-specific anxiety symptoms. A limitation is the university student sample, which may not represent clinical OCD populations with greater symptom severity.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing and treating OCD who encounter comorbid sound sensitivity complaints

title: Securing Adolescent Mental Health by Training Need Crafting: A Randomized Controlled Trial with School, Leisure, and Social Media Outcomes
authors: Marlies Van de Casteele, Bart Soenens, Daphne Van den Bogaard, Maarten Vansteenkiste
summary: In this pre-registered RCT, 299 adolescents (M age = 16.34; 55% female) were randomly assigned to a six-session online need crafting intervention (based on Self-Determination Theory) or a passive control, with assessments at baseline, post-intervention, and two-week follow-up. The intervention improved general need crafting and need satisfaction, with effects maintained at follow-up; however, effects were driven almost entirely by the 58% of participants classified as highly engaged. Serial mediation in the high-engagement group confirmed significant indirect effects on well-being via need crafting → need satisfaction (β = .22, SE = .06, p < .001) and on ill-being via need crafting → decreased need frustration (β = −.08, SE = .03, p = .003). Domain-specific effects replicated in school (β = .19, p = .009) and leisure (β = .19, p = .015) but were absent for social media use.
clinical_insight: Need crafting—the proactive skill of seeking out need-fulfilling experiences—can be trained in adolescents and operates through a clear SDT-consistent mechanism. However, engagement is critical: low-engaged participants showed no benefits and in some domain-specific outcomes fared worse than controls, suggesting that passive or minimal exposure may be counterproductive without active participation.
relevant_for: Adolescent mental health clinicians, school counselors, and prevention program developers working with youth aged 15–18.

title: Cognitive Dependencies and Psychological Health Correlates of Coherence in Autobiographical Reasoning
authors: Kapoor et al.
summary: This study (N=272, retest N=189) developed and validated a German version of the Awareness of Narrative Identity Questionnaire (ANIQ) measuring coherence in autobiographical reasoning. Coherence was independent of intelligence and verbal memory (partially dependent on figural memory), and was significantly associated with depression, positivity, self-esteem, and self-esteem stability—but not anxiety. The findings establish autobiographical coherence as a distinct psychological construct with salutogenic effects, particularly for mood-related outcomes.
clinical_insight: For therapists using narrative therapy, schema therapy, or identity-focused interventions, this study validates that helping clients build a coherent life narrative directly supports self-esteem and reduces depression—independent of cognitive ability. The dissociation from anxiety is clinically important: narrative coherence work may be more effective for depressive presentations than for anxiety disorders. The ANIQ offers a potential outcome measure for tracking progress in therapies targeting identity and self-concept. A limitation is the exclusively German sample and cross-sectional design, which cannot establish causation—depression may reduce narrative coherence rather than vice versa.
relevant_for: Clinicians using narrative therapy, schema therapy, or identity-based interventions with adults experiencing depression and low self-worth.

title: Can fluoxetine's diminished efficacy in pediatric depression be explained by study sites, baseline severity, age, and psychological interventions? A secondary exploratory meta-regression.
authors: Martin Plöderl, Richard Lyus, Florian Naudet
summary: This secondary exploratory meta-regression analyzed 12 placebo-controlled fluoxetine trials in pediatric depression (N = 2,788; 1,325 fluoxetine, 1,463 placebo), investigating whether number of study sites (NOSS), participant age, baseline severity, or added psychological interventions explain the drug's declining efficacy estimates. NOSS increased over time but was not linearly associated with outcomes; log-transformed NOSS showed associations with decreased efficacy (B = 1.57, 95% CI 0.24–2.90, p = .02) and greater placebo response (B = −1.90, 95% CI −3.41 to −0.40, p = .01), but these were driven entirely by the outlying single-center Emslie et al. (1997) trial (Cook's distance = 3.92) and did not survive Benjamini–Hochberg adjustment. Age, baseline severity, and psychological interventions showed no significant associations with efficacy or placebo response.
clinical_insight: Fluoxetine's diminished meta-analytic efficacy in pediatric depression cannot be attributed to changes in trial design characteristics such as multi-site recruitment, participant age, symptom severity, or concurrent psychotherapy. Clinicians should be aware that the evidence base for fluoxetine as first-line pharmacotherapy in youth depression is weaker than historically assumed, and the decline likely reflects sponsorship bias and publication issues rather than methodological artifacts of modern trials.
relevant_for: Child and adolescent psychiatrists, psychopharmacologists, and researchers interpreting antidepressant trial evidence.

title: Emotion Regulation and Psychopathology: Investigating Differential Associations Between Emotion Regulation Skills and Psychological Symptoms Using a Network Approach
authors: Trautmann et al.
summary: Using routine clinical data from 716 psychotherapy outpatients, this network analysis examined differential associations between nine emotion regulation skills and nine symptom dimensions. Bridge expected influence analysis revealed that only three ER skills were most directly connected to symptom severity: Tolerance, Confrontation, and Modification. The strongest direct edges were Modification-Depression, Confrontation-Obsession-Compulsion, and Tolerance-Interpersonal Sensitivity.
clinical_insight: This study addresses a longstanding clinical question: which ER skills actually matter for which symptoms? The finding that Tolerance, Confrontation, and Modification serve as the primary bridges between ER deficits and psychopathology has direct treatment implications. For depression, targeting cognitive modification may be most impactful; for OCD, confrontational approaches to unwanted thoughts may be key; for interpersonal sensitivity, distress tolerance is paramount. This supports transdiagnostic protocols like the Unified Protocol while suggesting that personalizing which skills receive the most emphasis could improve outcomes. A limitation is the cross-sectional network, which cannot establish directionality.
relevant_for: Clinicians delivering transdiagnostic treatments (UP, ACT, DBT skills) who need to prioritize which emotion regulation skills to target based on presenting symptoms.

title: Prediction and prediction error in autism: A meta-analysis of functional magnetic resonance imaging results
authors: Hjalmar Nobel Norrman, Yating Huang, Annelies van't Westeinde, Tessa M. van Leeuwen, Peter Fransson, Sven Bölte, Janina Neufeld
summary: This ALE meta-analysis aggregated fMRI findings (35 studies; 261 ASC, 289 NT participants) examining prediction and prediction error processing in autism spectrum condition. NT > ASC activation differences converged in a single 744 mm³ cluster in the medial frontal gyrus/cingulate gyrus (BA 9/32), overlapping the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex, a region implicated in error monitoring and uncertainty estimation. ASC > NT contrasts (8 experiments, 139 ASC, 150 NT) showed no significant convergence. Notably, 58.73% of all extracted experiments were null results, and a Fail-Safe N analysis indicated low robustness of the sole significant cluster, suggesting the finding should be interpreted cautiously.
clinical_insight: The reduced dACC response to prediction violation in autism may reflect attenuated differentiation between expected and unexpected outcomes, which could inform interventions targeting adaptive learning and cognitive flexibility. However, the high null rate means these neural findings are not yet ready to guide individualized clinical strategies—computational modeling studies are needed first to specify underlying mechanisms.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and researchers studying predictive processing in autism

title: Broaden-and-Build Cycles of Attachment Security in Therapy: Antecedents, Consequences, and Trajectory of Therapeutic Exploration
authors: Cherniak
summary: This study introduced the Client Therapeutic Exploration scales (CTE-4), a new measure of self-disclosure grounded in Bowlby attachment theory, scored on depth, emotional involvement, freedom, and spontaneity. Video-recorded therapy sessions from 36 patients across five time points were analyzed. Higher attachment anxiety consistently predicted less therapeutic exploration across all CTE-4 dimensions, while attachment avoidance was not significantly associated.
clinical_insight: The finding that attachment anxiety, but not avoidance, consistently constrains therapeutic exploration has nuanced implications for case formulation. Anxiously attached clients may need more explicit permission and safety-building before engaging in the exploratory work that drives change. The CTE-4 offers therapists a framework for monitoring the quality of client participation beyond session attendance. The tension between the theoretical model and mixed empirical findings highlights that therapeutic exploration is necessary but not sufficient for change. A gap is the small sample and the exploratory nature of the analysis.
relevant_for: Psychodynamic, attachment-based, and relational therapists seeking to understand how client attachment patterns shape the therapeutic process and depth of self-disclosure.

title: Coordinated Psychological Intervention Ensembles Outperform Single-Mechanism Designs Across Societal Challenges
authors: Anandita C. Sabherwal, Danielle Goldwert, Madalina Vlasceanu, Elke U. Weber
summary: Across two preregistered studies (N = 8,488 and N = 2,547 US adults), the authors tested whether "intervention ensembles"—coordinated combinations of distinct psychological modules—outperform single-mechanism interventions in shaping behavior and attitudes. Three design principles were evaluated: outcome-optimizing, mechanism-coverage, and multi-level synergy ensembles. In Study 1 (climate policy support and real donations), the strongest multi-level synergy ensemble increased policy support by 41.5% and donations by 43.0% relative to an informed control. In Study 2, an ensemble constructed from top-performing anti-animosity interventions significantly outperformed its individual components in reducing partisan animosity and strengthening democratic attitudes. LLM-assisted analysis of open-ended responses revealed that effective ensembles resolve complementary psychological barriers rather than simply providing more arguments.
clinical_insight: The ensemble approach—strategically combining interventions that target different psychological mechanisms (individual, collective, and systemic levels)—offers a transferable design framework for clinicians constructing multi-component treatment packages. Rather than seeking the single most effective technique, clinicians may achieve larger effects by identifying and addressing the specific constellation of barriers each patient faces through coordinated intervention modules.
relevant_for: Behavioral intervention designers and clinical researchers developing multi-component treatments

title: Anxiety as a Disorder of Uncertainty: Implications for Understanding Maladaptive Anxiety, Anxious Avoidance, and Exposure Therapy
authors: Brown et al.
summary: This paper integrates neurocomputational learning models with the clinical exposure therapy literature to propose that anxiety disorders are fundamentally disorders of uncertainty learning rather than exaggerated threat expectancies. The framework reconceptualizes exposure therapy as remediating maladaptive avoidance from dysfunctional explore/exploit decisions in uncertain situations, reconciling several inconsistencies in the literature including why exposure sometimes fails and why patients drop out.
clinical_insight: For clinicians delivering exposure-based treatments, this framework offers a conceptual shift: exposure works by restoring adaptive explore/exploit balance under uncertainty. This reframes common clinical challenges such as dropout, non-response, and habituation failure as predictable consequences of the patient uncertainty-learning architecture. It supports approaches that explicitly foreground uncertainty tolerance and suggests that exposure may be more effective when the explore/exploit framing is made explicit. A limitation is the absence of empirical data directly testing the framework.
relevant_for: Clinicians specializing in anxiety disorders and exposure therapy (CBT, ACT) who want a deeper theoretical understanding of why exposure works and when it fails.

title: Psychotic-like experiences and neurobehavioral reward processing in adolescents who use cannabis
authors: Shelley A. Gresko, Elizabeth C. Thompson, Meghan A. Gonsalves, Gillian LeBlanc, Elizabeth Lorenc, Jane Metrik, Michael Frank, Sarah Ryan, Emily Olenik, Leslie Brick, Anthony Spirito, Jodi Gilman, Sarah A. Thomas
summary: This cross-sectional observational study (N = 81 adolescents aged 14–17; 34 cannabis users, 47 controls) examined the interrelation of cannabis use (CU), psychotic-like experiences (PLEs), and dopamine-related neurobehavioral markers. A significant CU × PLEs interaction predicted reduced reward motivation (OR = 2.41, 95% CI 1.10–5.27, p = .028), such that cannabis-using adolescents with high PLEs showed less willingness to exert effort for rewards at low-to-medium effort levels. CU quantity and PLEs were positively associated at high levels of subcortical 1/nT2* (a tissue iron MRI marker linked to dopamine neurophysiology): daily concentrate hits interacted with 1/nT2* in the left putamen (β = 51.58, p = .01) and right thalamus (β = 70.93, p = .02), and daily cannabis grams in the left putamen (β = 73.34, p = .02), right putamen (β = 101.69, p = .001), and right thalamus (β = 102.38, p = .01).
clinical_insight: Adolescent cannabis users presenting with PLEs may exhibit distinct reward processing deficits—reduced motivation and less strategic effort allocation—that could serve as early behavioral markers of psychosis-spectrum risk. Screening for motivational changes alongside PLEs in adolescent cannabis users may help identify those at elevated risk who could benefit from early intervention targeting reward system dysfunction.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with adolescents using cannabis, especially those presenting with subclinical psychotic symptoms

title: Burst Versus Continuous Delivery Design in Digital Mental Health Interventions: Evidence from a Randomized Clinical Trial
authors: Marciniak et al.
summary: This RCT (N=93) compared burst delivery (five daily prompts in weeks 1 and 3 only) versus continuous delivery (five daily prompts throughout all three weeks) of ReApp, a mobile app promoting positive cognitive reappraisal. No significant differences emerged in adherence, depressive symptoms, anxiety symptoms, perceived stress, or perceived helpfulness. The burst delivery group showed significantly steeper decline in perceived difficulty over time. Both groups improved equivalently on clinical outcomes.
clinical_insight: This finding has practical significance for digital therapeutics design: delivering interventions in concentrated bursts with built-in breaks is as effective as continuous delivery while reducing perceived burden. For clinicians recommending digital adjuncts or step-down tools, this supports a less demanding approach that may improve long-term engagement and real-world adherence. The study specifically tested cognitive reappraisal, so generalizability to other therapeutic mechanisms remains uncertain. A gap is the small sample and the absence of follow-up data beyond the three-week intervention period.
relevant_for: Clinicians incorporating digital mental health tools and app-based interventions into their practice, particularly as adjuncts between sessions.

title: Emotional and physical abuse have dissociable impacts on punishment learning and avoidance behavior
authors: Holly Sullivan-Toole, Jeremy Haynes, John McClellan France, Suzanne C. Perkins, Monica Luciana et al. (7 authors)
summary: Childhood adversity is a robust risk factor for psychopathology, and growing evidence implicates developmental alterations in reward and punishment learning as potent mechanisms shaping this vulnerability. However, little is known about how specific types of adversity impact different learning processes, particularly during the sensitive adolescent period. To examine effects of different types of adversity on the development of valenced learning processes, we combined a subtype-specific approach to childhood adversity, a behavioral task and reinforcement learning (RL) model that capture distin

title: Agency in Post-traumatic Stress Disorder: Implications for Therapy
authors: Adrien et al.
summary: This paper proposes an enactive framework for understanding agency deficits in PTSD, linking peritraumatic dissociation to disrupted sense of control. The authors provide a roadmap for agency-based therapies and discuss how sensorimotor technologies such as gesture sonification could help restore agency during trauma recovery.
clinical_insight: The enactive perspective offers clinicians a principled way to understand why traditional exposure alone may fail when patients lack foundational agency. Sensorimotor approaches could complement phase-based trauma treatments, particularly for dissociative PTSD presentations. However, the framework remains theoretical and awaits empirical validation.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists working with dissociative PTSD and complex trauma seeking somatic adjuncts to phase-based treatment

title: Bringing together health and education with lived experience to co-produce a new eating disorder screening tool
authors: Lucy H. Eddy, Cara E Staniforth, Maryam I. Shuaib, Nell Schofield, Nat K. Merrick et al. (7 authors)
summary: Background: Research has shown high levels of under-diagnosis of Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD). National and international research has shown that pathways for assessment and support for DCD are convoluted, difficult to navigate and are ultimately not fit for purpose.
