authors: Danielle Cosme; Chelsea Helion; Yoona Kang; David M. Lydon-Staley; Bruce P. Doré; Ovidia Stanoi; Jeesung Ahn; Mia Jovanova; Amanda L. McGowan; Zachary M. Boyd
summary:
This proof-of-concept study combined functional neuroimaging and machine learning to develop a brain-based predictive model (neural signature) of mindful distancing, then used it to evaluate a smartphone-based mindful distancing intervention for reducing alcohol consumption among college students. The study involved 240 participants who completed an alcohol cue reactivity task during fMRI scanning followed by a 28-day experience-sampling intervention using a within-person crossover design (alternating active and control weeks).

In the laboratory, mindful distancing significantly reduced subjective craving for alcohol compared to natural reactions. This effect was moderated by between-person expression of the neural signature: individuals who more strongly expressed the mindful distancing signature showed larger craving reductions (β = -0.35, 95% CrI [-0.70, 0.02]). Notably, the neural signature was not correlated with self-reported confidence or trait mindfulness, indicating it captures distinct and complementary information about emotion regulation ability.

In daily life, multilevel sequential mediation modeling revealed two distinct pathways through which the intervention reduced alcohol consumption. First, a sequential indirect effect: the intervention increased mindful responses to alcohol (a1 path; β = 0.48, 95% CrI [0.24, 0.72]), which reduced craving (b1 path; β = -0.30, 95% CrI [-0.48, -0.11]), which in turn reduced consumption (b2 path; b = 0.64, 95% CrI [0.38, 0.90]). The sequential indirect effect was significant (a1*b1*b2 = -0.09, 95% CrI [-0.19, -0.02]). Second, a direct pathway: mindful responses to alcohol were directly associated with lower consumption (b3 path; b = -0.53, 95% CrI [-0.85, -0.19]), with an indirect effect through this path alone (a1*b3 = -0.24, 95% CrI [-0.48, -0.06]).

Critically, individual differences in neural signature expression predicted intervention effectiveness: participants with stronger expression of the mindful distancing signature showed stronger intervention-related increases in mindful responses (interaction β = 0.54, 95% CrI [0.30, 0.77]). This moderation effect was specific to the neural signature—only signature expression (not subjective confidence or trait mindfulness) moderated intervention-related increases in mindful responses.
clinical_insight:
This study has direct implications for clinicians working with substance use, particularly those incorporating mindfulness-based approaches into treatment. The finding that mindful distancing reduces alcohol consumption through two distinct pathways—a craving-mediated pathway and a direct pathway—suggests that mindfulness-based interventions may work through multiple mechanisms simultaneously, which has implications for treatment planning and psychoeducation. Clinicians can explain to patients that mindfulness is not merely about reducing cravings (though it does that too) but also about creating psychological space that enables more intentional, goal-oriented choices around alcohol, even when cravings are not prominently experienced.

The neural signature approach demonstrated here is particularly noteworthy for clinical practice. The study showed that the neural signature of mindful distancing was uncorrelated with both self-reported confidence in using the strategy and trait mindfulness, yet it was the only measure that predicted who would benefit most from the intervention. This finding suggests that clinicians should not rely solely on patients' self-reported mindfulness or confidence when assessing readiness for mindfulness-based interventions. While neuroimaging-based assessment is not currently feasible in routine clinical practice, this proof-of-concept supports the broader principle that individual differences in emotion regulation ability—particularly in how effectively a person can implement specific strategies like distancing or decentering—may be more important than global trait measures for predicting treatment outcomes.

The study focused on college students without alcohol use disorders, which limits direct generalizability to clinical populations. However, the results are consistent with prior evidence from clinical samples and extend the findings by identifying specific mechanisms in a preventative context. For clinicians working with patients who have alcohol use disorders, these findings support the use of mindful distancing or decentering techniques as part of a broader treatment approach, while highlighting that the strategy may be most effective for individuals who can genuinely engage in psychological distancing rather than merely reporting that they will try.
relevant_for: Clinicians incorporating mindfulness-based approaches into substance use treatment; therapists working with emerging adults around alcohol use; researchers interested in translational neuroscience approaches to evaluating psychotherapy mechanisms; clinicians seeking to understand individual differences in response to emotion regulation-focused interventions.

title: Virtual Reality Gaming as a Non-Pharmacological Intervention for Pain Management During Medical Procedures: A Systematic Review
authors: Marie Szabelski; Matthieu Burtin; Matthieu Casteran
summary:
This systematic review evaluated the effectiveness and safety of virtual reality (VR) gaming as a non-pharmacological intervention for pain and anxiety management during medical procedures. The review searched four databases (PubMed, Web of Science, PsycINFO, Embase) covering publications from 2000 to November 2025, identifying 48 relevant studies including 44 randomized controlled trials. Studies were categorized by procedure type (surgery, dressing changes, venipuncture, dental, physical therapy, and other medical procedures), participant age (pediatric vs. adult), and comparison group (standard care, passive VR, other distraction methods).

VR gaming showed significant pain reduction across multiple procedure types, with effectiveness varying by several key factors. Content designed specifically for pain management was substantially more effective than commercial or non-specific VR content: 68% of studies using purpose-built VR content reported significant pain reduction, compared to only 31% using commercial games. Active VR (interactive games) outperformed passive VR (360° videos or relaxation content), with active VR groups showing pain scores around 30/100 on VAS compared to approximately 50/100 for passive VR and control groups. The intensity of the procedure moderated effectiveness: more painful procedures showed greater benefit from VR gaming, suggesting that VR is most valuable when pain is severe.

Children were more responsive than adults, with approximately 80% of pediatric studies showing significant results when active games designed for pain management were used. However, this age difference may also reflect the types of procedures studied. VR gaming was generally superior to passive VR and standard care, though comparisons with tablet-based mobile games showed more mixed results, suggesting that the immersive quality of the headset itself contributes to analgesia but that content engagement is equally important.

Safety data indicated VR gaming was well-tolerated across all studies, with minimal adverse effects reported. The majority of studies did not report significant reduction in drug consumption, though this was often not the primary outcome measure. The review concluded that VR gaming represents a promising, safe, and accessible non-pharmacological option for procedural pain management, particularly when purpose-built content is used during painful procedures.
clinical_insight:
This review provides actionable guidance for clinicians working in medical settings where patients undergo painful procedures. For clinical psychologists and health psychologists working in hospital or perioperative settings, VR gaming represents a practical, low-cost intervention that can be recommended as an adjunct to standard care for procedural pain and anxiety. The key finding that purpose-built VR content significantly outperforms commercial games means that clinicians should advocate for the use of specifically designed medical VR applications rather than relying on off-the-shelf gaming content. This distinction is important because hospitals may be tempted to use commercially available VR games due to lower costs, but this review demonstrates that doing so substantially reduces the analgesic benefit.

The finding that VR is most effective for more painful procedures has clear clinical implications: clinicians should prioritize VR for patients undergoing known high-pain procedures such as burn dressing changes, rather than for minor procedures where the benefit may be minimal. The engagement factor identified in the review—that the degree of patient engagement with the VR content predicted pain reduction more than game realism or fun—suggests that clinicians should attend to whether patients are genuinely absorbed in the VR experience rather than simply providing any VR content.

For therapists working with chronic pain patients, the finding that active, engaging VR produces analgesia through immersive distraction is relevant to understanding non-pharmacological pain management mechanisms. While the review focused on acute procedural pain, the principles identified (active engagement, immersive content, distraction-based analgesia) may inform the use of VR in chronic pain management as well. Clinicians should also note that VR was rarely sufficient to eliminate opioid use, positioning it as a complementary rather than replacement strategy for pharmacological analgesia.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists and health psychologists in medical settings; pain management specialists; perioperative care teams; clinicians working with burn patients or patients undergoing repeated painful procedures; therapists interested in technology-assisted non-pharmacological interventions.

title: Maternal Stress and Mother-Child Interaction Dynamics: The Roles of Child Age and Maternal Emotion Regulation
authors: Julia Folz; Trinh Nguyen; Stefanie Hoehl; Vanessa van Ast; Milica Nikolic
summary:
This study examined how acute stress induction in 62 mothers of young children (aged 6-38 months) shaped dyadic interaction quality, measured through facial affect and cardiovascular activity (inter-beat intervals and respiratory sinus arrhythmia). Using a pre-post stress design, mother-child dyads engaged in two-minute face-to-face interactions before and after a standardized stress induction (the Trier Social Stress Test adapted for mothers). The study investigated how stress-induced changes in interaction quality varied as a function of both child age and maternal self-reported emotion regulation difficulties.

Results revealed a significant interaction between stress condition and child age. In interactions with younger children (6-18 months), both mothers and children showed less positive facial expressions and reduced synchrony in their facial expressions post-stress compared to pre-stress. In contrast, older children (19-38 months) showed stronger physiological arousal post-stress, evidenced by decreased inter-beat intervals and reduced respiratory sinus arrhythmia, but paradoxically displayed more positive facial expressions post-stress. This pattern suggests that older infants may attempt to compensate for maternal stress by increasing positive engagement, despite heightened physiological arousal.

Maternal emotion regulation difficulties moderated stress-induced changes in dyadic interaction. Mothers with higher self-reported emotion regulation difficulties expressed more negative affect post-stress, and their heart rates were less synchronized with their children's heart rates compared to pre-stress interactions. This finding indicates that maternal emotion regulation capacity serves as a buffer against stress contagion in the mother-child dyad. The study measured multiple indicators of dyadic synchrony—facial affect coordination, inter-beat interval synchrony, and RSA synchrony—providing a multimodal picture of how stress disrupts the co-regulatory processes that normally characterize healthy mother-child interactions.
clinical_insight:
This study is directly relevant for clinicians working in perinatal mental health, parent-infant therapy, and family therapy. The finding that maternal emotion regulation difficulties predict disrupted mother-child physiological synchrony and increased maternal negative affect under stress provides empirical support for a key assumption in parent-infant psychotherapy: that the caregiver's capacity to regulate their own emotional responses is foundational to maintaining healthy co-regulation with their infant. When mothers struggle with emotion regulation, the stress contagion effects become more pronounced, potentially creating a negative cycle that affects the developing attachment relationship.

Clinicians conducting parent-infant interventions should note the age-dependent patterns identified. The compensatory positive facial expressions observed in older infants (19-38 months) suggest that by the second year of life, children may already be attempting to regulate the dyadic interaction when their mother is stressed. However, this behavioral compensation co-occurs with heightened physiological arousal, indicating that the child is experiencing physiological stress despite appearing behaviorally positive. This discrepancy between behavioral and physiological responses in older infants means that clinicians should not rely solely on observed behavior when assessing how a child is responding to parental stress in a therapy session. A child who appears engaged and positive may actually be physiologically distressed.

For therapists working with mothers experiencing postpartum depression, anxiety, or high stress, this study provides concrete evidence that targeting maternal emotion regulation—rather than solely focusing on parenting skills or child behavior—may be a critical lever for improving mother-child interaction quality. The multimodal assessment approach (combining facial affect with autonomic measures) also offers a model for how clinicians might evaluate the effectiveness of parent-infant interventions, by looking beyond behavioral observation to consider the physiological co-regulation between parent and child.
relevant_for: Perinatal mental health clinicians; parent-infant therapists; family therapists working with mothers of young children; researchers studying maternal stress and emotion regulation; clinicians assessing parent-child attachment and dyadic regulation.

title: Co-constructed Emotion: A Collective Predictive Coding Theory of Interoception and Social Symbol Sharing
authors: Takato Horii; Chie Hieida; Yuri Terasawa; Hideki Ohira; Tadahiro Taniguchi
summary:
This theoretical paper proposes the Theory of Co-constructed Emotion (TCE), a computational framework that bridges the gap between biological and social constructionist accounts of emotion by integrating interoception into the predictive coding paradigm at a collective level. The authors argue that traditional psychological constructivism has remained confined to individual brain processes, while social constructivism has lacked a bottom-up computational mechanism explaining how language and culture emerge from bodily emotional substrates and dynamically evolve. The TCE addresses this by proposing that emotions are co-constructed through shared interoceptive predictions that are dynamically updated through social interaction and linguistic communication.

The theory extends the conventional predictive coding model by incorporating interoception, which is often marginalized in cognitive neuroscience approaches to emotion. In the TCE framework, individuals generate predictions about their own interoceptive states (internal bodily sensations), and these predictions are continuously updated through social interaction. Critically, the theory proposes that shared linguistic symbols (words like "anxiety," "anger," or "loneliness") compress heterogeneous bodily states into communicable categories, enabling collective prediction sharing across individuals. This process creates a shared emotional vocabulary that both enables and constrains emotional experience.

The paper provides a formal computational model showing how social symbol sharing emerges from individual-level interoceptive processing. The model predicts that the degree of shared emotional understanding within a group depends on both the similarity of interoceptive experiences and the richness of the shared linguistic repertoire. The authors apply their framework to several clinical phenomena, including redefining alexithymia computationally as a failure of interoceptive prediction that impairs emotional symbol sharing, and explaining how the compressive nature of emotional language can contribute to miscommunication in psychotherapy.
clinical_insight:
This theoretical framework has significant implications for clinical practice, particularly in understanding why emotional communication between therapists and clients can break down and how therapeutic language functions as a tool for emotional co-construction. The TCE provides a computational account of alexithymia that goes beyond the standard description of difficulty identifying and describing feelings. By framing alexithymia as a failure of interoceptive prediction that impairs emotional symbol sharing, the theory suggests that alexithymic patients may not simply lack awareness of their emotions but rather fail to map their bodily states onto shared linguistic categories. This reframing has direct implications for assessment and treatment: clinicians working with alexithymic patients might focus on helping patients develop more granular interoceptive awareness and vocabulary, rather than assuming the problem is simply a lack of emotional insight.

The theory also helps explain why certain therapeutic interventions work. For example, the practice of asking patients to rate and label their emotions in session can be understood not merely as a monitoring exercise but as a process of engaging the interoceptive prediction mechanism and updating the mapping between bodily states and emotional symbols. Similarly, group therapy may be effective partly because it provides a rich context for social symbol sharing, enabling patients to refine their emotional vocabulary through interaction with others who have similar interoceptive experiences.

For psychodynamic therapists, the TCE offers a computational formalization of concepts like projective identification and containment that have long been part of psychodynamic theory but have lacked a mechanistic grounding. The idea that emotional states are co-constructed through shared predictions between individuals maps onto the psychodynamic understanding of the therapeutic relationship as a space where new emotional meanings are jointly created. For CBT practitioners, the framework helps explain why cognitive reappraisal works: by changing the top-down predictions about the meaning of interoceptive states, reappraisal effectively alters the emotional experience itself.
relevant_for: Psychotherapists across modalities interested in the mechanisms of emotional communication in therapy; clinicians working with alexithymic patients; researchers studying the computational basis of emotion; therapists interested in the intersection of interoception, language, and social interaction in clinical contexts.

title: The effectiveness of a therapeutically applied Dungeons and Dragons group intervention in a community mental health setting - Protocol for a randomised controlled trial.
authors: Terry Rae, Matthew Modini, Poppy Watson
summary: This protocol paper describes a two-group randomised controlled trial (RCT) evaluating a CBT-infused, therapeutically applied role-playing game (TA-RPG) intervention using Dungeons and Dragons (D&D) for individuals with complex mental health needs at a community mental health centre in Australia. The 8-week group program embeds cognitive behavioural therapy techniques within the D&D gameplay structure, targeting anxiety and depressive symptomatology. An active control group will play standard board games for a comparable duration. Outcome measures are collected pre- and post-intervention for both groups, with the intervention group also completing 12-month follow-up assessments. The trial is registered on the Australian New
clinical_insight: TA-RPGs represent a novel therapeutic approach that harnesses the inherent structure of tabletop role-playing games to deliver psychotherapeutic content in an engaging, low-stigma format. The D&D framework naturally provides opportunities for social skills practice, behavioural activation, cognitive restructuring, exposure, and interpersonal learning within a group setting. For community mental health populations with complex needs who may be disengaged from traditional therapy, game-based interventions can improve attendance and therapeutic alliance. The use of an active control (board games) strengthens the design compared to earlier waitlist-controlled trials, allowing researchers to isolate the specific therapeutic ingredients of the TA-RPG approach beyond nonspecific group factors. If efficacy is demonstrated, this protocol could provide a template for integrating TA-RPGs into existing community mental health group programs, potentially improving treatment engagement for hard-to-reach populations.
relevant_for: Clinicians working in community mental health settings seeking innovative group-based interventions, therapists interested in gamification of CBT techniques, and researchers investigating therapeutic applications of role-playing games for anxiety and depression.

title: Self-harm in autistic and non-autistic people: network structure and prediction of suicidality over time
authors: Rachel Moseley, Ido Shalev, Carrie Allison, Simon Baron-Cohen, Sarah Cassidy, Nicola Gregory, Victoria Newell, Mirabel Pelton, Tanya Procyshyn, Elizabeth Weir
summary: This study examined the network structure of non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) behaviours and their relationships to suicidal thoughts and behaviour (STB) in 327 autistic and 260 non-autistic adults pooled from two online surveys. Using cross-sectional network analysis, the authors identified three distinct NSSI clusters in autistic people compared to one cluster in non-autistic people, revealing fundamentally different patterns of self-harm co-occurrence. In both groups, some NSSI behaviours were only indirectly linked to STB via other NSSI behaviours. Cutting and ingesting dangerous substances or sharp objects emerged as most strongly related to suicide attempts. In longitudinal analyses of autistic participants across two time-points one year apart (N=168), LASSO regression showed that self-biting, breaking bones, and fighting to get hurt remained significant predictors of STB-related outcomes after controlling for baseline STB, while cutting, self-burning, and self-hitting did not. Measures included the Non-Suicidal Self-Injury: Assessment Tool (NSSI-AT), Self-Injurious Thoughts and Behaviors Interview (SITBI), PHQ-9 for depression, and the Interpersonal Needs Questionnaire-10 (INQ-10) for perceived burdensomeness and thwarted belongingness.
clinical_insight: These findings have significant implications for clinical risk assessment in autistic adults. The differential network structure of NSSI in autistic versus non-autistic people suggests that standard risk assessment approaches developed in non-autistic populations may not adequately capture suicide risk in autistic clients. The identification of specific NSSI behaviours (self-biting, bone-breaking, fighting to get hurt) as longitudinal predictors of STB in autistic people, distinct from the cross-sectional prominence of cutting, indicates that clinicians should broaden their assessment beyond the most visible or common forms of self-harm. Qualitative distinctions made by autistic people between sensory/meltdown-driven self-injury and emotionally motivated self-harm are supported by these quantitative network findings. Clinicians should remain aware that all forms of NSSI warrant serious clinical attention, even those that appear less directly linked to suicidality in cross-sectional analyses, as they may contribute to risk through indirect longitudinal pathways including impacts on psychosocial functioning, emotional dysregulation, and psychopathology.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing and managing self-harm and suicide risk in autistic adults, researchers studying NSSI network structures, and mental health services developing autism-inclusive safety planning and risk assessment protocols.

title: The Impact of Stress Reappraisal and Mindset Interventions on Physiological, Endocrine, and Psychological Stress Outcomes: A Meta-Analysis
authors: Karishma K. Singh, Audrey-Ann Journault, Jeremy Jamieson
summary: This meta-analysis synthesised evidence from 35 randomised controlled studies (total N = 6,834) examining the efficacy of stress optimization interventions, which aim to help people appraise stress as enhancing and growth-supportive rather than debilitating. Random-effects models revealed that stress reappraisal and mindset interventions produced meaningful changes across multiple physiological outcomes: increased cardiac output (CO; d = 0.41), decreased total peripheral resistance (TPR; d = 0.47), and reduced salivary cortisol (d = 0.20) in acute stress contexts. For subjective outcomes, interventions reduced recent distress (d = 0.30) and improved affective responses (d = 0.15), but showed no significant effect on reported stress arousal (d = 0.07). No effects emerged for pre-ejection period (PEP; d = 0.11) or heart rate (HR; d = -0.03). Heterogeneity varied by outcome (I-squared from 0% to 67%). The findings demonstrate that brief psychological interventions targeting stress appraisals can shift both physiological and subjective stress responses, with the pattern of increased CO and decreased TPR suggesting a challenge-type cardiovascular response associated with approach-oriented coping.
clinical_insight: These findings provide robust evidence that stress reappraisal interventions can serve as a practical, low-cost adjunct to therapy for stress-related presentations. The dual impact on both physiological (cortisol, cardiovascular) and subjective (distress, affect) outcomes supports a genuinely biopsychological mechanism rather than a purely self-report effect. For clinicians working with anxiety, trauma, and stressor-related disorders, these interventions could be integrated into psychoeducation phases of treatment or used as standalone brief interventions. The challenge-pattern cardiovascular response (increased CO, decreased TPR) is particularly noteworthy, as it mirrors the physiological profile associated with active coping and resilience. The minimal effect on stress arousal despite improvements in affect and distress suggests that these interventions work by changing the relationship to stress rather than eliminating the stress experience itself. Clinicians should note the moderate heterogeneity, indicating that individual differences moderate treatment response.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating stress-related and anxiety disorders, therapists integrating CBT and third-wave approaches, health psychologists working with stress management, and researchers studying psychophysiological stress mechanisms.

title: Presence of Dissociative Experiences in Chronic Illness: A Systematic Review
authors: Jeremy Cooper, Courtney E Bowser
summary: This systematic review followed PRISMA guidelines to investigate whether and how dissociative symptoms manifest in chronic illness populations. Searching major psychological and medical databases, the authors identified eight articles meeting inclusion criteria. The analysis revealed several thematic domains: navigating chronic illness, coping, predisposition, experiential aspects, somatization, and ways of relating to self and others. The review found that dissociative symptoms are not a prominent or defining feature of chronic illness per se, but they appear in a pre-symptom fashion that can intensify into a distressing experience depending on the individual's mindset and coping strategies. The phenomenological analysis draws on embodiment theory to bridge clinical and lived-experience perspectives on dissociation, particularly in conditions where chronic pain or bodily changes alter one's sense of self and agency. The authors propose that dissociative experiences in chronic illness may represent a maladaptive extension of normally adaptive cognitive flexibility needed to cope with persistent bodily disruption.
clinical_insight: For clinicians working at the intersection of physical health and psychological wellbeing, this review highlights an under-recognised phenomenon: dissociative experiences in chronic illness that may be overlooked or misattributed. Patients presenting with chronic pain, autoimmune conditions, or other long-term physical health conditions may describe episodes of depersonalisation, derealisation, or dissociative absorption that are functionally related to their illness experience rather than reflecting a primary dissociative disorder. Understanding these experiences through the lens of embodied cognition and adaptive coping (rather than purely psychopathological frameworks) can inform more nuanced case formulation. The emphasis on mindset and coping strategies as modulators of dissociative severity suggests that therapeutic approaches targeting psychological flexibility, acceptance, and present-moment awareness (such as ACT or mindfulness-based interventions) may be particularly appropriate. Clinicians should routinely screen for dissociative experiences in chronic illness populations and consider their functional role in the patient's overall coping repertoire.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists working in health psychology or chronic pain settings, therapists treating somatic and functional disorders, and researchers studying the interface between dissociation, embodiment, and chronic illness.

title: Exploring Human and AI Emotional Support Through Reframing of Negative Situations
authors: Alina Herderich, Joanna Z. Li, Praveen Nair, Anat Perry, Amit Goldenberg
summary: This set of four studies (total N across studies = 3,227) compared the quality of cognitive reappraisals produced by GPT-4 versus trained human laypeople. In Study 1 (N=868), GPT-4 reappraisals of negative vignettes were consistently rated higher on effectiveness, empathy, and novelty by third-party raters compared to human reappraisals. Study 2 (N=386) replicated these findings in real-time interactions where recipients rated the reappraisals they received. Study 3 (N=1,477) demonstrated that incentivising humans with monetary bonuses increased time spent on reappraisals but did not close the quality gap with GPT-4, suggesting the difference is driven by skill rather than effort. Study 4 (N=496) found that labeling reappraisals as AI-generated reduced participants' evaluations of effectiveness, though GPT-4 was still rated higher than humans even when labeled. Language analysis revealed that GPT-4 used more lexically complex language and a distinct vocabulary profile; humans who used characteristic GPT-4 vocabulary produced higher-quality reappraisals, and including this vocabulary-origin interaction rendered the main effect of origin insignificant.
clinical_insight: These findings carry important implications for clinical practice as patients increasingly turn to AI for emotional support. Cognitive reappraisal is a core technique in CBT, DBT, and other evidence-based therapies, and this research shows that AI can produce reappraisals rated as more effective and empathic than those of laypeople. However, several cautions apply for clinical contexts. First, the comparison was with laypeople, not trained therapists, and the authors note that some humans outperformed GPT-4, suggesting skilled clinicians retain an advantage. Second, the label effect in Study 4 means that patients who know they are receiving AI-generated reappraisals may discount their value, a relevant consideration for any clinical tool that incorporates AI suggestions. Third, the linguistic analysis suggests that specific vocabulary choices drive perceived quality, which has implications for therapist training: teaching clinicians the specific language patterns associated with effective reappraisal could improve therapeutic communication. Finally, the authors emphasise that quality of a single response does not equate to the long-term relational support that characterises effective therapy, and privacy concerns remain significant when clients share emotional content with commercial AI systems.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners interested in understanding AI capabilities and limitations for emotional support, therapists whose clients use AI chatbots, clinical researchers studying emotion regulation strategy delivery, and developers of AI-assisted therapeutic tools.

title: Neurobiological and Familial Determinants of Narcissistic Personality Disorder: A Clinical Examination of Root Pathology and Evidence-Based Treatment within the Family System
authors: Juan Pereira
summary: This comprehensive examination traces NPD to its neurobiological and familial roots, arguing that the core pathology rests on wounds of unlovability and shame maintained through idealization-devaluation cycles that function as protective defenses. The paper proposes a family-systems-informed treatment framework that addresses these dynamics relationally.
clinical_insight: NPD remains one of the most stigmatized and poorly understood personality disorders. The emphasis on the unconscious wound of unlovability aligns with psychodynamic and schema therapy formulations. The family-systems lens helps therapists maintain empathic attunement. However, the paper is a clinical review rather than empirical, and notably lacks discussion of transference-countertransference dynamics.
relevant_for: Psychodynamic and schema therapy practitioners treating narcissistic personality pathology in adult private practice

title: Adverse Childhood Experiences and Adult Psychopathology: A Latent Class Analysis Approach
authors: George Mildred-Short, Sarah Tashjian
summary: This study uses latent class analysis to identify distinct patterns of adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) and their differential associations with co-occurring adult psychopathology, rather than relying on simple cumulative ACE scores. Results reveal that specific ACE profiles, such as emotional abuse combined with household dysfunction, predict different psychopathology patterns compared to profiles dominated by physical neglect or household substance use, demonstrating that ACE type matters beyond sheer quantity.
clinical_insight: The shift from cumulative ACE scores to latent class profiling has direct implications for case formulation in trauma-focused therapy. Rather than asking how many ACEs a client experienced, clinicians should map the specific pattern of adversity, as this study shows that different ACE profiles map onto different adult psychopathology clusters. For EMDR and schema therapy practitioners, this supports assessing not just trauma presence but trauma type when developing treatment plans. The tension in the literature between dimensional (cumulative) and categorical (typological) ACE models remains unresolved; this study supports the typological approach but cannot determine whether the identified classes reflect causal mechanisms or correlated risk factors. A blind spot is the reliance on retrospective self-report of ACEs, which is subject to recall bias and may underestimate the role of pre-verbal trauma that is nonetheless clinically significant in somatic and attachment-focused therapies.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists using EMDR, schema therapy, or CPT who conduct detailed trauma histories and ACE-informed case formulations

title: Is Employment Status Associated with Baseline Symptoms, Engagement, and Outcomes in Naturalistic Psychotherapy? Evaluation in a Large Community Mental Health Agency
authors: Zishan Jiwani, Qiang Xie, John J. Curtin, Kevin Riordan, Robbie Babins-Wagner, Derek Caperton, Mindi Thompson, Simon Goldberg
summary: In a large naturalistic study of 27,258 patients at a community mental health agency, this investigation examines how different employment statuses (employed, unemployed, disabled, student, retired) relate to baseline symptom severity, therapy engagement (session attendance, dropout), and treatment outcomes. The unusually large sample allows for granular employment categorization beyond the typical employed vs. unemployed dichotomy, revealing nuanced associations that smaller studies cannot detect.
clinical_insight: With 27,258 patients, this is one of the largest naturalistic psychotherapy studies to date, giving its findings exceptional ecological validity for community practice settings. For private practice clinicians, the key question is whether employment status functions as a moderator or mediator of treatment response. If unemployed patients show higher dropout, this has direct implications for engagement strategies at the outset of therapy. The tension between employment as a protective factor (structure, social contact, identity) versus a stressor (workplace toxicity, discrimination, accommodation needs) is particularly relevant for clients with personality disorders or chronic mental health conditions. A blind spot is that this study examines employment status categorically rather than assessing employment quality, which research increasingly shows is more clinically relevant than employment status alone for mental health outcomes.
relevant_for: Clinicians in community mental health and private practice who want to understand how socioeconomic factors like employment shape treatment engagement and outcomes

title: Intentional dating: A framework for sustaining erotic vitality in long-term relationships
authors: Anna Kelberg
summary: This paper introduces "intentional dating," a structured clinical framework grounded in Self-Determination Theory, the Dual Control Model, and Self-Expansion Theory to counteract the well-documented decline of sexual desire in long-term relationships. The framework emphasizes agency, competence, and attunement as core pillars for sustaining erotic vitality, offering therapists a systematic alternative to ad hoc suggestions about date nights or novelty exercises that often fail to address the deeper relational dynamics at play.
clinical_insight: Couples therapists can use this framework to move beyond generic advice and instead structure interventions around three evidence-based mechanisms: enhancing autonomous sexual agency, building relational competence through graduated challenges, and fostering attunement through mindful presence. The model bridges the gap between sexual science and clinical practice by providing a clear rationale for why standard relationship enrichment often falls short and what specific therapeutic moves can restore desire. A notable blind spot is the lack of empirical validation in clinical samples — the framework is currently theory-derived and awaits outcome trials.
relevant_for: Couples therapists and sex therapists working with clients experiencing desire discrepancy or erotic disengagement in long-term relationships

title: Integrating Digital Health Data into Clinical Practice: Perspectives and Practical Recommendations
authors: Marilyn Piccirillo, Miriam Hehlmann, Julian Burger, Mila Hall, Saskia Scholten
summary: This paper provides practical guidance for psychotherapists interested in using ambulatory assessment (AA) data from smartphones and wearables to track symptoms, experiences, and therapy processes between sessions. Unlike existing resources written for researchers, this guide is specifically tailored for clinical practitioners, offering recommendations on data collection, interpretation, and integration into the therapeutic workflow to enhance assessment precision and patient engagement.
clinical_insight: Therapists in private practice can leverage AA data to overcome the well-known limitations of retrospective self-report in session, gaining access to real-time fluctuations in mood, anxiety, and behavioral patterns that inform more precise case conceptualization and treatment monitoring. The paper bridges an important gap between digital mental health research and frontline clinical application, though it does not fully address barriers such as cost, patient digital literacy, or data privacy concerns that may limit adoption in smaller practices. A practical strength is the focus on therapist-friendly interpretation rather than technical implementation.
relevant_for: Clinicians in adult private practice interested in integrating digital assessment tools into their therapeutic workflow

title: Machine Learning Prediction of Treatment Response to Internet-Delivered Cognitive Behavioural Therapy vs. Antidepressant Medication
authors: Lee et al.
summary: This study developed and validated a multivariable predictive model for iCBT treatment response using real-world data from 776 patients. An elastic net regression with 27 predictors (including baseline depression severity, treatment expectation, and transdiagnostic symptoms) explained 14.6% of variance in depression change. A key finding was that models trained exclusively on iCBT patients showed treatment-specificity (R2=19.3% for iCBT vs. 10.8% for antidepressant-only), suggesting that treatment-specific models may better guide personalized treatment selection.
clinical_insight: This study supports the feasibility of using routine self-report data to guide treatment selection between iCBT and pharmacotherapy for depression. Clinicians should note that treatment expectation emerged as a key predictor, reinforcing the importance of expectancy management early in therapy. The finding that treatment-specific models outperformed mixed-treatment models has a notable blind spot: 24-34% of participants were concurrently receiving both treatments, potentially inflating cross-treatment generalizability. Nonetheless, this represents a concrete step toward personalized treatment matching in routine care.
relevant_for: Clinicians involved in treatment selection for depression, particularly those offering or referring to internet-based CBT programs

title: Examining the Impact of a Brief Compassion Focused Intervention on Everyday Experiences of Compassion in Autistic Adults Through Psychophysiology and Experience Sampling
authors: Sherwell et al.
summary: This study explored a brief one-week online self-compassion exercise in autistic adults using self-report, experience sampling, and HRV. Trait self-compassion improved significantly pre- to post-intervention, as did fears of self-compassion, but changes did not extend to acute psychological distress or emotion regulation difficulties. HRV increased significantly during self-compassionate practice. Experience sampling revealed that acting self-compassionately predicted greater activation of drive and soothing affective systems and reduced threat system activation in the moment.
clinical_insight: This study provides multimodal evidence that brief online compassion exercises are feasible and physiologically meaningful for autistic adults, even when acute symptom change is not yet detectable. The experience sampling finding that momentary self-compassionate action directly engages soothing and drive systems while dampening threat response offers clinicians a concrete rationale for encouraging between-session practice. The dissociation between trait-level improvement and acute distress reduction suggests that longer or more intensive CFT protocols may be needed before symptom change emerges. A blind spot is the absence of a comparison control group and the relatively short intervention duration.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with autistic adults experiencing anxiety, depression, or self-criticism, particularly those integrating compassion-focused therapy approaches.

title: The Integrative/Psychological Mediation Framework of Minority Stress in Asexual-Spectrum Individuals
authors: Nora McKellar, Gaia Steinberg, Liadh Timmins
summary: This cross-sectional study (N=261 asexual-spectrum individuals) provides the first test of **Hatzenbuehler's Integrative/Psychological Mediation Framework (IPMF)** in an asexual population, examining pathways from minority stressors to **psychological distress** (anxiety/depression). Using **PROCESS mediation analysis** with 5,000 percentile bootstraps, the study tested nine indirect pathways. Four were significant: outness predicted distress via **internalized acephobia** (ab=-.05, 95% CI [-.10, -.01]) and via **expressive suppression** (ab=-.05, 95% CI [-.09, -.01]); acephobic discrimination predicted distress via internalized acephobia (ab=.06, 95% CI [.02, .11]) and via expressive suppression (ab=.03, 95% CI [.005, .07]). All significant paths held in a combined **path analysis model** controlling for gender and age. Non-significant paths included rumination and cognitive reappraisal as mediators, though the authors note these may reflect insufficient power rather than true null effects. The study also introduces novel asexual-specific measures: the **Acephobic Harassment, Rejection and Discrimination Scale (AHRDS)** (alpha=.92), the **Internalized Acephobia Scale (IAS)**, and an outness-as-asexual measure.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with asexual clients should be aware that **internalized acephobia** and **expressive suppression** are key mechanisms linking minority stress to psychological distress. The **DSM-5 exemption for asexuality** within Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder (added in 2013) reflects progress, but clinicians may still inadvertently **pathologize asexuality** without affirmative practice. The IPMF offers a structured framework for developing asexual-specific interventions targeting these mediators. Notably, outness had complex effects: while it associated with *lower* distress via reduced internalized acephobia, it also associated with *higher* distress via increased discrimination exposure — mirroring the **concealment dilemma** well-documented in LGB populations. Therapists should explore both the **protective value of identity disclosure** and the **risks of increased prejudice exposure** with each client individually.
methodology_note:
This is a quantitative_experimental-type paper (cross-sectional mediation).

__Measurement Validity__: The study adapted established LGB minority stress measures for asexual populations, including the AHRDS (adapted from the HRDS, alpha=.92) and the IAS (adapted from the Internalized Homophobia Scale).
Novel asexual-specific measures showed acceptable reliability but require further validation.
Outness-as-asexual was reduced to two items (family, allosexual friends) due to high missingness (39.5% on the full scale), which limits the construct's representation.

__Statistical Rigor__: Mediation analyses used 5,000 bootstrap samples with 95% CIs, appropriate for testing indirect effects.
No multiple comparisons correction was reported for the nine tested indirect paths, inflating Type I error risk.
The cross-sectional design precludes causal inference, and the direction of mediation cannot be confirmed.

__Participant Transparency__: The sample was predominantly from the Global North (50% US, 15% UK, 8% Canada), limiting generalizability.
Asexual-spectrum labels were diverse (186 asexual, 27 gray-asexual, 30 demisexual), and 38% identified as transgender/non-binary.
Exclusion of non-identified asexual individuals (who may experience the same stigma without the label) may understate the impact of acephobia.

__Open Science__: The study is available as a preprint on OSF.
Measures are stated to be available at the lead author's website, though the URL was masked for review.
No data, analysis code, or preregistration is mentioned.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians working with LGBTQ+ and asexual-spectrum clients
- Therapists addressing minority stress and internalized stigma
- Researchers developing asexual-affirmative interventions
- Counselors exploring identity disclosure and concealment dynamics

title: Effects of One-to-One Trauma Center Trauma-Sensitive Yoga Sessions on Psychological Symptoms and Interoception over 6 Months
authors: Kelsey Madison Dietrich, Nicole Strumbel, Viann N Nguyen-Feng
summary: This observational study examined the effects of **one-to-one Trauma Center Trauma-Sensitive Yoga (TCTSY)** on psychological symptoms and interoception over six months in 10 clients across four countries (8 women, mean age 44.8, 90% with PTSD diagnoses). TCTSY is a protocolized, evidence-based adjunctive trauma treatment guided by five core principles: **choice-making, effective action, present-moment experience, non-coercion, and shared authentic experience**. Using **linear mixed models** with multiple imputation for missing data, the study found statistically significant improvements over time in **anxiety** (F(21,97.81)=9.76, p<.001), **depression** (F(21,101.94)=8.45, p<.001), **posttraumatic stress symptoms** (F(21,114.74)=4.99, p<.001), and three interoceptive awareness domains: **attention regulation** (F=6.03, p<.001), **self-regulation** (F=4.51, p<.001), and **body listening** (F=7.44, p<.001). Symptom reductions showed initial decreases at early time points with continued improvement by post-study. **Sleep** and **substance use** (measured by CAGE-AID) did not change significantly. Measures included the **DASS-21** (3-item short forms for session surveys, alpha=.71-.83), **PCL-5** (alpha=.95), **MAIA-2** subscales (alpha=.75-.95), **B-PSQI** (alpha=.63), and **PC-PTSD-5** (alpha=.71). This is the first study to examine TCTSY in a 1-1 format; previous RCTs only examined group-based delivery.
clinical_insight: This study extends the TCTSY evidence base to **individual therapy contexts**, finding that the bottom-up, body-based approach produces significant improvements in PTSD, anxiety, and depression comparable to group-delivered TCTSY (where RCTs show large effect sizes, d=1.07-1.18). For clinicians, 1-1 TCTSY offers a **flexible treatment option** for clients who may not tolerate group settings — particularly relevant given that trauma survivors with negative emotions related to physiological sensations are more likely to discontinue **top-down cognitive trauma therapies** like CPT. The significant improvements in **interoceptive awareness** (attention regulation, self-regulation, body listening) are clinically meaningful because interoceptive deficits are a core feature of PTSD and dissociation. Clinicians should note that TCTSY's emphasis on **choice and non-coercion** directly counters the loss of agency central to trauma, making it particularly suitable for complex trauma survivors. The lack of significant change in sleep and substance use may reflect the small sample or the specific mechanisms of TCTSY, which primarily targets interoceptive awareness rather than behavioral sleep or substance use patterns.
methodology_note:
This is a quantitative_experimental-type paper (observational longitudinal).

__Statistical Power__: The sample of N=10 is extremely small, limiting statistical power and generalizability.
The study acknowledges this limitation and used multiple imputation (5 datasets) and winsorization to handle missing data.
Effect sizes were not reported, making it difficult to compare with group-based TCTSY RCTs (d=1.07-1.18).

__Preregistration__: The protocol was preregistered with OSF, though the preregistration ID was masked in the manuscript.
Several deviations from protocol are transparently reported: ongoing enrollment, use of multiple imputation, and no between-group comparison.

__Statistical Rigor__: Linear mixed models with autoregressive covariance structures are appropriate for the repeated-measures design with unequal time points.
Five multiply imputed datasets were used with pooled estimates, appropriate for the 10% missing data rate.
No correction for the six primary outcome tests was reported.

__Measurement Validity__: Established measures were used (DASS-21, PCL-5, MAIA-2, B-PSQI, CAGE-AID, LEC-5).
Micro-longitudinal 3-item short forms were used for session surveys to reduce burden, which may reduce reliability.
B-PSQI had low internal consistency (alpha=.63) in this sample.

__Participant Transparency__: Sample was 80% women, predominantly White, from 4 countries (US, Australia, Greece, Israel).
90% had PTSD diagnoses; participants experienced an average of 5.60 trauma types.
The study was observational with no control group, limiting causal inference.

__Open Science__: Preregistered on OSF with transparent deviation reporting.
Deidentified data available upon request with community partner approval (not fully open due to community partnership agreements).
Published in the International Journal of Yoga Therapy (peer-reviewed).
relevant_for:
- Trauma therapists working with PTSD and complex trauma
- Clinicians integrating somatic and body-based approaches
- Yoga therapists working with trauma survivors
- Clinicians seeking alternatives for clients who drop out of cognitive trauma therapies

title: Rational Emotive Behavior Therapy for Career Decision Regret among Muslim University Students: A Systematic Literature Review
authors: Nengsih
summary: This systematic literature review, following **PRISMA 2020 guidelines**, synthesizes evidence from 52 studies (of 486 identified through Scopus and Google Scholar) to examine how **Rational Emotive Behavior Therapy (REBT)** can address **career decision regret** in Muslim university students. Using the **Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (MMAT)** for quality appraisal, the review identifies five thematic areas: (1) psychological determinants of career decision regret (counterfactual thinking, rumination, self-blame, reduced career adaptability); (2) effectiveness of REBT and **cognitive restructuring** in career counselling; (3) contributions of **Islamic psychology** and religious coping (tafakkur, muhasabah, hikmah); (4) the role of **hifz al-aql** (preservation and development of human intellect) as a philosophical foundation for rational thinking; and (5) contextual considerations in career development among Muslim students studying away from home communities. The review proposes a novel **Hifz al-Aql-Based REBT Framework** that integrates REBT's **ABCDE cognitive disputation** techniques with Islamic principles of reflective thinking (tafakkur), self-evaluation (muhasabah), and wise judgement (hikmah).
clinical_insight: This review offers a practical framework for clinicians working with Muslim university students experiencing **career decision regret** — a construct associated with **reduced career adaptability, emotional exhaustion, depressive symptoms, and poorer psychological well-being**. The proposed framework integrates Ellis's **ABCDE model** (Activating event, Beliefs, Consequences, Disputation, Effective new philosophy) with Islamic cognitive practices, making it a culturally responsive adaptation of CBT. For therapists in university counselling settings, the key insight is that career regret is sustained through **irrational beliefs and counterfactual reasoning** rather than simply poor information — requiring cognitive restructuring rather than additional career guidance. The integration of **tafakkur** (reflective thinking) as an Islamic parallel to cognitive reappraisal, and **muhasabah** (self-evaluation) as parallel to behavioral self-monitoring, provides culturally meaningful language for Muslim clients. Clinicians should note that Muslim students studying away from home communities may face additional cultural, familial, and religious pressures compounding career regret.
methodology_note:
This is a meta_analysis-type paper (systematic literature review).

__Statistical Rigor__: Follows PRISMA 2020 reporting guidelines with a clear flow diagram.
Searches were conducted in Scopus and Google Scholar, which may miss databases relevant to counselling psychology (e.g., PsycINFO, Web of Science).
The MMAT was used for quality appraisal, appropriate for reviews including diverse study designs.

__Measurement Validity__: No meta-analysis or effect size synthesis was conducted; findings are presented thematically.
The proposed Hifz al-Aql-Based REBT Framework is theoretical and has not been empirically validated.
The review acknowledges that evidence on Muslim university students' career regret remains limited.

__Participant Transparency__: The reviewed studies span diverse educational and cultural contexts.
The review highlights a gap: most career regret research has been conducted in Western, non-Muslim populations.
Specific demographic characteristics of participants across the 52 studies are not systematically reported.

__Open Science__: The review is available as a preprint on OSF.
No protocol was preregistered (noted as a limitation).
No data extraction sheet or analysis code is shared.
relevant_for:
- University counselors working with Muslim students
- REBT and CBT practitioners in multicultural settings
- Career counselors addressing decision regret
- Therapists integrating religious/spiritual frameworks with evidence-based practice

title: Patterns of Fatal Polysubstance Overdose: Exploratory Data Analysis of Decedents in Harris County, Texas, 2016-2025
authors: Dan Petrovitch, Kendall Farrell, Bruno Kluwe-Schiavon, Laura Stertz, Nikky Tran, Michael F. Weaver et al.
summary: This exploratory data analysis characterized **polysubstance use patterns** at time of death in 73 fatal overdose decedents from the **UTHealth Houston Brain Collection** (2016-2025). Using toxicology reports from the Harris County Institute of Forensic Sciences, drugs were attributed to parent compounds and collapsed into 25 classes. Decedents had a mean of **3.5 parent drugs** detected (SD=2.2) and **2.5 drugs implicated as cause of death** (M=2.2 drug classes). At least four drugs were detected in **43.8%** of cases. The **Gini-Simpson diversity index** was .96, indicating that 86.3% of the 51 observed drug-class combinations in cause-of-death determinations were unique to single cases. **Fentanyl** was present in the majority of cases, consistent with the "fourth wave" of the U.S. opioid crisis defined by fentanyl-stimulant co-involvement. The sample was 72.6% male, 71.2% White, with a mean age of 41.1 years (SD=14). The study argues that the extreme heterogeneity of polysubstance combinations poses fundamental challenges for epidemiological modeling and pharmacogenomic research, where simplifying assumptions about drug involvement may involve substantial tradeoffs between parsimony and granularity.
clinical_insight: For clinicians working in addiction medicine and harm reduction, this study underscores that **polysubstance use is the norm, not the exception** in fatal overdoses — with nearly half of decedents having four or more drugs detected. The extreme **heterogeneity of drug combinations** (GSI=.96) means that clinicians cannot rely on typical "drug profiles" when assessing overdose risk. Each patient's polysubstance pattern may be essentially unique, making individualized assessment critical. The findings have direct implications for **naloxone distribution** and **overdose prevention education**: the presence of multiple CNS depressants (fentanyl, benzodiazepines, alcohol) and stimulants complicates both the acute presentation and the reversal of overdose. Clinicians should also be aware that the **pharmacogenomic implications** are profound — genetic variants affecting metabolism of one drug (e.g., CYP3A4 substrates like fentanyl) may have cascading effects on the toxicity of co-involved substances, making personalized medicine approaches to overdose risk particularly challenging.
methodology_note:
This is a quantitative_experimental-type paper (exploratory data analysis of postmortem data).

__Statistical Rigor__: The study is explicitly exploratory, using descriptive statistics and the Gini-Simpson Index to quantify combinatorial diversity.
No inferential statistics or hypothesis tests are reported, appropriate for an EDA approach.
The mathematical argument about exponential growth of possible combinations (2^k - 1 for k drugs) provides a compelling theoretical framework.

__Measurement Validity__: Toxicology data from a medical examiner's office provides objective, high-quality substance detection.
Drugs were attributed to parent compounds using established metabolic pathways, though some attribution decisions involve clinical judgement.
The 25-class categorization system was developed for this study and may not align with other classification systems.

__Participant Transparency__: The sample is a convenience sample from the UTHealth Brain Collection (N=73), limiting generalizability.
The sample is 72.6% male, 71.2% White, 8.2% Hispanic, 1.4% Asian from Harris County, Texas.
9.6% of deaths were classified as fatal suicide, providing information about intent.
Deaths span 2016-2025, capturing the transition across opioid crisis "waves."

__Open Science__: Available as a preprint on OSF.
The UTHealth Houston Brain Collection is an established resource with documented protocols.
No individual-level data or analysis code is shared in the preprint.
relevant_for:
- Addiction medicine clinicians assessing polysubstance overdose risk
- Harm reduction workers and naloxone distribution programs
- Clinical toxicologists and emergency medicine physicians
- Researchers studying pharmacogenomic approaches to overdose prevention

title: "Behind a Dark Curtain": The Lived Experiences of Aphantasia
authors: Berengere Digard, Kiera Schul, Adam Zeman
summary: This qualitative **thematic analysis** of 52 aphantasic adults (mean VVIQ=16.5, age range 16-81, from a larger sample of 6,436 respondents) is the first systematic investigation of the **general lived experiences** of aphantasia — the inability to generate visual imagery when awake. Four main themes were identified: (1) Communicating and understanding the "mind's eye" in a world of visualisers, including frequent **disbelief and invalidation** from others; (2) The diverse experience of knowing without seeing, with some participants experiencing no imagery while others had faint or modality-specific imagery; (3) The **protective and disruptive ways** aphantasia shapes life and thoughts, with some reporting protection from trauma-related flashbacks while others described therapeutic techniques as ill-suited; (4) The search for meaning, including questioning whether aphantasia resulted from childhood emotional trauma. A critical finding was that many participants reported **standard therapies (CBT, exposure therapy, EMDR) were ineffective** because they rely heavily on imagery-based techniques. The study also found that some participants suspected their aphantasia was a **psychogenic response to childhood trauma**, though this remains unproven. Published in **Neuropsychologia**.
clinical_insight: This study has profound implications for clinical practice. Many standard evidence-based therapies for depression, anxiety, social anxiety, **PTSD, and OCD** rely on **imagery-based techniques** — yet for the estimated 3.9% of the population with aphantasia, these techniques may be inaccessible or ineffective. Clinicians should routinely assess clients' imagery ability, particularly when standard protocols (imaginal exposure, guided imagery, visualization-based CBT) are not working. For aphantasic clients, therapists may need to adapt techniques toward **sensory or verbal modalities** rather than visual imagery. The finding that some aphantasic individuals report a **protective effect against trauma-related flashbacks and intrusive images** suggests that aphantasia may mask or alter PTSD presentation — potentially leading to **underdiagnosis** since DSM-5 criteria include recurrent intrusive visual memories. Clinicians should also be aware that discovering one has aphantasia can cause **identity distress**, particularly when individuals realize therapies were not designed for their neurocognitive profile. Importantly, the study notes that aphantasia frequently co-occurs with **autism, ADHD, and dyslexia**, suggesting that neurodivergent clients may be disproportionately affected.
methodology_note:
This is a qualitative-type paper (thematic analysis).

__Measurement Validity__: Thematic analysis was conducted on open-ended survey items plus unprompted email testimonies, providing rich qualitative data.
The VVIQ confirmed aphantasia in participants (mean=16.5, floor range).
The dual data collection method (survey + emails) limits standardization but increases ecological validity.

__Participant Transparency__: Sample of 52 aphantasic adults from 6,436 survey respondents, with age range 16-81.
20 UK residents and 18 USA residents, with others from various countries.
14 women, with gender distribution not fully reported.
Participants were selected from a larger quantitative study (Digard et al., 2026) that identified subtypes.

__Open Science__: Published in Neuropsychologia (peer-reviewed, DOI: 10.1016/j.neuropsychologia.2026.109464).
Data available from corresponding author on reasonable request.
No preregistration (typical for qualitative research).
relevant_for:
- All clinicians using imagery-based therapeutic techniques
- Trauma therapists using EMDR, imaginal exposure, or prolonged exposure
- CBT practitioners whose protocols include visualization
- Clinicians working with neurodivergent populations (autism, ADHD, dyslexia)
- Therapists assessing treatment non-response

title: Understanding the Effects of Exposure to TikTok Content Related to ME/CFS and Long COVID on Associated Illness Perceptions and Stigma
authors: Andre Mason, Reenu Jose, Grace Johnstone, Stefana D. Huma, Amanda E. Clifford, Gareth J. Treharne
summary: This experimental study (N=796; 278 community members, 518 university students) used a **randomized block design** with **Linear Mixed Models** to examine how exposure to TikTok videos about **ME/CFS and Long COVID** influenced **illness perceptions**, **prejudice**, and **stigma**. Participants completed baseline measures then were randomly assigned to view up to four blocks of paired videos (one ME/CFS, one Long COVID per block), completing post-video measures after each. Exposure to TikTok content was generally associated with statistically significant **increases in perceived illness severity** for both conditions. Scores related to **illness prejudice and stigma typically decreased or remained comparable to baseline**, suggesting that personal narrative content on TikTok may reduce stigmatizing attitudes. The study addresses a critical gap: while previous research has examined TikTok health *content*, this is among the first to experimentally test how that content *shapes viewers' beliefs*. Both ME/CFS (affecting ~1% of the population) and Long COVID (~35%) are **medically invisible conditions** prone to dismissal, trivialization, and misdiagnosis, making public understanding particularly important for clinical outcomes.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with ME/CFS and Long COVID patients should be aware that **social media exposure significantly shapes illness perceptions** — including perceptions of severity. Increased severity perceptions after viewing TikTok content could be either beneficial (validating patients' experiences) or harmful (amplifying nocebo effects or health anxiety), depending on the content and the viewer. The finding that stigma and prejudice **decreased** after exposure is encouraging and suggests that patient-generated TikTok content may serve as a **destigmatizing force** — particularly valuable for conditions where medical dismissal is common. Clinicians should consider asking patients about their social media consumption related to their condition, as it may influence their **illness identity, help-seeking behavior, and treatment expectations**. The study also highlights the **algorithmic amplification** risk: TikTok's engagement-based algorithm may create "echo chambers" that repeatedly expose users to similar content, potentially reinforcing either adaptive or maladaptive illness beliefs. For both conditions, where symptoms are non-specific and diagnosis relies on self-report, **public understanding directly affects clinical encounters** — patients who have encountered validating content may be better advocates for their own care.
methodology_note:
This is a quantitative_experimental-type paper (randomized experimental design).

__Statistical Rigor__: Linear Mixed Models with pre/post contrasts within and between conditions are appropriate for the repeated-measures design.
Estimated marginal means were derived for effect estimation.
The study used four different video blocks, allowing some assessment of content variation effects.

__Measurement Validity__: The study used established illness perception constructs but does not specify standardized measures in the preprint.
Video content was curated by the researchers rather than algorithmically selected, which may not reflect typical TikTok viewing.
Participants could choose how many blocks to complete (up to 4), introducing self-selection bias.

__Participant Transparency__: 796 participants (278 community, 518 university students) from Aotearoa New Zealand.
Demographic breakdowns are reported in the study but not in the preprint abstract.
The mixed community/student sample improves generalizability but the New Zealand context may limit international applicability.

__Open Science__: Available as a preprint on OSF.
Raw data available from corresponding author upon request with a data use agreement (not fully open).
Funded by a University of Otago Research Grant.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians treating ME/CFS and Long COVID
- Health psychologists studying illness perceptions and medical dismissal
- Therapists working with chronic illness populations
- Clinicians interested in social media effects on patient beliefs and help-seeking

title: The 'Depressometer' Project: A Multimodal Longitudinal Dataset for Studying the Intrapersonal and Interpersonal Mechanisms of Change in Psychotherapy for Depression
authors: Dan Sayda, Omer Goren, Ido Yehezkel, Eva Gilboa-Schechtman, Dana Atzil-Slonim
summary:
The **Depressometer** project is a comprehensive method paper describing a **multimodal longitudinal dataset** for studying mechanisms of change in **psychotherapy for depression**. Data were collected from **58 patients** diagnosed with **Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)** and **13 therapists** during 16-session **supportive-expressive (SE) psychodynamic psychotherapy** at Bar-Ilan University. The dataset spans treatment-level, session-level, and within-session temporal resolutions, integrating self-report, clinician-rated, linguistic, vocal, facial, movement, physiological, and hormonal measures across **816 recorded sessions**.

The final cohort included 58 patients (M = 36.66 years, SD = 9.45; 64% female), diagnosed via the **MINI 5.0** and meeting entry criteria of **HRSD ≥ 14** or **BDI-II ≥ 17**. Treatment change indices showed that **68.6%** of patients scoring above the HRSD clinical cutoff at intake ended treatment below it, while **67.4%** showed reliable **BDI-II** improvement (RCI = 5.78) and **54.3%** showed reliable **ORS** improvement. The corpus includes approximately **six million words** of transcribed therapeutic dialogue (average 7,758 words per session), translated from Hebrew to English.

Multiple coding systems were applied to the "working phase" (mid-session) of five sessions per patient: the **MIND framework** (Multimodal Interpersonal and Intrapersonal Dynamics), coding ABCD self-state elements (Affect, Behavior, Cognition, Desire) at speech-turn and 5-minute segment levels; the **CIB** (Coding Interactive Behavioral Manual) for dyadic synchrony and tension (ICC = 0.76); **CEAS-III** for client emotional arousal (average ICC = .74); and **PIC** for therapist interventions. An **LLM-based framework** with four specialized agents (Supportive, Meaning-making, Exploratory, Directive) and a Judge-LLM was developed to compare AI-generated versus human therapist interventions. Physiological measures included **ECG** (for HRV), **EDA**, and **oxytocin** (salivary, pg/ml) collected across five sessions. Behavioral measures used **FaceReader-9** for facial expression valence and **Motion Energy Analysis (MEA)** for body movement.
clinical_insight:
The Depressometer dataset offers an unprecedented resource for understanding **how change unfolds within and across psychotherapy sessions** for depression. Clinicians may find this dataset particularly valuable because it operationalizes the **MIND framework**, which breaks down patient experiences into **core experiential building blocks (ABCD)** and maps how therapists' interventions interact with these shifting self-states moment-by-moment. The finding that **67.9%** of patients showed reliable BDI-II improvement and **52.7%** dropped below the HRSD clinical cutoff supports the efficacy of brief **SE psychodynamic therapy** in a naturalistic training clinic setting.

The multimodal design—integrating **verbal, vocal, facial, physiological, and hormonal data**—enables investigation of questions that single-modality studies cannot address, such as whether **dyadic synchrony** between patient and therapist predicts session-level outcomes, or how **oxytocin** responses during sessions relate to therapeutic alliance. The LLM component comparing AI-generated versus human interventions raises timely questions about the future role of **computational models in psychotherapy research** and training.
methodology_note:
This is an other-type paper (detailed methodology/dataset description paper). Statistical Power, Preregistration, and Effect Sizes are not applicable.

__Statistical Rigor__: The paper provides a thorough description of the multimodal dataset and coding procedures.
Treatment change indices were calculated using **reliable change indices (RCI)** with clinically meaningful cutoffs for the **HRSD** (cutoff = 14), **BDI-II** (cutoff = 17), **ORS** (cutoff = 24), and **HSCL-11** (cutoff = 1.92).
Inter-annotator agreement for the MIND framework was evaluated using **Gwet's AC1/AC2** coefficients alongside Krippendorff's α, with AC2 values ranging from moderate (.48) to near-ceiling (1.00) across dimensions.
The study acknowledges the **kappa paradox** and appropriately treats AC1/AC2 as primary coefficients given the low base rates of many psychotherapy markers.
CIB coding showed good inter-rater reliability (ICC = 0.76, range = 0.57–0.87).
CEAS-III emotional arousal coding achieved average ICC = .74 with 90% agreement on emotional valence.
LLM versus human intervention evaluation used three blinded expert judges.

__Measurement Validity__: Measures included well-validated instruments: **HRSD** (clinician-administered), **BDI-II** (α = .93), **OQ-45** (subscales α = .71–.91), **DERS** (α = .80–.93), **WAI-SR** (α = .85–.95), **ERS** (α = .86), **SES** (α = .84), **IIP-32**, **SWLS** (α = .87–.91), **PID-5-BF**, **RSE** (α = .93), **SDS**, **MULTI-30**, **POMS**, and **HSCL-11** (α = .92).
Physiological measures (ECG, EDA) used **Mindware software** with validated artifact-editing algorithms.
Facial expression analysis used **FaceReader-9** based on the **Facial Action Coding System (FACS)**, sampling at 25 fps.
Oxytocin was assayed in duplicates using auto-analyzer EVO75 with internal quality controls.
Hebrew transcripts were translated to English using Google Cloud Translation API, which introduces a potential source of measurement error.

__Open Science__: This is a method/dataset paper describing a resource for future research.
The paper does not report whether data, code, or materials are openly available beyond the method description.
No preregistration is mentioned.
The dataset appears to be an internal research resource at Bar-Ilan University's Psychotherapy Research Lab.

__Participant Transparency__: Sample of 58 patients with MDD (from 64 enrolled; 6 dropouts removed) and 13 therapists at a university clinic in Israel.
Patients were predominantly female (64%), Israeli-born (72%), and native Hebrew speakers (76%).
Mean age was 36.66 years (SD = 9.45, range 21–61).
Inclusion required primary MDD diagnosis via MINI 5.0 and HRSD ≥ 14 or BDI-II ≥ 17.
Exclusions: active suicidality, psychosis, addictions, bipolar disorder, brain damage, pregnancy, heart problems.
Therapists were advanced trainees (3–7 years experience; M age = 32.2 years).
Only 3 patients took psychiatric medication during treatment.
The sample is relatively homogeneous (Israeli, largely Hebrew-speaking, university-affiliated clinic), limiting generalizability.
relevant_for:
- Psychotherapy researchers studying mechanisms of change
- Clinicians interested in psychodynamic therapy process research
- Computational researchers working on AI applications in mental health

title: Modifying Empathic Disequilibrium Through a Brief Mindfulness-Based Meditation Training
authors: Ido Shalev, Emily Hayun, Alal Eran, Florina Uzefovsky
summary:
This study provides the first evidence that **empathic disequilibrium**—the imbalance between **cognitive empathy** and **emotional empathy**—is modifiable through intervention. A total of **157 undergraduate psychology students** (n = 89 experimental, n = 68 control; 80% female; M age = 23.7 years) at Ben-Gurion University completed ten days of daily **10-minute guided mindfulness meditation** or an active control condition involving **emotion regulation psychoeducation** and daily **POMS mood ratings**. Empathic disequilibrium was calculated as the difference between **standardized IRI** cognitive and emotional empathy subscale scores.

The **mindfulness training significantly reduced the magnitude of empathic disequilibrium** compared to controls (time × group interaction: F(1, 157.16) = 10.64, p < .01, **ηp² = .06**). Within the experimental group, empathic disequilibrium decreased significantly from T1 to T2 (p = .002, **d = .34**), while the control group showed no significant change (p = .10, d = -.19). The reduction reflected a **narrowing of the gap** between cognitive and emotional empathy rather than a directional shift toward one component. However, the hypothesized mediation through reduced **emotional reactivity** (measured by the **ERS**, α = .86) was not supported (BF₀₁ = 25.98, providing strong evidence for the null). **Propensity score weighting**, outlier exclusion, and controlling for **autistic traits** (AQ) all confirmed the robustness of the primary finding.
clinical_insight:
For clinicians working with clients who experience empathy-related difficulties, these findings suggest that **empathic disequilibrium**—whether emotional dominance (feeling overwhelmed by others' emotions) or cognitive dominance (feeling detached)—may be a **malleable interpersonal target**.
