- Assessment developers seeking behavioral measures of anxiety-related constructs
- Clinicians working with GAD, OCD, and anxiety disorders where IU is central

title: Reducing Concerns about Falling in Older Adults: Effectiveness of a multi-component intervention and the role of stress and inflammation markers - Long-term effects of the FEARFALL study
authors: Sabine Britting, Anja Muller, Robert Kob, Ellen Freiberger, Nicolas Rohleder
summary:
The FEARFALL study investigated the **long-term effects** of a **multi-component intervention** on **concerns about falling (CaF)**, **stress physiology**, and **inflammation** in 160 community-dwelling older adults (aged 70+, mean age ~79.5 years, ~74% female). Participants were randomized to either a 4-month intervention combining **exercise training** and **cognitive-behavioral strategies** (adapted from "A Matter of Balance") or a **sham control group** (health education lectures plus light stretching). Assessments occurred at baseline (T0), post-intervention (T1), and 8-month follow-up (T2). The per-protocol sample included 87 intervention and 73 control participants.

The primary hypothesis was not supported: **no between-group differences** emerged on any outcome domain. Both groups showed significant **time effects** across psychological, functional, and biomarker outcomes. CaF measured by the **FES-I** decreased over time (T0-T2 estimate = 3.85, p less than .001), as did FES-I Avoidance Behavior (estimate = 2.12, p = .001) and **UP-CoF** improved (estimate = -1.68, p less than .001). **Geriatric Anxiety Scale** scores increased over time (T0-T2 estimate = 1.88, p = .003), and **perceived stress** also increased (T0-T2 estimate = 1.46, p = .010). **Handgrip strength** declined significantly (T0-T2 estimate = -2.43 kg, p less than .001) and **SPPB** total scores decreased (estimate = -0.41, p = .034).

For biomarkers, **cortisol AUCg** showed a complex pattern with decrease from T0-T1 then increase from T1-T2, yielding a net increase at T2 (estimate = 0.66, p = .017). **Alpha-amylase AUCg** increased over time (estimate = -10.07, p = .013). **Inflammatory markers** showed a non-cohesive picture: **IL-6** and **IL-1b** increased (p = .002 and p = .004), **TNF-alpha** decreased (p less than .001), and **CRP** and **IL-10** remained stable. Multiple testing was controlled via **Benjamini-Hochberg FDR** correction.
clinical_insight: The **null between-group finding** is clinically important, suggesting that the sham control condition (health education lectures plus light stretching) produced comparable improvements to the active multi-component intervention. This could indicate either that non-specific factors (social contact, attention, structured activity) drive CaF improvement, or that the active intervention components did not sufficiently exceed the control in targeting psychological mechanisms of fall concerns. The **decline in functional measures** (handgrip strength, SPPB) across both groups despite CaF improvements suggests that psychological and functional trajectories may diverge in this age group. The **increase in anxiety and perceived stress** over 12 months, alongside improved fall self-efficacy, is a paradoxical finding. Practitioners should not assume that reducing fall concerns will automatically improve broader psychological outcomes.
methodology_note:
This is a quantitative_experimental paper.

__Statistical Power__: N = 160 randomized provides moderate power; per-protocol samples were 87 (IG) and 73 (SCG).
Power may have been insufficient to detect small between-group effects.
ITT analysis confirmed per-protocol findings, supporting robustness.

__Preregistration__: Registered at German Clinical Trials Register (DRKS00029171).
Study protocol published and preregistered on OSF.
Ethics approval obtained from Friedrich-Alexander-Universitat Erlangen-Nurnberg.

__Effect Sizes__: Effect sizes reported as estimated marginal mean differences with FDR-adjusted p-values.
Time effects were generally in the small-to-medium range for psychological outcomes.
No between-group effect sizes are reportable as no significant interactions emerged.

__Statistical Rigor__: Linear mixed-effects models with Type III F-tests and Satterthwaite-approximated degrees of freedom.
Random-intercept structure used throughout due to model instability with random slopes.
FDR correction applied at multiple levels (within-domain and across-domain via ACAT).

__Measurement Validity__: Well-validated measures used throughout: FES-I (alpha = 0.93, ICC = 0.94), FES-IAB, UP-CoF, GAS, GDS-15, PSS-10, EQ-5D VAS.
Biomarker assays used established protocols with CVs less than 10%.
SPPB and handgrip strength measured per EWGSOP2 recommendations.

__Open Science__: Preregistered on both DRKS and OSF.
Protocol published separately.
Supplementary results provided for all outcomes.

__Participant Transparency__: Detailed baseline characteristics reported for both groups showing no significant differences.
Inclusion criteria clearly specified (age 70+, FES-I score of 20 or above).
Exclusion criteria include anti-inflammatory medication use and cognitive impairment (MMSE less than 24).
High attendance rates reported (77% IG, 82.2% SCG meeting the 75% threshold).
relevant_for:
- Geriatricians and physical therapists working with fall prevention in older adults
- Clinical psychologists treating anxiety related to falls in aging populations
- Researchers studying psychoneuroimmunology and stress in older adults

title: From Emotion Recognition to Emotional Dynamics: A Framework for Personalized Affective Computing in Daily Life
authors: Aritz Irizar, Osho Rawal
summary:
This **perspective paper** proposes a comprehensive framework for extending **affective computing** from static **emotion recognition** to continuous modeling of **emotional dynamics** in everyday life. The authors argue that current affective computing research is limited by **tightly controlled laboratory paradigms**, short recordings, **static emotion classification**, and insufficient **personalization**. The proposed framework conceptualizes emotional life as a **complex adaptive system**, where continuous emotional trajectories in **arousal-valence space** can be modeled using **wearable physiological data** (e.g., PPG, EDA, skin temperature, acceleration).

The central innovation is the concept of **personalized effective potential landscapes** — individualized topological representations where stable regions correspond to emotional **attractor basins** that shape emotional transitions, recovery speed, and vulnerability to **maladaptive states**. The authors outline a **staged deployment roadmap**: (1) develop a population-level model using high-temporal-resolution affect labels from controlled experiments (including a proposed dual-pass paradigm where participants first experience stimuli naturally, then re-view them while continuously rating emotions); (2) deploy the model in naturalistic settings via smartphone-wearable systems for **continual learning**; and (3) adapt through **user-specific personalization** using EMA-informed prompts triggered by model-detected emotional changes.

**Conceptual simulations** illustrate how emotional landscapes might change gradually through cumulative life experience or rapidly after **major adverse events**. The paper discusses applications for **emotional self-awareness**, **psychotherapy support**, mental health research, and the study of **emotional temperament**, while highlighting ethical challenges related to **privacy**, **reification** (treating model outputs as emotional truth), **unequal performance** across populations, and the psychological effects of sustained emotion monitoring.
clinical_insight: This framework has substantial potential for clinical application in **mental health monitoring** and **psychotherapy augmentation**. The vision of passively tracking emotional dynamics via wearables could enable **early detection** of mood episodes in conditions like bipolar disorder, major depressive disorder, and anxiety disorders. The concept of **emotional landscapes** provides a quantitative language for discussing emotional **temperament** and **vulnerability** that could enhance case formulation. For therapists, having access to temporal patterns of emotional regulation (recovery speed, attractor stability, transition patterns) could inform treatment planning and progress monitoring. The emphasis that the system should **scaffold introspection** rather than replace it is clinically appropriate. However, the significant **ethical concerns** raised — particularly reification, where users might defer their own emotional understanding to algorithmic outputs — warrant careful consideration before clinical deployment.
methodology_note:
This is an other-type paper (theoretical/framework paper with conceptual simulations, no original empirical data).

__Measurement Validity__: The paper reviews and critiques existing approaches to physiological emotion recognition.
The proposed dual-pass paradigm for generating high-temporal-resolution training labels is a methodological contribution.
The effective potential landscape concept provides a novel representational framework but requires empirical validation.

__Open Science__: Published as a preprint on OSF.
No data to share as this is a perspective paper.

__Participant Transparency__: Not applicable to this theoretical work.
relevant_for:
- Digital mental health researchers developing wearable-based monitoring systems
- Clinical psychologists interested in technology-augmented psychotherapy
- Affective computing researchers and bioengineers working on personalized health models

title: Childhood Adversity, Household SES, and Decision-Making Under Uncertainty: A Latent Change Mediation Analysis
authors: Tochukwu Nweze, Marin Davidson, Becca Clarke, Adeola Olowokudejo
summary:
This study tests the hypothesis that **household SES** mediates the relationship between **childhood adversity** and **decision-making under uncertainty**, as measured by the **Cambridge Gambling Task (CGT)**. Past research has implicated both **childhood adversity** and **poverty** in impaired **reward processing** and **risk-taking behavior**, but the mediating role of socioeconomic circumstances in this relationship has been underexplored. The study employs **latent change mediation analysis** to decompose the pathway from early adverse experiences through socioeconomic conditions to adult decision-making patterns.

By using the CGT, which captures multiple dimensions of gambling behavior including **risk adjustment**, **quality of decision-making**, **deliberation time**, and **risk taking**, the study moves beyond simple risk-preference measures to assess the cognitive and affective components of decision-making under uncertainty. The **latent change mediation** approach allows for modeling change processes rather than relying on cross-sectional associations alone, providing a more dynamic picture of how childhood adversity may shape decision-making through its impact on socioeconomic trajectories.
clinical_insight: Understanding the pathway from **childhood adversity** to impaired **decision-making** through **household SES** has implications for assessing and treating individuals with adverse childhood experiences. If low SES mediates the relationship between early adversity and poor decision-making, interventions that address **economic stability** and **resource access** may be as important as trauma-focused therapies in improving cognitive functioning in this population. The CGT measures (risk adjustment, quality of decision-making) capture constructs relevant to **impulse control disorders**, **substance use**, and **behavioral addictions**, where decision-making under uncertainty is frequently impaired. The latent change mediation framework provides a model for understanding how **cumulative disadvantage** transmits from one generation to the next through cognitive pathways.
methodology_note:
This is an other-type paper (abstract-only review; full methodology cannot be assessed).

__Measurement Validity__: The Cambridge Gambling Task is a well-validated neuropsychological measure of decision-making under uncertainty.
Latent change mediation analysis is an appropriate approach for modeling mediated change processes.
Full details on childhood adversity measurement, SES operationalization, and model specification are not available from the abstract.

__Open Science__: Hosted on OSF, consistent with open science practices.
Full data, code, and preregistration status cannot be assessed from the abstract.

__Participant Transparency__: Sample characteristics are not available from the abstract.
relevant_for:
- Clinical neuropsychologists assessing decision-making impairments
- Researchers studying the intergenerational transmission of adversity
- Clinicians working with populations high in childhood adversity and low SES

title: Appreciation of social experiences and solitude in everyday life: the SOcial experiences daiLy Occurrence scale (SOLO)
authors: Daniela M. Pfabigan, Erik Rimestad Frogner, Fatemeh Asgari, Uta Sailer
summary:
This paper introduces the **SOcial experiences daiLy Occurrence (SOLO) scale**, a brief **end-of-day diary** measure designed to capture daily enjoyment of both **social interactions** and **solitude experiences** within a single instrument. Two validation studies were conducted: a single-day assessment (Sample 1, N = 203, recruited via Prolific, mean age 28.96) and a 14-day diary assessment (Sample 2, N = 75).

Item development began with 28 items covering social interaction quality, duration, superficial and meaningful encounters, and solitude enjoyment. Through **exploratory factor analysis** (promax rotation, eigenvalues greater than 0.3 criterion), items were reduced to 13 forming two scales: the **SOLO Social Interaction subscale** (10 items, alpha = .751, omega = .797) and the **SOLO Solitude subscale** (3 items, alpha = .611, omega = .634). The two subscales were empirically **independent** of each other, supporting the conceptualization of social enjoyment and solitude preference as **distinct dimensions**. The SOLO was initially developed for use in a clinical trial on changes in daily social interactions following **bariatric surgery**.

In Sample 1, the Social Interaction subscale correlated positively with the **Social Network Index** diversity score and the **Social Connectedness Scale-Revised**, and negatively with the **UCLA Loneliness Scale** and **Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale**, demonstrating good convergent and discriminant validity. The Solitude subscale was largely independent of these measures, confirming it captures a unique construct. In the 14-day Sample 2, the Social Interaction subscale showed meaningful associations with daily **well-being** and **positive affect**. A priori power analysis recommended N = 197 for detecting correlations of rho = 0.25 at 95% power, which was met by Sample 1.
clinical_insight: The SOLO scale fills a genuine measurement gap by capturing both **social engagement** and **solitude** in a single brief diary, which is valuable for clinical populations where both dimensions are relevant. In **eating disorder** treatment (the scale was originally developed for bariatric surgery patients), assessing how patients experience daily social interactions versus solitude could inform interventions targeting **social avoidance** or **social eating**. The finding that the two subscales are independent supports clinical work that treats social engagement and solitude comfort as separate therapeutic targets. The **end-of-day diary format** offers a practical compromise between intensive EMA and retrospective questionnaires, making it feasible for clinical trial settings. The inclusion of **brief encounters with weak ties** addresses an underappreciated aspect of daily social experience relevant for socially anxious or isolated patients.
methodology_note:
This is a psychometric paper.

__Measurement Validity__: The SOLO demonstrates adequate convergent validity through correlations with established measures (SNI, UCLA Loneliness, LSAS, SCS-R, SOBI).
Discriminant validity is supported by the independence of Social Interaction and Solitude subscales.
The Solitude subscale has only 3 items with moderate reliability (alpha = .611), which is a limitation.
The 14-day test-retest data in Sample 2 provides evidence for temporal stability of the factor structure.

__Open Science__: Analysis code and scale materials available on OSF (view-only link provided for peer review).
AI-assisted writing (Claude 4.6 by Anthropic) was used for improving clarity, transparently declared.

__Participant Transparency__: Sample 1 recruited via Prolific (N = 203, 104 women, 99 men, mean age 28.96, SD = 10.42).
Participants were asked to refrain if suffering from untreated depressive or psychotic states.
One participant excluded for completing the survey in less than 4 minutes.
Both samples are predominantly young adults, limiting generalizability.
relevant_for:
- Clinical researchers measuring social functioning in intervention trials
- Health psychologists studying social isolation and well-being
- Clinicians treating conditions characterized by social withdrawal or avoidance

title: Key Concepts in Online Learning and Decision Making for Personalizing Just-in-Time Adaptive Interventions (pJITAIs)
authors: Asim H. Gazi, Susobhan Ghosh, Yongyi Guo, Daiqi Gao, Ziping Xu, Inbal Nahum-Shani, Susan A. Murphy
summary:
This **conceptual paper** provides a comprehensive introduction to **personalizing Just-in-Time Adaptive Interventions (pJITAIs)**, which extend standard JITAIs by incorporating **online learning and decision-making algorithms** that update **decision rules** during deployment using streaming data. The paper unifies two dominant theoretical perspectives: **online reinforcement learning (RL)** and **adaptive control engineering**, which have progressed in parallel but share substantial conceptual overlap.

Standard JITAIs deliver adaptive support via four components: **decision points** (times at which intervention is initiated), **intervention options** (available support types), **decision rules** (if-then logic mapping tailoring variables to options), and **tailoring variables** (internal/external factors guiding decisions). The critical limitation of standard JITAIs is that **static decision rules** informed by pre-deployment data can become **outdated** due to societal shifts or differences between deployment cohorts. pJITAIs address this by enabling **within-deployment modification** of decision rules based on real-time data.

The paper provides detailed terminology mapping between RL/control engineering and JITAI components. In RL: time steps = decision points; actions = intervention options; state variables = tailoring variables plus predictors; policy = decision rules; reward = proximal/distal outcome. The paper distinguishes between **learning** (updating parameter estimates in the algorithm's model) and **decision-making** (using updated rules to select intervention options). A key concern addressed is **intervention fidelity**: the paper emphasizes that online learning modifies the pJITAI's decision rules (which are prespecified as modifiable) but does not change the fundamental pJITAI components (outcomes, tailoring variables, intervention options, decision points), preserving the intervention's theoretical grounding.
clinical_insight: pJITAIs represent the next generation of **digital mental health interventions**, with direct relevance to conditions requiring **real-time adaptive support** such as substance use (craving management), depression (activity scheduling), anxiety (exposure reminders), and chronic disease management (medication adherence). The ability to **update decision rules** during deployment addresses a fundamental limitation of current digital interventions: the assumption that relationships observed in development data remain stable across contexts and over time. For clinical implementation, the **intervention fidelity** discussion is crucial — clinicians need assurance that algorithmic adaptation is bounded and theoretically constrained rather than unconstrained optimization. The unification of **RL and control engineering** perspectives will help interdisciplinary teams communicate more effectively when designing personalized digital interventions. However, the **nascent evidence base** for pJITAIs means that controlled comparisons against standard JITAIs are still needed before widespread clinical adoption.
methodology_note:
This is an other-type paper (conceptual/review paper, no original empirical data).

__Measurement Validity__: Not applicable.
The paper provides conceptual clarity on mapping between RL/control engineering terminology and JITAI components.
A detailed comparison table maps equivalent concepts across the two perspectives.

__Open Science__: The paper explicitly states no data were created or analyzed.
Transparency statements confirm the conceptual nature of the work.

__Participant Transparency__: Not applicable to this conceptual work.
relevant_for:
- Digital health intervention designers and developers
- Clinical researchers designing JITAIs for mental health conditions
- Biostatisticians and data scientists working on adaptive intervention trial designs

title: Validation of the English Social Attunement Questionnaire (SAQ): associations with substance use, impulsivity and sensation seeking across the lifespan
authors: Emese Kroon, Karis Colyer-Patel, Christophe Romein, Janna Cousijn
summary:
This study validated the **English translation** of the 11-item **Social Attunement Questionnaire (SAQ)** and examined its associations with **substance use**, **impulsivity**, and **sensation seeking** across the adult lifespan (N = 449, 54.5% female, age range 16-61, M = 33.86, SD = 12.04). The SAQ measures the tendency to **harmonize with and adapt to the social environment** to find social reward in the absence of explicit group pressure, consistent with the **social plasticity hypothesis** (Cousijn et al., 2018).

**Confirmatory factor analysis** supported the original two-factor structure (Cognition subscale, 5 items; Behavior subscale, 6 items) with acceptable to good fit (RMSEA = .068, CFI = .959, TLI = .947). Internal consistency was good for the full scale (alpha = .82, omega = .83), Cognition (alpha = .75, omega = .75), and Behavior (alpha = .78, omega = .78). The SAQ demonstrated good **measurement invariance to sex** across configural, metric, scalar, and strict levels. No **sex differences** were found in SAQ scores. Younger age was associated with higher SAQ scores.

SAQ full-scale scores — guided by the **Behavior subscale** — were positively associated with **AUDIT** scores (driven by alcohol-related problems, AUDIT-P), **CUDIT-R** scores, and **IMPSS impulsivity**. The Cognition subscale showed no significant associations with substance use or impulsivity outcomes. **Age moderated** the association between SAQ and alcohol consumption (B = -.003, p = .036) and between SAQ and sensation seeking (B = -.003, p = .013): associations were positive in younger individuals but became more negative with increasing age. The alcohol moderation effect would not survive Bonferroni correction (p = .108). Age did not moderate SAQ-cannabis associations. Data were analyzed using **Spearman correlations** and **multiple linear regression** with moderation in JASP 0.19.3.
clinical_insight: The SAQ captures a construct — **social attunement** — that is distinct from traditional measures of **peer pressure** and **conformity**, and its age-dependent associations with substance use have direct clinical implications. The finding that high social attunement is associated with greater **alcohol use** and **sensation seeking** in younger individuals, but these associations reverse with age, supports the **social plasticity hypothesis**: social attunement may facilitate both the escalation and de-escalation of substance use across development. Clinically, this suggests that high social attunement in adolescence could be a **risk factor** for substance use escalation, but the same trait may become a **protective factor** in adulthood when social norms shift away from heavy use. The SAQ could help clinicians identify adolescents whose substance use is socially driven and who may benefit from **social skills interventions**. The distinction between the Cognition and Behavior subscales is clinically relevant: the Behavior subscale appears more consequential for substance use than the Cognition subscale.
methodology_note:
This is a psychometric paper.

__Measurement Validity__: The two-factor CFA model showed acceptable to good fit (RMSEA = .068, CFI = .959, TLI = .947).
The one-factor model showed substantially worse fit (RMSEA = .094, CFI = .920), supporting the two-subscale structure.
Good measurement invariance across sex (all levels including strict).
Internal consistency is adequate to good across all scales.
The SAQ Cognition subscale showed no associations with most outcomes, raising questions about its incremental validity in this sample.

__Open Science__: Data available on OSF (https://osf.io/mdpgb/overview).
Analyses conducted in JASP 0.19.3, which is open-source.
Translation procedure (forward translation, team review, native speaker check) is clearly described but informal.

__Participant Transparency__: N = 449 after exclusion of 108 participants (17 failed catch questions, 91 inconsistent responding).
Highly educated sample (92.8% high education).
96.2% reported lifetime alcohol use, 67.9% lifetime cannabis use, reflecting recruitment targeting substance users.
Multiple nationalities represented, though predominantly European.
Two participants with non-binary sex at birth were excluded from sex-based analyses.
relevant_for:
- Addiction researchers studying social mechanisms of substance use across development
- Clinical psychologists assessing social drivers of alcohol and cannabis use
- Adolescent substance use prevention program developers

title: The Sophie Sugar Addiction Scale: Conceptualisation, Development and Validation
authors: Sophie Hascher, Ori Ossmy, Halley M. Pontes
summary: This study developed and validated the **Sophie Sugar Addiction Scale (SSAS)**, a novel psychometric instrument that conceptualizes sugar addiction as a distinct behavioral addiction separate from general food addiction. Grounded in Griffiths' (2005) components model of addiction, the initial 18-item pool covered six dimensions: salience, mood modification, tolerance, withdrawal, conflict, and relapse. Data from 1,017 adults (M age = 28.7, SD = 8.8; 41% female) were collected via Prolific. **Confirmatory Factor Analysis** supported a refined six-item unidimensional structure with high internal consistency (alpha = .87, omega = .86). The SSAS demonstrated strong **convergent validity** with the Yale Food Addiction Scale 2.0 (YFAS 2.0) and meaningful associations with ADHD, OCD, depression, anxiety, and stress symptoms. A key finding: **28.9% of participants met criteria for sugar addiction without meeting food addiction thresholds** on the YFAS 2.0, supporting the construct's discriminant validity and uniqueness.
clinical_insight: The SSAS fills an important gap in addiction assessment by isolating **sugar-specific addictive behaviors** from broader food addiction. The finding that over a quarter of participants with sugar addiction did not meet food addiction criteria suggests that current food addiction measures may be **missing a clinically distinct subgroup**. For clinicians working in eating disorder and addiction settings, the SSAS offers a brief (six-item), psychometrically sound tool that can help differentiate sugar-focused addictive patterns from general overeating or food addiction. The associations with ADHD, OCD, and internalizing symptoms also suggest that sugar addiction may serve as a **transdiagnostic marker** for impulse control and emotion regulation difficulties, warranting integrated assessment approaches. The scale's brevity makes it suitable for routine screening in primary care, weight management clinics, and addiction treatment settings.
methodology_note:
This is a psychometric validation study using cross-sectional online data.

__Measurement Development__: Initial 18-item pool derived from Griffiths' (2005) components model of addiction, reduced to 6 items via CFA. Unidimensional structure supported.

__Convergent Validity__: Strong associations with YFAS 2.0. Also correlated with ADHD (ASRS), OCD (OCI-R), depression (DASS-21), anxiety (DASS-21), and stress (DASS-21).

__Discriminant Validity__: 28.9% of participants met SSAS sugar addiction criteria without meeting YFAS 2.0 food addiction criteria, supporting distinct constructs.

__Limitations__: Cross-sectional design precludes causal inference. Sample recruited via Prolific (online convenience). Self-report measures only. No clinical interview validation.

__Sample__: N = 1,017 adults, M age = 28.7 (SD = 8.8), 41% female. Recruited via Prolific.
relevant_for:
- Addiction clinicians assessing behavioral addictions beyond substance use
- Eating disorder specialists differentiating sugar addiction from food addiction
- Researchers studying the intersection of impulsivity, emotion regulation, and food-related behaviors

title: The Perceived Utility and Scope of Ecological Momentary Assessment and Interventions for Non-Suicidal Self-Injury: A Needs-Based Dynamic Risk and Intervention Model
authors: Mirthe Luijsmans, Laurence Claes, Ruth Tatnell, Marlies Houben, Thomas Vaessen, Tom Smeets, Inez Myin-Germeys, Glenn Kiekens
summary: This qualitative study explored the perceived utility and scope of **Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA)** and **Ecological Momentary Interventions (EMIs)** for **non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI)** from dual perspectives: 30 treatment-seeking individuals who engaged in NSSI (after completing a four-week EMA protocol) and 14 mental health professionals who used EMA in treatment. Semi-structured interviews were analyzed using **reflexive thematic analysis**. Three themes emerged: (1) EMA and EMIs extend therapeutic support by enhancing awareness, reflection, and skill use in daily life; (2) EMIs must be tailored to dynamically changing risk levels, leading to a novel **Needs-Based Dynamic Risk and Intervention Model** using a traffic-light analogy across low (mental health promotion), moderate (vulnerability regulation), and high (behavioral interruption) risk states; (3) EMIs are most valuable when embedded in **blended care** and implemented through shared decision-making, balancing system-initiated and user-initiated support.
clinical_insight: This study makes a significant contribution by proposing the first **dynamic, risk-stratified model for delivering just-in-time interventions for NSSI**. Current NSSI treatments rely heavily on retrospective, session-based approaches that cannot capture the rapid within-person fluctuations in urges and thoughts that characterize self-injury. The Needs-Based Dynamic Risk and Intervention Model offers a clinically actionable framework: at low risk, the system promotes general mental health; at moderate risk, it targets vulnerability regulation with coping skills; at high risk, it prioritizes behavioral interruption. For clinicians, this model provides a structured way to think about **digital augmentation of NSSI treatment** that adapts in real time. The emphasis on shared decision-making and blended care (combining digital tools with human therapists) is particularly important, as it avoids the pitfall of replacing therapeutic relationships with technology.
methodology_note:
Qualitative study using semi-structured interviews with dual stakeholder groups.

__Participants__: 30 treatment-seeking individuals with NSSI (post four-week EMA) + 14 mental health professionals using EMA clinically.

__Analysis__: Reflexive thematic analysis.

__Key Output__: Needs-Based Dynamic Risk and Intervention Model for NSSI (traffic-light framework: green = promotion, amber = regulation, red = interruption).

__Strengths__: Dual-perspective design (service users and clinicians). Participants had actual EMA experience before interview (informed perspectives). Novel model with clear clinical applicability.

__Limitations__: Qualitative only; model not yet empirically validated. Sample from treatment-seeking population only. EMA experience may have been brief (four weeks).
relevant_for:
- Clinicians developing or implementing digital interventions for NSSI
- Researchers designing adaptive EMI systems for self-injury
- Mental health services integrating EMA into routine NSSI treatment

title: Efficacy of Arts-based Social Prescribing for Mental Health in Adults with Psychiatric Diagnoses: Two Subsequent Randomized Controlled Trials
authors: Nikos Stefanis, Antonis Tsionis, Theodoros Sergentanis, Andreas Katsimpris, Eleni Giannouli, Lambros Yotis, Sophia Martinaki, Katerina Mavromichali, Marlen Mouliou, Aphrodite Pantagoutsou
summary: This study conducted **two sequential 12-week randomized controlled trials** evaluating **Arts on Prescription (AoP)** as an adjunct to care as usual (CAU) for adults receiving community psychiatric care in Greece. The pilot trial (N = 380) and confirmatory trial (N = 541) together enrolled 921 participants referred by mental health professionals. AoP involved structured participation in arts activities including music, dance, cinema, storytelling, theatre, environmental arts, and visual arts. The primary outcome was depression symptom severity (PHQ-9) at 12 weeks. Across both trials, AoP plus CAU produced significantly greater reductions in depression compared with CAU alone: **pilot: -1.91 PHQ-9 points (95% CI = -3.09 to -0.72, p = .001); confirmatory: -0.97 points (95% CI = -1.89 to -0.06, p = .001)**. Significant improvements were also observed for anxiety (GAD-7) and wellbeing (WEMWBS), but not loneliness. Attrition was modest (15.7-18.7%) with no serious adverse events.
clinical_insight: This is among the strongest evidence to date for **social prescribing** in a rigorously selected psychiatric population. Unlike prior studies that focused on general or subclinical populations, this trial specifically enrolled adults already receiving community psychiatric care, making the findings directly translatable to clinical practice. The effect sizes are modest but clinically meaningful, particularly given that AoP is a **low-cost, low-risk, person-centered intervention** that can be scaled alongside existing treatments. The lack of effect on loneliness despite improvements in depression and wellbeing suggests that the therapeutic mechanism may operate through creative engagement and meaning-making rather than social connection per se. For mental health services, AoP represents a viable adjunct that can be offered to patients who may be ambivalent about or saturated with traditional psychotherapy or pharmacotherapy, providing an additional pathway to symptom relief.
methodology_note:
Two sequential RCTs (pilot + confirmatory) with identical design.

__Design__: 12-week parallel-group RCT. AoP + CAU vs. CAU alone. Randomization after referral from mental health professionals.

__Sample__: Total N = 921 (pilot 380 + confirmatory 541). Adults receiving community psychiatric care in Greece.

__Outcomes__: Primary: PHQ-9 at 12 weeks. Secondary: GAD-7, WEMWBS, loneliness.

__Analysis__: Intention-to-treat linear mixed-effects models.

__Results__: Pilot: -1.91 PHQ-9 (p = .001). Confirmatory: -0.97 PHQ-9 (p = .001). Both significant for anxiety and wellbeing; not for loneliness.

__Strengths__: Two-trial design increases robustness. Intention-to-treat analysis. Psychiatric (not general) population. Low attrition.

__Limitations__: No active control (CAU only). Greece-specific cultural context may limit generalizability. 12-week follow-up only (no long-term data). No mediation analysis of mechanisms.
relevant_for:
- Community mental health teams seeking scalable adjunctive interventions
- Clinicians treating treatment-resistant or ambivalent patients who may benefit from non-traditional approaches
- Service designers implementing social prescribing programs in psychiatric settings

title: Improving Autobiographical Episodic Memory, Quality of Life, and Sense of Self with a Smartphone Intervention in Early Dementia: A Case Study
authors: Miranda Chang, Bryan Hong, Morgan Barense
summary: This **case study** examined whether a digital reminiscence intervention targeting **recent autobiographical memory** could improve episodic recall and psychosocial outcomes in early dementia. G.F., a 79-year-old man with early-stage dementia, completed an 11-week intervention using **HippoCamera**, a neuroscience-based smartphone application that helps users generate and review multimodal memory cues (video, audio, text) from everyday events. The key experimental manipulation was that some events were recorded AND reviewed, while others were recorded but NOT reviewed. Events reviewed with HippoCamera were recalled with significantly greater **episodic detail** than non-reviewed events. Post-intervention, G.F. reported improvements in **quality of life, life satisfaction, self-concept, and perceived memory abilities**, along with reduced depressive symptoms. Qualitative feedback indicated the intervention helped him regain confidence, re-engage socially, and feel more optimistic.
clinical_insight: This case study addresses a critical gap in dementia care: while traditional reminiscence therapy focuses on **remote memories** (which are relatively preserved in early dementia), the most clinically impactful deficit is the loss of **recent autobiographical memories** — the experiences that maintain a coherent, up-to-date sense of self. HippoCamera's approach of creating and reviewing multimodal cues for recent events directly targets this vulnerable domain. The finding that reviewed events were recalled with more episodic detail than non-reviewed events (within the same patient) provides a compelling within-subject demonstration of the tool's mechanism. For clinicians working in dementia care, this represents a **practical, accessible, and low-cost intervention** (a smartphone app) that patients can use independently, potentially bridging the gap between clinic-based therapy and daily life. The psychosocial benefits (confidence, social re-engagement, optimism) are particularly noteworthy as they address the broader disability associated with memory loss, not just the cognitive symptom.
methodology_note:
Single-case experimental design (SCED) with 11-week intervention.

__Participant__: G.F., 79-year-old male, early-stage dementia.

__Intervention__: HippoCamera smartphone app — 11 weeks of recording and reviewing multimodal memory cues from daily events. Within-subject design: some events reviewed, others recorded only.

__Measures__: Episodic memory detail scoring (reviewed vs. non-reviewed events). Pre/post psychosocial measures: Quality of Life (QoL-AD), life satisfaction, self-concept, depressive symptoms, perceived memory ability. Qualitative interviews.

__Key Finding__: Reviewed events recalled with greater episodic detail. Post-intervention improvements in QoL, life satisfaction, self-concept, reduced depressive symptoms.

__Limitations__: Single case (N = 1). No control condition. Open-label design (participant knew which events were reviewed). Generalizability unknown. 66-page PDF suggests detailed methodology but inherently limited by SCED design.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians working in dementia and cognitive rehabilitation seeking accessible digital tools
- Neuropsychologists interested in novel reminiscence approaches targeting recent memory
- Caregivers and family members looking for practical memory support strategies

title: Stress Biases Alcohol Choice Across Timescales in Daily Life, but Context Gates Its Expression
authors: Jonas Dora, Caspar J. van Lissa, Maxwell Shinn, Adam M. Kuczynski, Gavin Kelly, Aleksandra Kaurin, Kevin King
summary: This study applied a **value-based decision-making (VBDM) framework** to resolve the long-standing paradox that laboratory studies consistently show stress increases alcohol seeking, but **ecological momentary assessment (EMA) studies have repeatedly failed** to detect within-person associations between stress/negative affect and drinking. A sample of 250 at-risk adult drinkers (median AUDIT = 9) completed a 14-day EMA protocol with two daily scheduled prompts plus optional event-contingent prompts following stressors (5,978 assessments total). At each prompt, participants completed 160 trials of a cognitive choice task pairing personalized alcoholic and non-alcoholic drinks. **Drift diffusion modeling** decomposed choices into decision parameters. Stressful events increased alcohol choice by approximately **10 percentage points** and selectively biased evidence accumulation toward alcohol — replicating laboratory findings in daily life. However, **contextual moderators** (presence of strangers, alcohol expectancies, time of day, alcohol cue visibility) determined whether the stress-drinking association was expressed behaviorally, explaining why raw EMA correlations often fail.
clinical_insight: This study is a methodological and theoretical breakthrough for understanding **real-time mechanisms of stress-related drinking**. The key clinical insight is that stress does increase alcohol choice in daily life, but its behavioral expression is **gated by contextual factors** — meaning a clinician cannot simply ask "are you stressed?" to predict drinking. Instead, assessment should consider the interaction between stress and context: drinking is most likely when a stressed person is around strangers, holds positive alcohol expectancies, or is exposed to alcohol cues. This has direct implications for **relapse prevention planning**: identifying high-risk contexts (not just high-affect states) allows for more targeted intervention. The study also validates the use of **cognitive choice tasks within EMA protocols** as a more sensitive measure of drinking motivation than self-report alone. For clinicians, this suggests that patients' self-reported stress-drinking links may be inaccurate not because the link does not exist, but because context determines its expression.
methodology_note:
EMA study with embedded cognitive choice task and drift diffusion modeling.

__Sample__: N = 250 at-risk adult US drinkers (median AUDIT = 9), recruited via Prolific. Non-treatment-seeking.

__Protocol__: 14-day EMA. 2 daily scheduled prompts + optional event-contingent prompts after stressors. 5,978 completed assessments. 160-trial choice task per prompt (personalized alcoholic vs. non-alcoholic drink pairs). 99.5% task completion rate.

__Analysis__: Drift diffusion modeling of choice/RT data. Mixed-effects LASSO and random forest for identifying moderators.

__Key Finding__: Stress increased alcohol choice by ~10pp. Contextual moderators (stranger presence, expectancies, time-of-day, cue visibility) gated expression. Raw stress-drinking associations attenuated with covariates.

__Strengths__: Large EMA sample. Novel combination of EMA + cognitive task + computational modeling. Resolves a paradox in the literature.

__Limitations__: Online recruitment (Prolific). Non-treatment-seeking sample. 14-day protocol only. US-based. Self-reported stressors. No clinical outcomes.
relevant_for:
- Addiction researchers studying real-time mechanisms of stress-related drinking
- Clinicians developing contextual relapse prevention plans for alcohol use disorder
- Methodologists interested in combining EMA with cognitive tasks and computational modeling

title: Elevated Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors, Proximal Suicide Risk Factors, and Non-Suicidal Self-Injury Among Adults Reporting Cannabinoid Hyperemesis Syndrome Symptoms
authors: Brian M. Hicks, Blair J. Whittington, Amanda Price, Paula Goldman, Mark A. Ilgen
summary: This study used data from the **National Firearms, Alcohol, Cannabis, and Suicide (NFACS) survey**, a nationally representative sample of 7,034 US adults, to examine associations between **cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome (CHS)** symptoms and suicidal thoughts, behaviors, and proximal suicide risk factors. Four groups were compared: daily cannabis users with CHS symptoms (n = 191), daily cannabis users without CHS symptoms (n = 882), past-year non-daily cannabis users (n = 1,288), and non-users (n = 4,673). The CHS group showed the **highest lifetime and past-12-month prevalence** of suicidal thoughts and behaviors, with rates significantly exceeding even those of daily cannabis users without CHS. The CHS group also reported elevated levels of proximal suicide risk factors from the **interpersonal theory of suicide**: perceived burdensomeness, thwarted belongingness, defeat, and entrapment. These associations persisted above and beyond daily cannabis use alone, suggesting CHS represents a distinct risk marker.
clinical_insight: This study identifies **cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome as a marker of elevated suicide risk** that is independent of heavy cannabis use itself. This is clinically important because CHS is a physical condition (cyclic nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain) that may present in emergency departments or primary care, yet its **psychiatric implications are rarely assessed**. The elevated suicide risk may be driven by multiple pathways: distress from chronic gastrointestinal symptoms, psychiatric comorbidity, substance use comorbidity, and the specific interpersonal risk factors identified (burdensomeness, thwarted belongingness). For clinicians, particularly those in emergency medicine, gastroenterology, and addiction medicine, this study provides a strong rationale for **routine suicide risk screening** in patients presenting with CHS symptoms. It also suggests that CHS patients who are daily cannabis users should be considered a high-priority subgroup for suicide prevention efforts, rather than being managed solely for their gastrointestinal complaints.
methodology_note:
Cross-sectional analysis of nationally representative survey data.

__Data__: National Firearms, Alcohol, Cannabis, and Suicide (NFACS) survey. N = 7,034 US adults. Nationally representative.

__Groups__: Daily cannabis + CHS (n = 191), daily cannabis without CHS (n = 882), past-year non-daily use (n = 1,288), no past-year use (n = 4,673).

__Measures__: CHS symptoms (self-report). Suicidal thoughts and behaviors (lifetime and past 12 months). Proximal risk factors: perceived burdensomeness, thwarted belongingness, defeat, entrapment. NSSI.

__Analysis__: Group comparisons on prevalence and mean-levels of outcomes.

__Key Finding__: CHS group had highest suicidal thoughts/behaviors AND highest proximal risk factors, even vs. daily users without CHS.

__Limitations__: Cross-sectional (no causal inference). CHS assessed via self-report (not clinical diagnosis). Relatively small CHS subgroup (n = 191). Cannot determine directionality.
relevant_for:
- Emergency physicians and gastroenterologists who encounter CHS patients
- Addiction clinicians assessing suicide risk in heavy cannabis users
- Suicide prevention researchers interested in physical health markers of risk

title: The Body Appreciation Scale-2 for Autistic Adults (BAS-2A): Psychometric Properties in Autistic Gender Diverse and Trans People
authors: Phaedra Longhurst, Luka White, Viren Swami
summary: This study validated the **Body Appreciation Scale-2A (BAS-2A)**, a measure of positive body image, specifically within the **autistic transgender and gender diverse (TGD)** adult population — a group at elevated risk for **body dissatisfaction** and **eating disorder symptomatology**. A sample of **191 autistic TGD adults** (aged 18–85) from Western countries completed the BAS-2A alongside measures of physical wellbeing, eating pathology, self-compassion, self-esteem, and quality of life. **Confirmatory factor analysis** supported a **12-item unidimensional structure** with excellent composite reliability. The BAS-2A demonstrated strong **convergent validity** (positive associations with self-compassion, self-esteem, and quality of life), **concurrent validity** (negative association with eating disorder symptomatology), **discriminant validity** (distinct from gender dysphoria), and **incremental validity** (predicting outcomes above and beyond existing measures). Body appreciation was significantly associated with lower eating disorder symptoms, highlighting its potential protective role in this underserved population.
clinical_insight: This validation addresses a critical gap: autistic TGD individuals experience intersecting vulnerabilities to **body image disturbance** and **eating pathology**, yet most body image measures have not been validated for this population. The BAS-2A offers clinicians a brief, reliable tool to assess a **protective factor** — body appreciation — rather than focusing solely on pathology. This is particularly important because body appreciation may foster resilience against eating disorder development in a group where both **autism-related sensory sensitivities** and **gender dysphoria** can compound body-related distress. The finding that body appreciation retained its measurement structure and validity in this population suggests that clinicians can use the same 12-item instrument across diverse autistic adults, simplifying assessment workflows. For eating disorder clinicians working with autistic TGD patients, incorporating the BAS-2A into routine assessment could help identify patients with stronger protective factors who may benefit from **strengths-based therapeutic approaches**.
methodology_note:
This is a psychometric study using cross-sectional online data.

__Measurement Validity__: CFA supported 12-item unidimensional structure.
Excellent composite reliability reported.
Convergent, concurrent, discriminant, and incremental validity all demonstrated using both observed and latent scores.
Validated specifically in autistic TGD adults (N = 191), a population with limited measurement tools.

__Participant Transparency__: N = 191 autistic TGD adults, aged 18–85, from Australia, Canada, UK, and US.
Recruited online (convenience sampling).
Demographics reported by country but detailed intersectional characteristics not fully described.

__Open Science__: Data and materials availability not explicitly stated in the abstract.
relevant_for:
- Eating disorder clinicians working with autistic and gender diverse populations
- Assessment-focused clinicians seeking validated measures for neurodivergent adults
- Researchers studying protective factors for body image in intersectional populations

title: Exploring How to Improve Experimenter Training in Experimental Psychopathology: Lessons from and for the Trauma Film Paradigm
authors: Tommy McConnell, Elisabeth Leehr, Matthew Garner, Deborah Talmi, Emily A Holmes
summary: This pragmatic review addresses a critical threat to the **translational pipeline** from laboratory **experimental psychopathology (EPP)** to clinical treatment: the erosion of **experimenter training** under "publish or perish" pressures. A systematic search identified only **seven articles** offering methodological guidance for EPP paradigms, with just one providing an explicit training framework. Using the **Trauma Film Paradigm (TFP)** as a worked example, the authors developed an **8-step training framework** grounded in clinical therapy training principles (e.g., **CTS-R competency scales**) and **signal detection theory**. The eight steps progress from conceptual knowledge → participant experience → expert observation → protocol manual study → supervised role-play → **competency rating** (7-point scale, 0 = "Absence" to 6 = "Excellence") → pilot testing with pre-defined data quality criteria → ongoing **fidelity checks**. Two detailed **competency rating scales** are provided: one for delivering film-viewing instructions (where small wording changes can substantially alter the **intrusive memory** analogue) and one for instructing participants on the **intrusive memory diary** using **teach-back** methods. The paper advocates for **"Slow Science"**, standardised training reporting, and exploration of **AI-augmented training** tools.
clinical_insight: This paper carries direct implications for clinical translation. The TFP is used as an **analogue model of trauma** to develop interventions for **intrusive memories** — a core **PTSD** symptom. If experimenters deliver immersion instructions inconsistently, the analogue trauma exposure is weakened, producing unreliable intrusion data that undermines the evidence base for treatments like the **imagery-competing task** (ANEMONE). The proposed **competency rating scales** mirror the **CTS-R** used in CBT training, creating a parallel pathway where EPP could borrow **deliberate practice** methods from clinical supervision. For clinicians who collaborate on research or supervise trainees, the **signal detection framing** is valuable: experimenters must learn to **maximise Hits** (detecting when participants meet inclusion criteria) while **minimising False Alarms** (over-interpreting ambiguous responses). The paper also highlights that the quality of evidence behind treatments depends on methodological rigour that often remains invisible in published reports.
methodology_note:
This is an other-type paper (pragmatic review and conceptual framework), so Statistical Power, Preregistration, and Effect Sizes are not applicable.

__Measurement Validity__: The 8-step framework draws on the Cognitive Therapy Scale-Revised (CTS-R), an established competency measure in CBT training.
Novel 7-point competency rating scales are proposed for TFP-specific delivery (film-viewing instructions and diary instructions), but these have not yet been empirically validated.
No psychometric data (reliability, inter-rater agreement) provided for the new scales.

__Open Science__: The paper itself is a review; no primary data or code.
Advocates for open reporting of experimenter training procedures in publications.
Mentions potential for AI-augmented training tools (e.g., RAG for protocol teach-back).

__Participant Transparency__: Not applicable (review paper).
The TFP framework is intended for use with adult non-clinical participants in experimental settings, with implications for generalisation to clinical trauma-exposed populations.
relevant_for:
- Clinical researchers using experimental paradigms to develop trauma interventions
- CBT supervisors and trainers interested in experimenter competence assessment
- Research teams implementing the Trauma Film Paradigm or analogous EPP methods

title: Active and Passive Social Media Use, Momentary Thought Patterns, and Emotion in Young Adults
authors: Xiangyu Deng, Thuy-vy Nguyen
summary: This **14-day ecological momentary assessment (EMA)** study examined how **active** versus **passive social media use** relates to momentary thought patterns and affective states in **146 young adults** (aged 18–23), yielding **4,325 total reports** (1,171 indicating recent social media use). An **exploratory factor analysis** of 14 binary thought items identified three cognitive dimensions: (1) **Episodic, social, memory-based thought**; (2) **Intentional, task-focused thought**; and (3) **Intrusive or negative thought**. The third factor showed the strongest associations with distress: higher **high-arousal negative affect** (b = 8.93, p < .001), higher **fatigue** (b = 6.65), higher **sadness** (b = 11.32, p < .001), and lower positive affect. **Active use** (chatting, posting) was associated with higher engaged thought (f-squared = 0.236) and less boredom than passive use, but crucially did **not** differ in intrusive thoughts or sadness. **Moderation analyses** revealed that the active-use benefit for reducing intrusive thoughts only occurred among individuals **lower** in **rumination**, **social anxiety**, **envy**, and **social comparison** — those higher on these traits showed no benefit from active engagement.
clinical_insight: This study challenges the prevalent clinical recommendation that patients should simply "be more active and less passive" on social media. The finding that **active use does not reduce intrusive or negative thoughts** — and fails to benefit those with higher **rumination** or **social anxiety** — suggests that behavioural advice alone is insufficient. **Intrusive or negative thought** during social media use emerged as the strongest cognitive marker of momentary distress (with large effects across multiple affective outcomes), aligning with clinically familiar phenomena like **rumination** in depression and anxiety. The moderation findings indicate that patients high in social anxiety or rumination may be particularly vulnerable when engaging actively — posting or commenting may trigger **self-evaluative processing** rather than social connection. Interventions could incorporate **metacognitive awareness** training, helping patients notice whether their active use feels connecting versus evaluative, rather than prescribing blanket behavioural changes. The **thought pattern framework** (three factors) provides a clinically useful schema for assessing social media experiences beyond time-based metrics.
methodology_note:
This is a quantitative_experimental study using intensive longitudinal EMA design.

__Statistical Power__: N = 146 (127 with personality data), 4,325 EMA reports.
Minimum detectable effect size f-squared = 0.03 for main effects, f-squared = 0.0014 for interactions (80% power).
Adequately powered for all reported significant effects.

__Effect Sizes__: Main effect of intrusive thought on sadness: b = 11.32 (large).
Active vs. passive use on episodic thought: f-squared = 0.236 (large).
Moderation effects significant but small (f-squared < 0.01 for most interactions).
No clinical significance thresholds discussed.

__Statistical Rigor__: EFA with parallel analysis and oblique rotation.
Linear mixed-effects models with random intercepts for participant ID.
21 moderation tests conducted without multiplicity correction — inflation of Type I error risk for interaction analyses.
Causal direction cannot be established from EMA design.

__Measurement Validity__: Thought items adapted from the Multidimensional Experience Sampling (MDES) framework.
Affect measured via five dimensions (not standardised clinical scales).
Personality measures are established: LSAS-SR (social anxiety), RRS-Brooding (rumination), INCOM (social comparison), DES (envy), SRQ (social reward).

__Open Science__: Data and code availability not mentioned in the abstract.

__Participant Transparency__: N = 146 young adults aged 18–23.
Predominantly female.
Recruited from a university population (Durham).
Restricted to young adults, limiting generalisability.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians addressing social media-related distress in young adults
- CBT practitioners working with rumination and social anxiety
- Digital mental health researchers designing EMA-based assessments

title: Design Psychology: Treating Digital Design as a Modifiable Factor in Mental Health
authors: Nejra van Zalk, Joseph Firth, Lion Shahab, Jamie Brown, Yvonne Rogers, Jake Linardon, John Torous
summary: This perspective article proposes **Design Psychology**, an interdisciplinary framework that reconceptualises **digital design features** (notifications, infinite scroll, like buttons, algorithmic feeds) as **modifiable, upstream influences on mental health** — analogous to environmental interventions in public health. The authors argue that reliance on **coarse exposure measures** like screen time has produced inconsistent associations (social media use accounting for **<0.4% of variance** in adolescent wellbeing), and advocate shifting the question from *"how much technology?"* to *"how is it designed?"* The framework maps a three-level pathway: **Level 1 (Digital Design)** — features, affordances, and algorithmic architecture; **Level 2 (Psychological Processes)** — **attentional capture**, **reinforcement learning**, **habit formation**, **social comparison**, and emotional reactivity; **Level 3 (Mental Health Outcomes)** — anxiety, depression, stress, and compulsive use, with bidirectional feedback loops. Specific mechanistic links are detailed: visible **like counts** trigger social comparison and anxiety; **infinite scroll** exploits attentional capture to drive compulsive use; **variable algorithmic rewards** engage reinforcement learning to form habits. The concept of **mental-health-proofing** — proactively designing systems to minimise psychological harm — parallels public health approaches like removing tobacco advertising.
clinical_insight: For clinicians, Design Psychology provides a structured framework for discussing digital technology with patients beyond binary advice ("use less"). It explains *why* certain apps feel harder to put down (e.g., **variable ratio reinforcement** from algorithmic feeds) or why specific interactions trigger distress (e.g., **visible like counts** enabling **upward social comparison**). This is particularly relevant for working with adolescents, for whom developmental sensitivity to **peer evaluation** and **reward processing** may amplify design effects. The concept of **design friction** (strategically adding effort or delay) parallels therapeutic techniques like **impulse control training** and **stimulus control**. Clinicians could use this framework to psychoeducate patients about the **mechanisms** through which digital environments affect their mental state, shifting blame from individual "addiction" to design-level factors. The framework also has direct implications for **digital mental health intervention design**, where understanding which engagement features drive adherence versus distress could improve therapeutic apps.
methodology_note:
This is an other-type paper (perspective/conceptual framework), so Statistical Power, Preregistration, and Effect Sizes are not applicable.

__Measurement Validity__: No primary measures — the paper proposes taxonomies for future measurement development.
Integrates evidence from HCI, behavioural science, and digital mental health meta-analyses.
Calls for standardised taxonomies of design features, psychological mechanisms, and outcomes.

__Open Science__: Perspective article — no primary data.
Integrates findings from open-access meta-analyses (e.g., Orben and Przybylski, 2019 dataset).

__Participant Transparency__: Not applicable (framework paper).
Discusses developmental considerations including adolescence as a sensitivity window for social evaluation and reward processing.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians discussing digital technology use with patients
- Digital mental health intervention designers
- Public health researchers interested in technology-related mental health policy

title: Can Mindfulness in Virtual Reality Modify Food Cravings?
authors: Sophie Hascher, Anoushka Parylo, Mike Kendig, Poppy Watson, Ori Ossmy, Halley M. Pontes
summary: This study investigated the immediate effects of a single **10-minute mindfulness intervention** on **food cravings**, comparing a **virtual reality (VR)** delivery format against a **2D computer screen** control viewing the same nature scene. **56 adults** from the UK and Australia were randomly assigned to conditions. Cravings were assessed using a **food rating picture task** and the **Food Cravings Questionnaire-State (FCQ-S)**, alongside state mindfulness measures. Mixed ANOVAs indicated that the **mindfulness intervention significantly reduced food cravings** and **increased mindfulness** scores across both groups, with **no significant differences between VR and control conditions**. Sub-analyses showed significant reductions in cravings for both healthy and unhealthy food images. **Self-reported immersion** did not moderate craving changes. **Sugar addiction scores** (measured by the SSAS) were positively associated with sweet food cravings at baseline but did not predict changes following the intervention. The findings suggest that the **mindfulness practice and nature-based context**, rather than immersive VR technology itself, drove the effects, potentially via enhanced **interoceptive awareness** and **emotion regulation**.
clinical_insight: For eating disorder and addiction clinicians, this study provides evidence that **brief mindfulness interventions** can modulate craving states, but adds an important caveat: **VR delivery offers no additional benefit over a simple 2D nature scene**. This is practically significant because VR equipment is expensive and less accessible than a computer or phone screen. Clinicians can confidently recommend brief, accessible mindfulness exercises for patients experiencing **food cravings** without needing to invest in VR technology. The mechanism appears to be the mindfulness practice itself rather than immersion level, suggesting that even low-resource formats (audio-guided mindfulness, smartphone apps) may be effective. However, the finding that **sugar addiction severity did not moderate treatment response** suggests that brief mindfulness may be equally effective regardless of craving severity, though the lack of a no-treatment control limits this conclusion. For **food addiction** treatment, where accessibility barriers are well-documented, this supports scaling simple, technology-light mindfulness interventions.
methodology_note:
This is a quantitative_experimental study using a between-groups experimental design.

__Statistical Power__: N = 56 total (28 per condition).
No a priori power analysis reported.
The sample is small for detecting medium interaction effects, increasing risk of Type II error for the VR vs. control comparison.

__Effect Sizes__: Significant main effect of time (pre-post) on cravings (mindfulness reduced cravings in both conditions).
No significant group x time interaction (VR vs. control).
Effect sizes for the main effects are reported via F-statistics but Cohen's d or partial eta-squared not explicitly highlighted.

__Statistical Rigor__: Mixed ANOVAs used for primary analyses.
No correction for multiple comparisons across outcome measures.
No a priori power analysis — the study may be underpowered for the key VR vs. control comparison.
Non-significant finding for VR vs. control could reflect a true null or insufficient power.

__Measurement Validity__: Food Cravings Questionnaire-State (FCQ-S) — established measure.
Food rating picture task — less standardised.
Sugar addiction assessed via SSAS (authors include the SSAS developers).
State mindfulness measured but specific scale not detailed in abstract.

__Open Science__: Data and code availability not explicitly stated.

__Participant Transparency__: N = 56 adults from the UK and Australia.
Demographic details limited in abstract.
Community sample (not clinical eating disorder population).
relevant_for:
- Eating disorder clinicians seeking accessible craving management strategies
- Addiction therapists working with food addiction
- Researchers evaluating VR vs. non-VR delivery of mindfulness interventions

title: POWERED to Recover: Preliminary Evaluation of a Semi-structured Interview to Support Individualized Expected Body Weight
authors: Agatha Laboe, Mahathi Gavuji, Lilian Palmer, Ava Heyrman, Anna Kreynin, Katherine Schaumberg
summary: This study evaluated the preliminary **feasibility and acceptability** of the **POWERED interview** (Personalized Optimization of Weight Recovery in Eating Disorders), a novel **semi-structured clinical interview** designed to provide a standardised method for determining **expected body weight (EBW)** in eating disorder treatment. Currently, no reproducible individualised method exists for EBW determination. **17 youth and young adults** (ages 14–20) with restrictive-spectrum eating disorders completed the POWERED interview, which captures medical, psychiatric, and eating disorder behaviour history for use alongside automated **growth chart-based tools**. Participants provided quantitative and qualitative feedback. Results showed high participant confidence in providing accurate responses across interview domains, supporting feasibility, though participants agreed the interview was **somewhat tiring and difficult**. Most preferred being informed of their own EBWs (**76.5%**) and having treatment providers informed (**82.4%**), yet only **47.1%** wanted parents or guardians informed. **Higher EBWs elicited greater distress and fear** regardless of determination method, suggesting EBW discussions may function as **therapeutic exposures** in eating disorder treatment.
clinical_insight: The POWERED interview addresses a longstanding gap in eating disorder treatment: EBW determination is central to nutritional rehabilitation, yet it is typically done idiosyncratically without standardised data collection. By systematically capturing **medical history, psychiatric history, and ED behaviour patterns**, the interview supports more individualised and defensible EBW targets. The finding that **higher EBWs elicited greater distress** is clinically important — it confirms that EBW discussions are inherently exposure-like, which clinicians can leverage by framing these conversations as **therapeutic opportunities** rather than purely informational. The preference data (most want to know their EBW themselves and have providers know, but fewer want parents involved) provides direct guidance for **collaborative care planning**. The POWERED interview is particularly valuable for multidisciplinary teams where dietitians, therapists, and medical providers need a shared, standardised data source for weight-based treatment decisions. The tiring/difficult feedback highlights the need to pace the interview and consider splitting it across sessions.
methodology_note:
This is a mixed_methods study combining quantitative feasibility ratings with qualitative feedback.

__Measurement Validity__: The POWERED interview is a novel instrument — this is a preliminary feasibility study, not a full psychometric validation.
No reliability data (inter-rater, test-retest) reported.
Content validity supported by clinical expert development process.
Quantitative feasibility ratings used but specific measures not detailed in abstract.

__Participant Transparency__: N = 17 youth and young adults aged 14–20 with restrictive-spectrum EDs.
Small sample for preliminary evaluation — appropriate for feasibility phase.
Age range spans adolescent and young adult, which may capture different developmental considerations.
Exclusion criteria and clinical vs. community setting not detailed in abstract.

__Open Science__: Data and materials availability not explicitly stated.
relevant_for:
- Eating disorder clinicians and dietitians involved in nutritional rehabilitation
- Treatment teams seeking standardised EBW determination methods
- Researchers developing assessment tools for eating disorder treatment planning

title: Imagining the Future: Modality Effects of Mental Imagery, Verbal Reasoning, and Virtual Reality on Behavioural Engagement
authors: Eleanor Burton, Matthew Coxon, Jennifer Shevchenko
summary: This research investigated whether **simulating future activities** could improve prospective evaluations of **behavioural engagement** in dysphoria and depression, and whether increasing immersion via **virtual reality (VR)** would enhance these effects. Across two studies, participants completed **mental imagery**, **verbal reasoning**, or (Study 2) **VR tasks** while evaluating future activities. In Study 1, both **mental imagery and verbal reasoning** improved perceived likelihood of engagement, importance, vividness, anticipatory pleasure, and mood. Mental imagery showed some selective advantages for vividness and anticipatory pleasure, but verbal reasoning produced broadly comparable effects. Study 2 introduced VR to test whether greater immersion would strengthen outcomes — contrary to predictions, **VR did not produce additional benefits** over mental imagery or verbal reasoning. All three modalities improved prospective evaluations across most measures, while mental imagery and verbal reasoning were **more effective than VR** in increasing motivation. Participants in the verbal reasoning condition reported similar levels of imagery use to those in the imagery condition, suggesting **spontaneous imagery engagement** during verbal reasoning. The findings suggest that **structured, deliberate simulation of future events** — regardless of modality — may be sufficient to improve expectations about future activities.
clinical_insight: For clinicians working with **depression** and **dysphoria**, these findings support the use of low-cost, accessible **future-thinking interventions** to improve **anticipatory pleasure** and **behavioural activation** — core treatment targets. The key clinical insight is that the **specific technique matters less than the act of structured future simulation itself**. Verbal reasoning was as effective as mental imagery, and both outperformed VR, suggesting that complex or technology-heavy interventions may be unnecessary. This is practically important because verbal reasoning exercises (e.g., "describe step-by-step what you would do, see, and feel during this activity") require no special equipment and can be easily integrated into **CBT** or **behavioural activation** sessions. The finding that verbal reasoning spontaneously triggers imagery suggests clinicians need not worry about patients being "non-visual" — the cognitive process may converge across modalities. For patients with **anhedonia** who struggle to anticipate pleasure from future activities, a brief structured exercise during session could provide immediate benefit.
methodology_note:
This is a quantitative_experimental study using two experimental designs.

__Statistical Power__: Sample sizes for Studies 1 and 2 not fully detailed in abstract.
No a priori power analysis mentioned.
Small sample sizes typical of experimental psychology may limit generalisability.

__Effect Sizes__: Both mental imagery and verbal reasoning produced significant improvements across multiple outcomes.
VR did not add benefit over simpler techniques.
Specific effect sizes (Cohen's d, partial eta-squared) not highlighted in abstract.

__Statistical Rigor__: Mixed ANOVAs across studies.
Study 2 added a VR condition as a between-groups factor.
No mention of multiple comparison corrections across the multiple outcome measures.

__Measurement Validity__: Outcome measures included perceived likelihood of engagement, importance, vividness, anticipatory pleasure, and mood — appears to use study-specific rating scales.
The use of non-standardised measures limits comparability across studies.

__Open Science__: Data and materials availability not stated.

__Participant Transparency__: Community sample (not clinical depression sample).
Exact N and demographics not detailed in abstract.
Studies conducted with non-clinical participants, limiting direct clinical generalisability.
relevant_for:
- CBT practitioners using behavioural activation for depression
- Clinicians working with anhedonia and anticipatory pleasure deficits
- Therapists seeking low-cost, accessible in-session techniques for motivation

title: Technology-Natives and Technology-Adopters: Age Differences in Remote Cognitive Assessment in Alzheimer's Disease
authors: Samhita Katteri, Hannah Wilks, Matt Welhaf, Andrew J. Aschenbrenner, Colleen C Frank, Clara Vila-Castelar, Sarah H. Stout, Martin Sliwinski, Tammie Benzinger, Florencia Clarens
summary: This study examined **generational differences** in performance on **high-frequency smartphone-based cognitive testing** for detecting early cognitive changes in **Alzheimer's disease (AD)**. **489 older adults** from the Knight Alzheimer Disease Research Center and **130 younger adults** from DIAN completed an in-clinic cognitive battery and brief smartphone tests up to **four times daily for seven days**. Older adults showed **higher adherence** (79% vs. 57%) than younger participants. However, younger adults achieved **higher reliability with fewer sessions** and exhibited **much smaller practice effects**. **Smartphone-to-in-clinic test correlations were stronger in younger adults**. These findings demonstrate that generational differences in technology familiarity create systematic biases in remote cognitive assessment that could affect the detection of early AD-related cognitive decline.
clinical_insight: For clinicians and researchers using **digital cognitive assessment** in dementia care, these findings are consequential. The higher adherence among older adults might seem advantageous, but their **larger practice effects** and **weaker smartphone-clinic correlations** suggest that their scores may reflect learning rather than true cognitive ability. Conversely, younger "technology-native" adults produce more reliable, less practice-contaminated data. This has direct implications for **clinical trials** targeting preclinical AD, where remote cognitive endpoints are increasingly used: if practice effects differ by age cohort, **treatment effects could be obscured or inflated**. Clinicians interpreting remote cognitive scores should consider the patient's **technology familiarity** as a moderating factor. The findings also suggest that **normative data** for smartphone-based cognitive tests must be stratified by technology experience, not just age. For **multidisciplinary memory clinics**, this study supports using remote assessments as supplementary rather than standalone tools for older cohorts.
methodology_note:
This is a quantitative_experimental study using intensive longitudinal smartphone assessment.

__Statistical Power__: Older adults N = 489, younger adults N = 130.
Substantial sample for detecting generational differences.
Multiple comparisons across adherence, reliability, practice effects, and correlations — correction approach not detailed.

__Effect Sizes__: Specific effect sizes not highlighted in abstract.
Key findings are in the direction and pattern of differences rather than magnitude.

__Statistical Rigor__: Compared adherence rates, reliability curves, practice effect trajectories, and smartphone-clinic correlations between age groups.
Multiple dependent measures increase family-wise error risk.
Practice effects quantified but statistical model for group differences not described.

__Measurement Validity__: In-clinic cognitive battery used as criterion measure (established).
Smartphone tests are brief — validity for detecting early AD changes is the research question, not an assumption.
Reliability assessed via internal consistency/split-half approaches.

__Open Science__: Data collected through established research centers (Knight ADRC, DIAN).
Data and code availability not explicitly stated.

__Participant Transparency__: Older adults: N = 489 from Knight ADRC.
Younger/middle-aged adults: N = 130 from DIAN (at-risk for autosomal dominant AD).
Detailed demographic and clinical characteristics not provided in abstract.
relevant_for:
- Neuropsychologists using digital cognitive assessment in dementia care
- Clinical trial researchers using remote cognitive endpoints in AD prevention
- Memory clinic teams interpreting smartphone-based cognitive test results
